The following table shows the relevant classification places for different layers of lithographic printing plate precursors and lithographic printing plates:  *If the invention lies in the constituents of the non-imaging layers, Indexing Codes B41C2201/10 , B41C2201/12 and/or B41C2201/14 shall be given accordingly in addition to the Indexing Codes specifying the location or the type of the layers.
[B41C 1/1008] by removal or destruction of lithographic material on the lithographic support, e.g. by laser or spark ablation; by the use of materials rendered soluble or insoluble by heat exposure, e.g. by heat produced from a light to heat transforming system; by on-the-press exposure or on-the-press development, e.g. by the fountain of photolithographic materials -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for preparing lithographic printing formes by removal or destruction of lithographic material on the lithographic support, e.g. by laser or spark ablation; by the use of materials rendered soluble or insoluble by heat exposure, e.g. by heat produced from a light to theat transforming system; by on the press exposure or on the press development, e.g. by the fountain of photolithographic materials.  Lithographic printing forme precursors and lithographic printing formes characterised by the compositions of the image-forming layers. Said compositions of the image-forming layers are specifically adapted to the methods for preparing lithographic printing formes covered by this group.
[B41C 1/1016] characterised by structural details, e.g. protective layers, backcoat layers or several imaging layers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The lithographic printing plates or the plate precursors with different layers including particular details to the different layers not including the single imaging layer.
[B41C 1/1025] using materials comprising a polymeric matrix containing a polymeric particulate material, e.g. hydrophobic heat coalescing particles -- The B41C 1/1025 subclass covers methods of preparing a printing form that employ a polymeric matrix embedding polymeric particles, such as hydrophobic heat-coalescing particles, to improve the formation or transfer of ink images. These particulate additives modify the surface or drying characteristics of the form during the printing process.
[B41C 1/1033] by laser or spark ablation -- The code B41C 1/1033 covers forming printing formes by removing material with a laser beam or electrical spark, a process that ablates the surface to create the desired pattern. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, specifically printing processes for manufacturing or reproducing printing surfaces, and more narrowly under forme preparation.
[B41C 1/1041] by modification of the lithographic properties without removal or addition of material, e.g. by the mere generation of a lithographic pattern -- The subclass covers printing processes that alter the lithographic characteristics of a surface without adding or removing material, for example by creating a pattern through exposure, chemical change or other physical modification that changes how the surface will accept ink. It includes methods such as laser-induced or radiation-induced pattern generation that merely modifies the existing lithographic properties.
[B41C 1/105] by electrocoagulation, by electro-adhesion or by electro-releasing of material, e.g. a liquid from a gel -- B41C 1/105 covers methods of forming printing surfaces in which material is transferred or fixed by electrical means such as electrocoagulation, electro-adhesion or electro-releasing, for example extracting a liquid from a gel to create the printable layer.
[B41C 1/1058] by providing a magnetic pattern, a ferroelectric pattern or a semiconductive pattern, e.g. by electrophotography -- B41C 1/1058 covers methods for preparing a printing form by creating a magnetic, ferroelectric or semiconductive pattern on the surface, such as in electrophotographic processes. The technique is used to define image areas that will later attract or repel ink or toner during printing.
[B41C 1/1066] by spraying with powders, by using a nozzle, e.g. an ink jet system, by fusing a previously coated powder, e.g. with a laser -- The subclass B41C 1/1066 covers methods for preparing printing forms by depositing powder material onto a surface, typically using a nozzle such as an ink-jet system, and then solidifying the powder, for example by laser or other fusing techniques, to create the desired print pattern.
[B41C 1/1075] Mechanical aspects of on-press plate preparation  B41C1/1008  -  B41C1/1066  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatuses for lithographic printing plate preparation when using heat or infrared exposure.
[B41C 1/1083] Mechanical aspects of off-press plate preparation  B41C1/1008  -  B41C1/1066  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatuses for lithographic printing plate preparation when using heat or infrared exposure.
[B41C 1/1091] by physical transfer from a donor sheet having an uniform coating of lithographic material using thermal means as provided by a thermal head or a laser; by mechanical pressure, e.g. from a typewriter by electrical recording ribbon therefor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Only forme preparation for lithographic printing.
[B41C 1/12] Multicolour formes -- The B41C 1/12 subclass deals with the preparation of multicolour formes, meaning the creation of printing surfaces that contain several colour separations at once. It covers techniques and equipment for arranging and curing inks or pigments on a plate or cylinder so that multiple colours can be printed in a single impression. This enables simultaneous multicolour printing without separate colour plates.
[B41C 1/14] for stencil-printing or silk-screen printing -- B41C 1/14 covers methods for producing the stencil or silk-screen form used in printing. It includes steps such as coating a mesh with a resist, exposing or cutting the pattern, and preparing the screen so that ink can pass through the open areas onto the printed surface.
[B41C 1/141] by cutting or perforation with mechanical means; Electrical spark cutting -- B41C 1/141 concerns the preparation of printing forms by removing material through cutting or perforation, either with mechanical tools such as knives, drills or saws, or by using an electrical spark to cut the substrate. The methods create the desired image or pattern on the printing surface, supporting various relief and stencil printing techniques.
[B41C 1/142] using a galvanic or electroless metal deposition processing step -- This subclass covers methods for preparing printing forms that incorporate a galvanic or electroless metal-deposition step, such as plating a surface to create a printable pattern or image. It is situated under processes for the manufacture or reproduction of printing surfaces, specifically within the broader category of printing operations.
[B41C 1/144] by perforation using a thermal head -- B41C 1/144 covers a method of preparing printing forms in which a thermal head creates perforations in the material to form the image or pattern. The technique uses heat-activated elements to selectively puncture the substrate, enabling later ink transfer or image reproduction. It falls within the broader category of printing processes for manufacturing or reproducing printing surfaces.
[B41C 1/145] by perforation using an energetic radiation beam, e.g. a laser -- This CPC subclass concerns the preparation of printing forms in which the image or pattern is produced by perforating the substrate with an energetic radiation beam such as a laser. The method creates perforations that define the printable and non-printable areas for subsequent printing processes.
[B41C 1/147] by imagewise deposition of a liquid, e.g. from an ink jet; Chemical perforation by the hardening or solubilizing of the ink impervious coating or sheet -- This subclass covers techniques for preparing a printing forme by depositing liquid imagewise, such as by ink-jet ejection, or by chemically perforating an ink-impervious coating or sheet through hardening or solubilising agents that create the image areas.
[B41C 1/148] by a traditional thermographic exposure using the heat- or light- absorbing properties of the pattern on the original, e.g. by using a flash -- This subclass covers printing processes that form a printing surface by traditional thermographic exposure, using the heat- or light-absorbing properties of the pattern on the original, for example by a flash lamp, to develop the image without applying ink.
[B41C 1/16] Formes with areas rendered ink-resistant by covering with an amalgam -- The entry B41C 1/16 covers printing forms that have specific regions made ink-resistant by coating them with an amalgam, typically a mixture of metal and mercury or other metallic compounds. This technique is used to protect selected areas of the forme during ink transfer, allowing precise control of image reproduction.
[B41C 1/18] Curved printing formes or printing cylinders -- The B41C 1/18 subclass covers printing equipment in which the printing surface is formed on a curved shape, such as a cylindrical or other non-flat forme used to transfer ink onto a substrate. It includes designs and methods for manufacturing or adapting such curved printing formes or cylinders for offset, flexographic, gravure or similar printing processes. This subclass falls under processes for making or reproducing printing surfaces within the broader printing operations hierarchy.
[B41C 1/182] Sleeves; Endless belts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The sleeves and endless belts having a printing layer thereon.
[B41C 1/184] by transfer of the design to the cylinder, e.g. from a lithographic printing plate; by drawing the pattern on the cylinder; by direct cutting of the pattern on the cylinder -- The subclass B41C 1/184 covers ways of forming a printing cylinder by directly applying the pattern onto its surface, either by transferring the design from a lithographic plate, by hand-drawing the image on the cylinder, or by cutting the pattern directly into the cylinder material. It relates to processes used in manufacturing or reproducing printing surfaces.
[B41C 1/186] by casting -- This subclass deals with methods of preparing printing plates or forms where the image-bearing layer is formed by casting a liquid material that solidifies into the desired relief. It is part of processes for manufacturing printing surfaces under printing.
[B41C 1/188] characterised by means for liquid etching of cylinders already provided with resist pattern -- B41C 1/188 covers a forme-preparation technique in which a printing cylinder that already carries a resist pattern is treated with a liquid etchant to remove the unprotected metal and form the imaging relief. The process combines pattern transfer by resist with chemical etching to create the final printing surface.
[B41C 2201/00] Location, type or constituents of the non-imaging layers in lithographic printing formes -- This classification addresses the placement, material type and composition of the non-image layers in lithographic printing formes, defining where such layers are located and what constituents they contain. It covers specifications of their position, function and component substances in the lithographic process.
[B41C 2201/02] Cover layers -- B41C 2201/02 covers the specification of cover layers used on lithographic printing formes, defining their location, material type and constituent composition within the non-imaging layers of the plate. The classification is applied to patents describing protective or functional coatings that affect plate handling, durability or ink transfer in printing processes.
[B41C 2201/04] Intermediate layers -- The code B41C 2201/04 covers intermediate layers used in lithographic printing formes, defining the location, type or material composition of non-imaging layers that lie between the substrate and the imaging surface. It is part of the broader category of processes for manufacturing or reproducing printing surfaces.
[B41C 2201/06] Backcoats -- B41C 2201/06 covers backcoats, which are coating layers applied to the non-imaging side of lithographic printing formes. The backcoat controls ink uptake, provides planarity and protection, and may include specific materials or structures to influence printing behavior.
[B41C 2201/10] characterised by inorganic compounds, e.g. pigments -- This subclass covers lithographic printing plates where the non-image areas contain inorganic materials, typically pigments, defining their composition, placement or nature within the plate's non-imaging layer. It is part of the processes for manufacturing or reproducing printing surfaces.
[B41C 2201/12] characterised by non-macromolecular organic compounds -- This classification covers lithographic printing formes in which the non-imaging layers are defined by low-molecular-weight organic compounds rather than macromolecular polymers. It includes patents that disclose the composition, location or structure of such non-macromolecular organic additives in the non-image areas of the printing plate.
[B41C 2201/14] characterised by macromolecular organic compounds, e.g. binder, adhesives -- B41C 2201/14 covers lithographic printing formes whose non-imaging layers are defined by macromolecular organic compounds, such as binders or adhesives, specifying the location, type or constituents of those layers. It falls within the hierarchy of performing operations, printing, and processes for manufacturing or reproducing printing surfaces.
[B41C 2210/00] Preparation or type or constituents of the imaging layers, in relation to lithographic printing forme preparation -- It covers the formulation and selection of materials that constitute the imaging layer of a lithographic printing forme, including the chemical composition, physical properties and methods for applying that layer to create a printable image.
[B41C 2210/02] Positive working, i.e. the exposed (imaged) areas are removed -- This CPC subclass covers the preparation of imaging layers for lithographic printing formes in which the process is positive-working, meaning that after exposure the areas that have been imaged are removed to form the printing surface.
[B41C 2210/04] Negative working, i.e. the non-exposed (non-imaged) areas are removed -- The code B41C 2210/04 covers processes for preparing lithographic printing formes in which a negative-working method is used; the imaging layer is applied, exposed to form an image, and then the non-exposed (non-imaged) portions of the layer are removed to create the printable surface.
[B41C 2210/06] Developable by an alkaline solution -- The CPC subclass B41C 2210/06 concerns the preparation of the imaging layer or its constituents for lithographic printing forme where the image can be developed with an alkaline solution. It falls under processes for manufacturing or reproducing printing surfaces in lithographic printing operations.
[B41C 2210/08] Developable by water or the fountain solution -- This subclass deals with lithographic printing forms whose imaging layers can be developed using water or the fountain solution employed in the printing press. It includes preparations where the image-forming material is water-soluble or otherwise removable by the aqueous fountain solution, enabling direct development during printing.
[B41C 2210/10] Developable by an acidic solution -- B41C 2210/10 designates lithographic printing-form preparation methods in which the imaging layer is formulated to be developed with an acidic solution. It pertains to the selection and preparation of compounds or layer structures that become visible or removable after acid treatment, enabling pattern formation on the printing surface.
[B41C 2210/12] Developable by an organic solution -- This CPC subclass concerns the preparation of imaging layers or their constituent materials used in lithographic printing forme preparation, where the imaging layer can be developed with an organic solvent. It includes formulations, coatings and processes that render the layer removable or polymerizable by organic solutions.
[B41C 2210/14] Multiple imaging layers -- The code covers methods for preparing lithographic printing plates that incorporate two or more distinct imaging layers, each containing different photosensitive or functional materials to control image formation and plate performance. It includes the selection, layering and treatment of these constituents during plate fabrication.
[B41C 2210/16] Waterless working, i.e. ink repelling exposed (imaged) or non-exposed (non-imaged) areas, not requiring fountain solution or water, e.g. dry lithography or driography -- The B41C 2210/16 entry covers waterless working in lithographic printing, where ink-repelling exposed or non-exposed areas are created without fountain solution or water, commonly called dry lithography or driography. It relates to preparing the imaging layers of printing surfaces for printing-form manufacture.
[B41C 2210/20] characterised by inorganic additives, e.g. pigments, salts -- This CPC subclass B41C 2210/20 covers the preparation of lithographic printing formes where the imaging layers incorporate inorganic additives such as pigments or salts. It falls under processes for making or reproducing printing surfaces, specifically the preparation of type or constituents of the imaging layers.
[B41C 2210/22] characterised by organic non-macromolecular additives, e.g. dyes, UV-absorbers, plasticisers -- The code B41C 2210/22 covers processes for preparing lithographic printing formes in which the imaging layer contains organic non-macromolecular additives such as dyes, UV-absorbers or plasticisers to modify colour, absorption or flexibility of the printed surface.
[B41C 2210/24] characterised by a macromolecular compound or binder obtained by reactions involving carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds, e.g. acrylics, vinyl polymers -- The subclass B41C 2210/24 covers lithographic printing-form preparation in which the imaging layer contains a macromolecular binder or compound formed by reactions of carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds, such as acrylics or vinyl polymers. It falls under processes for making or reproducing printing surfaces within the broader category of printing operations.
[B41C 2210/26] characterised by a macromolecular compound or binder obtained by reactions not involving carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds -- This CPC subclass relates to the preparation of imaging layers for lithographic printing plates in which the macromolecular binder is produced by chemical reactions that do not involve carbon-carbon unsaturated bonds. It falls under processes for the manufacture or reproduction of printing surfaces within the broader printing operations hierarchy.
[B41C 2210/262] Phenolic condensation polymers, e.g. novolacs, resols -- B41C 2210/262 pertains to the use of phenolic condensation polymers, such as novolacs and resols, in the preparation or composition of imaging layers for lithographic printing forme preparation. These materials are crucial for enhancing the durability and print quality of the printing surfaces.
[B41C 2210/264] Polyesters -- B41C 2210/264 designates the use of polyesters as components in the preparation of imaging layers for lithographic printing formes. It covers polyester binders, coatings or other polyester-based materials employed in forming the image-bearing surface of printing plates.
[B41C 2210/266] Polyurethanes -- The subclass B41C 2210/266 relates to the use of polyurethane materials in the preparation of imaging layers for lithographic printing forme. It covers polyurethane-based formulations, coatings or binders applied to the surface that will receive the image, as part of processes for manufacturing or reproducing printing surfaces.
[B41C 3/00] Reproduction or duplicating of printing formes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Reproduction or duplicating of printing formes, including:  stereotyping, reproduction or duplicating of printing formes to produce rubber printing blocks or to produce printing blocks from plastics. And further, electrotyping and application of backing layers thereon.
[B41C 3/02] Stereotyping -- B41C 3/02 covers stereotyping, the process of producing printing formes by creating a solid copy, usually from a mold or a pattern, to replace the original type or plate. This method enables rapid, repeatable printing of the same layout and is used in offset, gravure and other mass-production printing technologies. It falls under processes for manufacturing or reproducing printing surfaces within the broader printing operations.
[B41C 3/04] to produce rubber printing blocks -- B41C 3/04 covers methods for making rubber printing blocks, the flexible relief plates used to transfer images onto paper in offset or letterpress printing. It details forming rubber or elastomeric presses from original images to create reusable printing formes and falls under processes for manufacturing printing surfaces.
[B41C 3/06] to produce printing blocks from plastics -- B41C 3/06 covers methods for producing printing blocks or formes from plastic materials. It belongs to the performing-operations section for printing, specifically the processes used to manufacture or reproduce printing surfaces by duplicating printing formes. The code is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the EPO and USPTO.
[B41C 3/08] Electrotyping -- Electrotyping is a process that creates a metal copy of a printing forme by depositing metal onto a mould through electrolytic deposition, allowing precise duplication of printing plates and type. The method is used to reproduce printing surfaces for high-volume production and to preserve original formes.
[B41D] APPARATUS FOR THE MECHANICAL REPRODUCTION OF PRINTING SURFACES FOR STEREOTYPE PRINTING -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Making of stereotype printing surfaces, means for casting stereotype plates including machines, moulds and accessories, processes for handling and treating stereotype plates, and processes for preparing stereotype matrices.  Means and processes for shaping into relief form elastic or deformable material like rubber or plastics. The raised parts having an almost flat top surface define the image to be printed, e.g. alphanumerical or decorative symbols, in relief or letterpress type printing apparatus.  **Glossary:** - Stereotype plates: stiff metallic plates cast from a multilayer tissue paper and/or plaster containing matrix. This latter matrix is produced by applying fluid plaster or said multilayer tissue paper by using contact pressure onto the surface of a type bed being filled with a prearranged set of printing types carrying alphanumerical or decorative symbols representing e.g. a full page to be printed, fixed together in an open frame. - Stereotype printing surfaces: letterpress or relief type printing devices used in flat type bed printing presses and in rotary letterpress machines.
[B41D 1/00] Preparing or treating stereotype matrices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Shaping the matrices by exerting pressure and further secondary treating steps like cleaning, moistening or drying the matrices, specific technology mostly used until year 2000.
[B41D 1/02] using brushes -- B41D 1/02 covers the use of brushes to clean, smooth or otherwise treat stereotype matrices during the mechanical reproduction of printing surfaces. The step may involve applying a brush to remove debris, level the surface or prepare the matrix for subsequent ink transfer. This sub-class falls under apparatus for stereotype printing within the broader printing operations category.
[B41D 1/04] using cylinders or rollers -- The B41D 1/04 subclass addresses the use of cylinders or rollers when preparing or treating stereotype matrices for the mechanical reproduction of printing surfaces; it falls under the apparatus for stereotype printing within the broader printing operations category.
[B41D 1/06] using presses, e.g. toggle or like lever-and-link type presses -- This subclass covers the use of presses such as toggle or lever-and-link types in the preparation or treatment of stereotype matrices for the mechanical reproduction of printing surfaces. It relates to apparatus for creating stereotype printing plates.
[B41D 1/08] using hydraulic presses -- B41D 1/08 relates to the use of hydraulic presses to shape, press or otherwise treat stereotype printing matrices, applying controlled hydraulic pressure to form or modify the matrix material for the mechanical reproduction of printing surfaces.
[B41D 1/10] Performing secondary operations on matrices -- B41D 1/10 covers secondary processes applied to stereotype matrices after their initial formation, such as cleaning, trimming, hardening or surface treatment to ready them for printing. It falls under the subclass dealing with apparatus for mechanically reproducing printing surfaces.
[B41D 1/12] Humidifying or moistening -- B41D 1/12 covers methods and devices that humidify or moisten stereotype matrices during their preparation or treatment, ensuring the printing surface receives the proper moisture level for effective mechanical reproduction. This subclass falls under apparatus for mechanically reproducing printing surfaces used in stereotype printing.
[B41D 1/14] Drying -- B41D 1/14 covers drying processes used in the preparation or treatment of stereotype matrices, focusing on methods and equipment that remove moisture from the matrix to stabilize its surface before it is used for printing.
[B41D 3/00] Producing printing surfaces -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines, casting and moulding devices specifically adapted for manufacturing stereotype printing plates, specific technology mostly used until the year 2000.
[B41D 3/02] Horizontal moulds for casting flat plates -- B41D 3/02 covers equipment that forms flat printing plates by casting in horizontal moulds, where the mould lies flat during the pouring and solidification of the plate material. It is used in stereotype printing to produce uniform, planar printing surfaces for subsequent mechanical reproduction.
[B41D 3/04] Moulds, e.g. horizontal, for casting curved, e.g. semi-cylindrical, plates -- B41D 3/04 covers moulds used to cast curved printing plates, such as horizontal moulds that produce semi-cylindrical or other curved surfaces for stereotype printing. These moulds form the substrate on which the image is transferred for high-speed mechanical reproduction.
[B41D 3/06] Vertical moulds -- B41D 3/06 pertains to vertical moulds employed in stereotype printing, where a solid printing plate is formed from a mould held in a vertical orientation. The classification covers apparatus that creates printing surfaces by mechanically reproducing the image on such vertically positioned moulds.
[B41D 3/08] Moulds, e.g. horizontal, for casting tubular, i.e. cylindrical, plates -- B41D 3/08 relates to moulds used to cast tubular or cylindrical plates that form printing surfaces in stereotype printing. It covers devices such as horizontal moulds designed to produce the continuous metal plates onto which inked images are transferred for high-speed mechanical reproduction.
[B41D 3/10] Vertical moulds -- B41D 3/10 pertains to vertical moulds employed in stereotype printing to form printing plates or cylinders; the moulds are positioned upright to receive a refractory or polymer material that replicates a type-fixed surface for high-volume reproduction, forming part of apparatus that mechanically produces printing surfaces.
[B41D 3/12] Multiple mould stereotype casting machines -- A B41D 3/12 machine is a type of apparatus that produces printing surfaces by stereotyping, using several moulds at once to cast multiple plates in a single operation. It automates the mechanical reproduction of printing plates, enabling faster, continuous production of identical copies for offset or letterpress printing.
[B41D 3/14] Details -- B41D 3/14 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers specific details related to producing printing surfaces for stereotype printing, focusing on apparatus used for mechanical reproduction. This includes components and mechanisms that facilitate the creation of these surfaces but does not cover the entire process or broader machinery.
[B41D 3/16] Matrix clamps -- Matrix clamps are devices that secure a printing matrix or stereotype plate within a press, maintaining precise alignment and uniform pressure during the production of printing surfaces. They enable consistent transfer of inked images onto paper in stereotype printing operations.
[B41D 3/18] Devices for closing or opening moulds -- Devices for closing or opening moulds are mechanisms that actuate the mould cavity in stereotype printing presses, enabling the insertion and removal of printing plates. The entry includes levers, hydraulic or pneumatic pistons, cams and other actuators that secure or release the mould during the printing cycle.
[B41D 3/20] Melting pots -- The B41D 3/20 entry covers melting pots used in devices that mechanically produce printing surfaces for stereotype printing. It pertains to equipment that melts metal alloys to cast matrices or plates, enabling the creation of printing forms for high-volume reproduction.
[B41D 3/22] with pumps for conveying the molten metal into the casting chambers or boxes -- B41D 3/22 covers printing-plate equipment that incorporates pumps to transport molten metal into the casting chambers or boxes used for stereotype plate production. The code applies to apparatus designed for mechanically reproducing printing surfaces by feeding liquid metal into the molds that form the final printing plates.
[B41D 3/24] with control valves -- B41D 3/24 classifies devices for mechanically reproducing printing surfaces used in stereotype printing that incorporate control valves to regulate the flow of fluids or gases during the process. It is a subcategory of apparatus for the mechanical reproduction of printing surfaces under the broader headings of printing and performing operations.
[B41D 3/26] with devices for stirring the molten metal -- B41D 3/26 covers machines used in stereotype printing that form printing surfaces by casting molten metal, specifically those equipped with mechanisms to stir or agitate the molten metal during the casting process to ensure uniformity and improve surface quality.
[B41D 3/28] Devices for cooling the moulds -- The CPC subclass B41D 3/28 covers devices that cool the moulds used in stereotype printing. These cooling mechanisms are incorporated into equipment that forms printing surfaces by mechanically reproducing type or image onto a mould, helping control temperature, improve surface quality and shorten cycle time.
[B41D 5/00] Working, treating, or handling stereotype plates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Deforming, e.g. bending, and transporting flat or curved stereotype plates, specific technology mostly used until the year 2000.
[B41D 5/02] by bending -- The B41D 5/02 entry covers techniques and devices that shape or manipulate stereotype plates by bending them, either to form the final printing surface or to aid subsequent processing. It includes mechanisms that apply controlled flexure to plates during or after their production.
[B41D 5/04] of flat plates -- B41D 5/04 covers operations, treatment and handling of flat stereotype plates used in mechanical reproduction of printing surfaces, including cleaning, heating, embossing, mounting and other preparatory or post-printing processes for flat plates. It relates to apparatus and methods that manipulate these plates in stereotype printing.
[B41D 5/06] of curved plates -- B41D 5/06 pertains to the specific processes and apparatus for working, treating, or handling curved stereotype plates in the mechanical reproduction of printing surfaces. This classification is part of a detailed system used by the European Patent Office and United States Patent and Trademark Office to categorize patent documents.
[B41D 7/00] Shaping elastic or deformable material, e.g. rubber, plastics material, to form printing surfaces -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Shaping of rubber or plastics exclusively for manufacturing of relief type or of letterpress type printing surfaces, e.g. flexographic printing plates, mechanical apparatus for said shaping of rubber or plastics.  **Glossary:** - Relief type printing surfaces: printing surfaces that are defined by raised parts having an almost flat top surface defining an alphanumerical letter or defining any other design or symbol.
[B41D 7/02] by impression -- B41D 7/02 covers the shaping of elastic or deformable materials such as rubber or plastic by impression to form printing surfaces used in stereotype printing. It falls under printing apparatus for mechanically reproducing printing plates, specifying the method of creating the surface through an imprinting process.
[B41D 7/04] Forming printing surfaces by covering printing forms with a thin elastic skin, e.g. rubber foil, and retaining the latter thereon -- This subclass pertains to the method of shaping elastic or deformable material to form printing surfaces for stereotype printing by laying a thin elastic skin, such as rubber foil, over a printing form and securing it in place, thereby creating a flexible replica surface used for subsequent printing.
[B41D 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preparing or treating sterotype matrices -> B41D1/00 - Casting sterotype plates; machines, moulds, or devices therefore -> B41D3/00 - Working, treating, or handling strereotype plates -> B41D5/00 - Shaping elastic or deformable material, e.g. rubber, plastic material, to form printing surfaces -> B41D7/00
[B41F] PRINTING MACHINES OR PRESSES -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines or presses always include a fixed or static printing form, usually a printing plate, carrying a graphical and/or alphanumerical design forming the printing image,  after applying ink on the surface of said printing form, said inked image is applied by contact pressure onto the surface of a substrate which will form the printed matter,  machines or presses as a whole as well as all parts, details, objects and controls specifically adapted for use in such machines, e.g. drying, dampening, heating, cooling, cleaning, air blowing and suction devices,  feeding and/or handling of substrates to be printed upon is covered from the in-feed device through the whole machine down to the delivery device,  auxiliary folding, cutting, collecting and depositing specifically adapted for printing machines or presses in covered by B41F13/54 - B41F13/70 ,  machines or apparatus for printing operations in combination with other operations, e.g. embossing, are covered by B41F19/00 ,  machines or apparatus for printing operations in combination with perforating operations are covered by B41G7/00 .  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Apparatus or devices for printing for office or other commercial purposes -> B41L  **Glossary:** - Printing: always encompasses use of ink or colour - Print image or printing image: any design having any colour and or alphanumerical elements - Offset print or offset printing: using an intermediate element, most commonly a blanket cylinder, for transfer in one step of the whole inked print image on the printing element, most commonly on a printing plate, onto the surface of the substrate to be printed upon - Screen dots: very small coloured printed points on the surface of the printed substrate forming the printed image - Rotary machines: using at least one printing plate in cylindrical form
[B41F 1/00] Platen presses, i.e. presses in which printing is effected by at least one essentially-flat pressure-applying member co-operating with a flat type-bed -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Printing technology having the same working principle as used since the very first printing machines made in Europe in the 15th century, e.g. from Guttenberg.  Types are almost rigid three-dimensional printing elements having on their top surface an alphanumerical or similar symbol, which latter will be inked for directly printing onto a substrate.
[B41F 1/02] Hand-operated platen presses -- It refers to manually powered printing presses where a flat platen contacts a flat type-bed to transfer ink, used for small-scale or specialty printing. Typically hand-cranked or lever-operated devices for letterpress or relief printing.
[B41F 1/04] for mono-impression printing, e.g. on sheets -- The B41F 1/04 subclass covers platen presses designed for mono-impression printing, where a single impression transfers the image onto each sheet. It includes machines that use a flat pressure-applying member against a flat type-bed to produce continuous prints on individual sheets. This subclass falls under the broader categories of performing operations, printing, and printing machines or presses.
[B41F 1/06] with platen maintained parallel to bed during movement -- B41F 1/06 refers to platen presses in which the platen is kept parallel to the type-bed throughout its travel, ensuring consistent pressure application during printing. This configuration is used to improve print uniformity and reduce distortion as the platen moves.
[B41F 1/08] on webs -- The subclass B41F 1/08 covers platen presses that print on continuous web material rather than on individual sheets, such as rolls of paper, plastic film or fabric. It includes machines where a flat pressure plate contacts the moving web to transfer ink or other markings.
[B41F 1/10] for multi-impression printing in one or more colours, e.g. on webs -- B41F 1/10 relates to platen printing machines that can produce several impressions in a single pass, using one or more colours, typically on a continuous web or sheet. The subclass addresses multi-impression, multi-colour processes integrated in the press.
[B41F 11/00] Rotary presses or machines having forme cylinders carrying a plurality of printing surfaces, or for performing letterpress, lithographic, or intaglio processes selectively or in combination -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Rotary presses or machines, which have at least two printing units of different type or at least two printing plates of different type in one printing unit.
[B41F 11/02] for securities -- B41F 11/02 covers rotary printing presses or machines with forme cylinders that carry multiple printing surfaces and are designed specifically for printing securities such as banknotes, checks, stamps or other protected documents.
[B41F 1/12] on sheets -- B41F 1/12 relates to platen presses that print directly onto individual sheets, where a flat pressure member engages a flat type-bed to transfer ink onto the sheet. The classification covers methods and apparatus for handling, feeding and processing sheet material during the printing operation.
[B41F 1/14] using swinging platens, or forme supports -- B41F 1/14 refers to platen presses that employ swinging platens or forme supports to hold the flat type-bed during printing. It covers designs where the pressure-applying member moves on a pivot or hinge, allowing rapid opening, closing or adjustment of the printing surface in flat-bed printing machines.
[B41F 1/16] for offset printing -- This sub-class covers platen presses that are specifically configured for offset printing, where an inked image from a flat plate is first transferred to a rubber blanket and then onto the substrate using a flat pressure-applying member.
[B41F 1/18] for lithography -- B41F 1/18 designates platen presses that are used for lithographic printing, in which a flat lithographic plate or stone contacts a flat type-bed under pressure to transfer the image. The code specifies the pressure-applying platen arrangement and lithographic process within the wider class of printing machines.
[B41F 1/20] for perfecting sheets, i.e. for printing on both sides of sheets -- B41F 1/20 designates platen printing presses that incorporate a perfecting mechanism for printing on both sides of a sheet in a single pass.
[B41F 1/22] specially adapted for printing heads, e.g. letter heads -- The subclass B41F 1/22 pertains to platen presses that have been specially adapted to handle printed heads such as letter-heads, incorporating features that allow the flat pressure-applying member to accommodate pre-printed or attached heading sheets during the printing cycle.
[B41F 1/24] specially adapted for proof printing -- B41F 1/24 covers platen printing presses that are specially adapted for proof printing, where a flat pressure-applying platen works with a flat type-bed to produce trial copies before full runs. Such machines include modifications for fine-detail impression, quick changeover, and precise control of ink transfer to ensure accurate test prints.
[B41F 1/26] Details -- B41F 1/26 pertains to specific components or aspects of platen presses, which are printing machines that use at least one flat pressure-applying member in conjunction with a flat type-bed. This classification covers the detailed design and operational features of these press elements.
[B41F 1/265] Conveying webs  in general  B65H -- B41F 1/265 pertains to the conveying of webs in platen presses, where at least one essentially-flat pressure-applying member works with a flat type-bed. This classification is specific to the mechanics of web handling in printing processes.
[B41F 1/28] Sheet-conveying, -aligning or -clamping devices -- B41F 1/28 covers devices that convey, align or clamp sheet material in a platen press, ensuring accurate positioning and secure handling during printing. The mechanisms may include rollers, guides, vacuum systems or mechanical clamps that work with the flat pressure-applying member and type bed.
[B41F 1/30] using rotary grippers -- The CPC subclass B41F 1/30 covers platen printing presses that employ rotary grippers to seize and move the printed sheet, rotating the grip around an axis as the material is fed through the press. This subcategory falls under printing machines or presses, specifically platen presses that use flat pressure-applying members.
[B41F 13/00] Common details of rotary presses or machines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Driving devices adapted and/or used in rotary presses or machines, and their electric or hydraulic or mechanical features in form of constitutive elements, e.g. motors, gearings, couplings, clutches, rotary energy transmitters;  auxiliary devices or processes associated with the drives, e.g. vibration-dampers, noise reduction, lubrication;  driving devices associated with particular installations or situations;  brakes;  conveying or guiding webs through presses or machines including:  Cylinders including forme cylinders, impression cylinders, transfer and offset cylinders and including their accessories;  cylinder registering devices;  cylinder tripping devices;  cylinder-impression adjustments;  cylinder lifting or adjusting devices;  cylinder bearings;  supports for bearings or supports for cylinders;  cylinder cooling and/or heating;  machines frames, guards and/or covers;  registering and control of registering with means for:  Auxiliary devices for:
[B41F 13/0008] Driving devices  B41F13/004  and  B41F13/008  take precedence -- The B41F 13/0008 subclass defines the driving devices that supply motion to rotary printing presses, including motor, gear, hydraulic or pneumatic actuators used to rotate cylinders or webs. It is part of the printing-machines hierarchy and is superseded by the more specific groups B41F13/004 and B41F13/008.
[B41F 13/0016] Storage devices for printing cylinders -- The code B41F 13/0016 pertains to devices that store printing cylinders when they are not mounted on a rotary press. It includes racks, holders or other mechanisms that protect, organize and secure the cylinders during idle periods.
[B41F 13/0024] Frames -- B41F 13/0024 covers the frames that support the various components of rotary printing presses, including the structural chassis, bearing housings and supporting skeleton that maintain alignment of the printing cylinders and beds. The code is used for patents describing the design, material and arrangement of such frames in rotary press machines.
[B41F 13/0032] Auxiliary numbering devices -- The CPC subclass B41F 13/0032 relates to auxiliary numbering devices used with rotary printing machines. These devices add sequential numbers, dates, or other identifying marks to printed items such as forms, tickets, or invoices, and are integrated with or attached to the press for automatic marking during operation.
[B41F 13/004] Electric or hydraulic features of drives -- The subclass covers electric or hydraulic elements that power or control the drive of rotary printing machines, including motors, hydraulic cylinders, drive-control mechanisms and related power-transmission components.
[B41F 13/0045] Electric driving devices -- The CPC subclass B41F 13/0045 concerns electric driving devices for rotary printing presses, encompassing motors, gear drives, and related control mechanisms that convert electrical energy into motion to operate the press. It falls under the hierarchy Performing operations - Printing - Printing machines or presses - Common details of rotary presses or machines.
[B41F 13/008] Mechanical features of drives, e.g. gears, clutches -- B41F 13/008 pertains to the mechanical components of drive systems in rotary printing presses, specifically gears, clutches and similar transmission elements that transfer power to the printing unit. It is classified under performing operations, printing, printing machines or presses, describing common details of rotary presses.
[B41F 13/012] Taking-up backlash -- The CPC class B41F 13/012 covers the taking-up stage of rotary printing presses and the handling of backlash, that is, the slack or excessive movement that can occur in the web between the print and take-up sections. It includes devices and methods for controlling web tension, adjusting take-up rollers and preventing or correcting slack during continuous printing.
[B41F 13/016] Brakes -- B41F 13/016 refers to brakes for rotary printing presses. It covers devices that control the rotation of the press drum or cylinder by applying friction or other stopping mechanisms to regulate feed, tension, or stopping the press during operation.
[B41F 13/02] Conveying or guiding webs through presses or machines -- B41F 13/02 covers mechanisms that move or direct continuous webs of material within rotary printing presses or similar machines. It includes conveyors, rollers, guides and other devices that ensure precise web positioning and steady feed through printing zones, facilitating consistent image transfer and sheet formation.
[B41F 13/025] Registering devices -- Registering devices are mechanisms or sensors used in rotary printing presses to align and position printing forms, plates, or sheets accurately during operation, ensuring proper registration between successive impressions. They may include registration pins, optical detectors, or adjustable guides that coordinate paper feed and impression timing.
[B41F 13/03] Threading webs into printing machines -- The B41F 13/03 subclass addresses the feeding of continuous web material into printing presses, particularly rotary machines, by describing mechanisms that guide, align and introduce the web onto the press rollers. It covers methods and apparatus for threading the web from its roll into the printing machine.
[B41F 13/04] intermittently -- B41F 13/04 covers rotary printing presses that operate intermittently, meaning the web or sheet is indexed, stopped or advanced in steps rather than moving continuously during printing. It includes mechanisms for feeding, holding, and moving the material between successive impressions on rotary machines.
[B41F 13/06] Turning-bar arrangements -- Turning-bar arrangements are mechanisms in rotary printing presses that guide and support the web or sheet as it moves past the printing cylinder, providing precise tension control and alignment while redirecting the material through the press.
[B41F 13/08] Cylinders -- B41F 13/08 covers the cylinders of rotary printing presses, the rotating elements that hold the printing form and transfer ink to the substrate. The code includes design, mounting and drive features of these cylinders used in printing machines.
[B41F 13/085] with means for preventing or damping vibrations or shocks -- This subclass concerns rotary printing presses that incorporate devices to prevent or dampen vibrations and shocks during operation, such as dynamic balancers, shock absorbers or isolation mounts built into the machine. It is a specific refinement of printing machines or presses within the broader categories of performing operations and printing.
[B41F 13/10] Forme cylinders -- B41F 13/10 relates to forme cylinders, the rotary elements that carry the printing forme and rotate within a press to transfer ink onto the web. The code covers their construction, materials, surface treatments and mounting features as used in offset, gravure, flexographic and similar rotary printing machines.
[B41F 13/11] Gravure cylinders -- Gravure cylinders are the engraved rotary elements used in intaglio printing presses to transfer ink from recessed cells onto paper or other substrates. They are typically made of copper, steel or polymer and are mounted on the press cylinder to enable high-speed continuous printing.
[B41F 13/12] Registering devices -- The subclass B41F 13/12 covers devices that detect or control the position of printed material during operation of a rotary printing press, such as registration sensors, alignment marks, and mechanisms that ensure precise sheet or web registration before or during impression. It includes any apparatus that monitors or adjusts registration to maintain proper image alignment.
[B41F 13/14] with means for displacing the cylinders -- The subclass B41F 13/14 covers rotary printing presses that include mechanisms for moving or adjusting the position of the cylinders relative to each other, such as to achieve precise registration, control print width, or vary cylinder pressure during operation. These displacement means are integral to the common details of rotary presses.
[B41F 13/16] with means for displacing the printing formes on the cylinders -- This subclass covers rotary printing presses that include devices for moving the printing forms along the surface of the cylinders during operation. The displacement mechanisms allow rapid changing of the printed area, improve registration, or enable multiple impressions without stopping the press.
[B41F 13/18] Impression cylinders -- B41F 13/18 covers impression cylinders used in rotary printing presses, describing their design, surface treatment and mounting arrangements for transferring ink onto the substrate during the printing operation.
[B41F 13/187] for rotogravure -- The B41F 13/187 subgroup concerns rotary printing presses designed for rotogravure, where engraved cylinders transfer ink onto a substrate. It includes the specific arrangement of cylinders, ink-delivery systems and feed mechanisms that are characteristic of rotogravure machines.
[B41F 13/193] Transfer cylinders -- B41F 13/193 covers transfer cylinders used in rotary printing presses, describing their design, arrangement and function for transferring inked images from one cylinder to another or to the substrate. The code falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, printing, printing machines or presses, and common details of rotary presses.
[B41F 1/32] using air pressure, e.g. vacuum -- B41F 1/32 pertains to platen printing machines where the impression is produced by applying air pressure, such as a vacuum, to the flat pressure member that contacts the type-bed. The subclass groups presses that use pneumatic or vacuum suction instead of solely mechanical force to transfer inked images onto a substrate.
[B41F 13/20] Supports for bearings or supports for forme, offset, or impression cylinders -- B41F 13/20 pertains to components that hold or secure the bearings and the forme, offset or impression cylinders in rotary printing presses. These supports provide mechanical stability, precise alignment and mounting for the cylinders, ensuring reliable operation of the printing machine.
[B41F 13/21] Bearer rings -- Bearer rings are components of rotary printing presses that support and carry the printing cylinders, ensuring proper alignment and stability during operation. This subclass covers the design, construction, and variations of these rings used in common rotary press machines.
[B41F 13/22] Means for cooling or heating forme or impression cylinders -- Means for controlling the temperature of forme or impression cylinders in rotary printing presses, such as cooling channels, heating elements, fluid-circulation systems or combined devices that regulate cylinder temperature to improve ink transfer, paper handling and print quality.
[B41F 13/24] Cylinder-tripping devices -- B41F 13/24 concerns cylinder-tripping devices, which are mechanisms that release or stop the printing cylinder in rotary presses. These devices control cylinder rotation during start-up, shut-down or interruption of the printing cycle, ensuring precise positioning and preventing damage to the press or printwork.
[B41F 13/26] Arrangement of cylinder bearings -- The CPC entry B41F 13/26 covers the arrangement of cylinder bearings in rotary printing presses, detailing how bearing assemblies are positioned and secured to support the printing cylinders. It lies within the B41F group for printing machines, specifically the sub-class for common rotary-press features.
[B41F 13/28] Bearings mounted eccentrically of the cylinder axis -- This subclass covers rotary printing machines in which the cylinder's supporting bearings are positioned off-center from the cylinder axis, providing eccentric mounting. Such arrangement allows the cylinder to be tilted or displaced for improved pressure control and registration during printing.
[B41F 13/30] Bearings mounted on sliding supports -- B41F 13/30 relates to bearings that are mounted on sliding supports within rotary printing presses or machines. The classification covers devices that permit linear movement of the bearing while supporting a rotating shaft, facilitating adjustment, alignment and maintenance in printing equipment.
[B41F 13/32] Bearings mounted on swinging supports -- B41F 13/32 refers to the arrangement of bearings that are fixed to swinging supports in rotary printing presses. The classification includes designs where the bearing housing or the bearing itself is mounted on a pivoting or movable bracket, allowing adjustment or compensation of shaft alignment during operation. It falls under common details of rotary presses within the printing machines section of the CPC.
[B41F 13/34] Cylinder lifting or adjusting devices -- Cylinder lifting or adjusting devices are mechanisms installed in rotary printing presses that raise, lower, or tilt a printing cylinder to set its position relative to other cylinders, control pressure and ensure accurate sheet registration during operation. These devices enable fine tuning of cylinder alignment for consistent print quality.
[B41F 13/36] Cams, eccentrics, wedges, or the like -- This subclass covers cams, eccentrics, wedges and similar devices used in rotary printing presses to convert continuous rotary motion into controlled reciprocating or intermittent movements, facilitating functions such as plate feeding, paper transport, impression timing or pressure adjustment within the machine.
[B41F 13/38] electrically or magnetically operated -- This CPC subclass (B41F 13/38) covers rotary printing presses or machines that are powered by electrical or magnetic means, including their drive systems, control devices and related components. It is situated under the hierarchy performing operations - printing - printing machines or presses - common details of rotary presses.
[B41F 1/34] Registering devices, e.g. gauges -- The CPC class B41F 1/34 relates to registering devices such as gauges that monitor and record printing parameters on platen presses, where a flat pressure member contacts a flat type-bed. These devices help ensure proper alignment, pressure distribution and other process controls during printing operations.
[B41F 13/40] fluid-pressure operated -- B41F 13/40 covers rotary printing machines that are driven by fluid pressure, such as hydraulic or pneumatic systems, to transfer ink onto a substrate. It pertains to presses where the motion of the print cylinder or web is generated by fluid-pressure mechanisms rather than mechanical gearing.
[B41F 13/42] Guards or covers, e.g. for preventing ingress or egress of foreign matter -- B41F 13/42 relates to protective guards or covers used on rotary printing machines or presses, designed to prevent foreign material from entering or exiting the machine during operation, such as shields for rollers, plates, or other moving parts.
[B41F 13/44] Arrangements to accommodate interchangeable cylinders of different sizes to enable machine to print on areas of different sizes -- This subclass deals with mechanisms that let a rotary printing press interchange cylinders of varying diameters so the machine can print on sections of different sizes. It includes adjustable cylinder beds, quick-change fittings and modular frames that enable rapid reconfiguration of the press.
[B41F 13/46] Printing units for inserting latest news -- This CPC subclass B41F 13/46 covers printing units that insert the latest news into publications, such as newspaper inserts or supplements, and is used in rotary printing presses. It includes mechanisms that feed, align and apply fresh news content during the printing process.
[B41F 13/48] Arrangements to enable printing to be effected over selected areas of a single forme cylinder -- The code covers devices or methods that selectively apply ink to specific regions of a single forme cylinder, allowing printing to be performed only on chosen areas of the cylinder surface while the remaining portions remain unprinted.
[B41F 13/50] by effecting relative movement of forme and impression cylinders during printing cycle -- B41F 13/50 covers rotary printing presses that achieve printing by moving the form cylinder and the impression cylinder relative to each other during the printing cycle, such as by shifting, rotating or otherwise adjusting one cylinder with respect to the other to transfer ink.
[B41F 13/52] by projecting and retracting parts of the surface of the forme cylinder -- B41F 13/52 covers rotary printing presses in which parts of the forme cylinder's surface are projected outward and then retracted, allowing the cylinder to form impressions by moving sections of the printing surface during operation. This mechanism enables variable or selective printing patterns on a single pass through the press.
[B41F 13/54] Auxiliary folding, cutting, collecting or depositing of sheets or webs -- B41F 13/54 covers auxiliary mechanisms that fold, cut, collect or deposit printed sheets or continuous webs in rotary printing presses. It includes devices that handle the material after printing, such as folder-cutters, stackers or depositors integrated with the press.
[B41F 13/56] Folding or cutting -- B41F 13/56 relates to the folding or cutting functions of rotary printing presses, covering devices and mechanisms that trim, crease or fold printed webs or sheets as part of the printing operation. It includes cutting knives, scoring tools and folding units integrated into the press, as well as arrangements for synchronising these processes with the rotary motion.
[B41F 13/58] lengthwise -- B41F 13/58 covers features of rotary printing presses that relate to the lengthwise orientation or movement of the web, feed mechanisms or component arrangement along the machine's longitudinal axis.
[B41F 1/36] Clamps for conveying sheets and for holding same on the platens -- B41F 1/36 relates to clamps that convey individual sheets and hold them on the platens of flat-bed printing presses. These devices grip the paper while it is positioned and retain it during the printing cycle.
[B41F 13/60] crosswise -- B41F 13/60 relates to rotary printing presses where the printing units or cylinders are positioned crosswise to the direction of web travel, allowing the web to be printed across its width rather than along its length. This arrangement is used to achieve simultaneous multi-color or double-sided printing on a single pass.
[B41F 13/62] Folding-cylinders or drums -- B41F 13/62 covers the folding cylinders or drums used in rotary printing presses to bend printed sheets into a predetermined shape, such as a fold or roll. These components are designed to operate continuously with the press, guiding the material through precise folding actions during high-speed printing processes.
[B41F 13/64] Collecting -- The subclass B41F 13/64 concerns the devices that gather printed sheets from a rotary press, including collection bins, conveyors, stacking mechanisms and other systems that direct the output to a receptacle for subsequent handling. It covers the collection stage of the printed product in rotary-press operations.
[B41F 13/66] and stapling -- B41F 13/66 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to processes and devices for stapling in conjunction with common details of rotary presses or machines, focusing on the integration of stapling mechanisms within printing operations.
[B41F 13/68] Adding inserts -- This subcategory deals with techniques for placing additional elements into printed material during rotary press operation, such as inserting sheets, labels, brochures or other inserts into the web. It covers mechanisms, timing devices and control systems for insert insertion in rotary printing presses.
[B41F 13/70] Depositing -- B41F 13/70 pertains to the depositing stage in rotary printing presses, covering methods and apparatus for applying inks, coatings, or other substances onto a moving web or sheet. It includes mechanisms that control the amount, pattern and timing of deposition during the printing operation.
[B41F 13/705] Interleaving slip sheets -- Interleaving slip sheets are thin, non-adhesive sheets placed between printed or unfixed sheets in rotary printing presses to prevent sticking, facilitate handling, and protect surface quality. They are typically made of paper, film or coated material and may be pre-perforated for automatic feeding.
[B41F 1/38] Platens or type-beds -- This subclass covers the flat pressure members (platens) and the flat type-beds used in platen printing presses, describing their construction, mounting, adjustment and any heating or cooling features. It includes variations in size, material and the mechanisms by which the platens cooperate with the type-bed during printing.
[B41F 1/40] Inking units -- In the CPC, B41F 1/40 covers the inking units of platen printing presses, i.e., the mechanisms that transfer ink to the type or printing form before it contacts the paper. These units may include ink fountains, rollers, distribution systems and related adjustments, and are intended for flat-bed pressure-applying presses.
[B41F 1/42] using flat inking elements, e.g. discs -- B41F 1/42 relates to platen presses that employ flat inking elements such as disc-shaped rollers to apply ink onto the type bed. The classification covers arrangements where the ink-applying member is essentially flat, ensuring uniform transfer during printing.
[B41F 1/44] using inking ribbons -- This subclass covers methods and apparatus that employ inking ribbons to apply ink to type or printing forms in platen presses, where a flat pressure-applying member contacts a flat type-bed. It relates to the ribbon-based ink delivery process used in such printing machines.
[B41F 1/46] using rollers -- The subclass B41F 1/46 covers the use of rollers in platen presses, where a flat pressure-applying plate works with a flat type-bed. It includes rollers that feed, transfer, or support the sheet or substrate during printing in such machines.
[B41F 1/48] pivoting around the platen or base -- B41F 1/48 covers platen presses in which either the printing plate, impression cylinder or the entire press body pivots about the platen or the base of the machine, enabling angled or variable-force printing actions within flat-bed printing operations.
[B41F 1/50] supported on carriages for movement in or on runways -- This classification covers platen printing presses that are mounted on carriages allowing movement in or on runways. The carriage support lets the press be positioned and travelled along the printing line for flexible layout and automation.
[B41F 15/00] Screen printers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  screen printing apparatus, which always use a perforated printing screen, the perforated parts forming the printing image.  Apparatus and machines adapted to print on sheets or webs of any material, e.g. including textiles, and  adapted to print on any substrate having any shape, e.g. filamentary, cylindrical, polyhedral, conical, spherical and all kind of irregularly shaped three-dimensional surfaces.
[B41F 15/02] Manually-operable devices -- B41F 15/02 covers manually-operable devices used in screen-printing machines, such as hand-driven squeegees, frames or other tools that let an operator apply ink through a screen without powered assistance. These devices are intended for printing patterns or images on various substrates.
[B41F 15/04] for multicolour printing -- B41F 15/04 covers screen-printing devices or processes designed to apply several inks in a single operation, enabling multicolour images in one pass. It includes mechanisms that register multiple screens, control ink flow, and synchronize movements to produce overlapping colour layers without separate passes. This subclass is part of the broader category of printing machines and presses.
[B41F 15/06] with auxiliary equipment, e.g. for drying printed articles -- B41F 15/06 relates to screen-printing machines that incorporate auxiliary equipment, such as drying or curing devices, for treating printed articles after ink deposition.
[B41F 15/08] Machines -- B41F 15/08 designates machines employed in screen printing, encompassing the equipment that forces ink through a mesh stencil onto a substrate. These devices form a sub-category of printing machines or presses within the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[B41F 15/0804] for printing sheets -- This CPC subclass covers screen printing machines designed to print on individual sheets, focusing on the mechanisms that transfer ink through a mesh onto flat substrates. It falls under printing machines or presses within the performing operations sector.
[B41F 15/0809] with cylindrical or belt-like screens -- B41F 15/0809 covers screen printing machines that employ cylindrical or belt-like screens to transfer ink onto substrates. The classification applies to equipment where the screen is formed as a rotating cylinder or continuous belt, enabling continuous or high-speed printing. It falls under printing machines or presses, specifically screen printers.
[B41F 15/0813] with flat screens -- B41F 15/0813 designates screen-printing machines that employ flat screens rather than cylindrical ones. It falls under Performing Operations → Printing → Printing machines or presses → Screen printers and covers apparatus and processes for flattening the mesh to achieve uniform ink transfer onto substrates.
[B41F 15/0818] with a stationary screen and a moving squeegee -- This subclass covers screen-printing devices in which the screen remains fixed while a squeegee travels relative to it, depositing ink onto the workpiece. The moving squeegee may be driven mechanically, pneumatically or hydraulically and is used for continuous or batch printing on flat or curved substrates.
[B41F 15/0822] on a stack of sheets -- The subclass B41F 15/0822 covers screen-printing techniques in which the print image is formed on a stack of sheets, typically by forcing ink through a screen onto the top sheet while the stack is held together, allowing simultaneous printing of multiple sheets. It is a sub-category of screen printers within the broader group of printing machines and presses.
[B41F 15/0827] with a stationary squeegee and a moving screen -- B41F 15/0827 denotes screen-printing equipment in which the squeegee stays stationary while the screen travels, typically across the printed material. The arrangement forces ink through the moving mesh onto the substrate, differing from conventional designs where the squeegee moves. This subclass falls under printing machines or presses in the CPC hierarchy.
[B41F 15/0831] for printing webs -- B41F 15/0831 covers screen printing machines that apply inks or other substances to continuous web materials such as paper, film or fabric. The classification is used for equipment designed to print on moving webs rather than discrete items. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, printing, and printing machines or presses.
[B41F 15/0836] by means of cylindrical screens or screens in the form of endless belts -- B41F 15/0836 covers screen-printing techniques where the mesh is formed as a rotating cylinder or an endless belt, allowing continuous transfer of ink onto a substrate. The method enables high-speed, uniform application and is used for printing on various materials such as textiles, plastics or metals.
[B41F 15/084] simultaneously printing on two sides -- The B41F 15/084 subclass covers screen-printing machines that are capable of applying ink to both sides of a material in a single operation, using coordinated screens or print heads to deposit images simultaneously on opposite faces, typically for thin or flexible substrates.
[B41F 15/0845] with flat screens -- B41F 15/0845 covers screen-printing equipment that employs flat, planar mesh screens for transferring ink onto substrates. The classification pertains to machines where the screen lies parallel to the workpiece, distinguishing them from rotary or cylindrical screen systems.
[B41F 15/085] with a stationary screen and a moving squeegee -- This subclass covers screen-printing apparatus in which the mesh screen remains fixed while the squeegee traverses across it, applying ink to the substrate. The moving squeegee creates the print line, distinguishing it from machines where both screen and squeegee move.
[B41F 15/0854] with a stationary squeegee and a moving screen -- B41F 15/0854 covers screen-printing equipment in which the squeegee remains fixed while the screen moves relative to it, enabling the ink to be transferred onto the substrate. This arrangement is used in devices classified under printing machines or presses, specifically screen printers with a stationary squeegee and a moving screen.
[B41F 15/0859] movable on a long printing table -- The B41F 15/0859 subclass pertains to screen-printing machines in which the screen or printing head is mounted on a long table and can be moved along its length to print across extended substrates such as wide fabrics or large panels. This arrangement allows continuous, linear positioning for uniform image transfer during the screen-printing process.
[B41F 15/0863] with a plurality of flat screens mounted on a turntable -- B41F 15/0863 covers screen-printing machines that employ several flat screens arranged on a common turntable, allowing the workpiece to be indexed sequentially under each screen for continuous multi-color printing. This subclass falls under printing machines or presses within the performing operations field.
[B41F 15/0868] for printing on filamentary articles -- B41F 15/0868 designates screen-printing processes that deposit ink, pigment or other functional layers onto filamentary articles such as threads, yarns, cords or ropes. The classification covers machines, screens and methods adapted to feed, support and print on these elongated, flexible substrates, often in continuous or batch operations.
[B41F 15/0872] for printing on articles having essentially cylindrical surfaces -- B41F 15/0872 covers screen-printing techniques specially adapted for applying images or markings to items whose shape is essentially cylindrical, such as bottles, tubes, cans or rollers. The method typically involves rotating the workpiece while a screen transfers the ink, allowing uniform coverage around the circumference.
[B41F 15/0877] of varying cross-section -- The CPC subclass B41F 15/0877 covers screen-printing apparatus or methods in which the screen openings have a varying cross-section, enabling differential ink flow across the print area. This feature allows the printer to produce patterns or gradients by altering the amount of ink transferred through differently shaped apertures. It is a specific refinement within the broader category of screen-printing machines.
[B41F 15/0881] for printing on polyhedral articles -- A subclass of screen-printing machines, B41F 15/0881 covers apparatus or processes specifically adapted for printing on polyhedral articles such as dice, prisms or other multi-sided objects. It details the means of applying ink or other media to the flat faces of these shaped items, often using specialized screens, squeegees or positioning systems to ensure accurate registration on each facet.
[B41F 15/0886] for printing on conical or frusto-conical surfaces -- This subclass covers screen-printing techniques specifically designed to apply ink onto conical or frusto-conical objects such as bottles, cones or similar shapes. It includes apparatus and methods that adapt the screen, squeegee and feed mechanisms to accommodate the tapered surface.
[B41F 15/089] for printing on essentially spherical surfaces -- This CPC subclass B41F 15/089 relates to methods and apparatuses for printing on objects that are essentially spherical, such as balls or globes. It falls under printing machines or presses, specifically screen printers, and covers techniques for applying ink or other printable media to curved spherical surfaces.
[B41F 15/0895] for printing on curved surfaces not otherwise provided for -- This classification covers screen-printing methods specifically designed for applying ink to curved objects where standard techniques are not applicable. It falls under printing machines or presses, screen printers, and addresses operations that deposit pigment on non-flat surfaces. It is identified by code B41F 15/0895.
[B41F 15/10] for multicolour printing -- B41F 15/10 covers screen-printing equipment or processes designed to apply several colours in a single job, typically using multiple screens, stations or sequential passes to register different inks precisely on the substrate. It includes mechanisms that automate colour changes, alignments and curing steps to achieve multicolour prints efficiently.
[B41F 15/12] with auxiliary equipment, e.g. for drying printed articles -- B41F 15/12 designates screen-printing machines that incorporate auxiliary devices, such as drying or curing units, to treat printed articles immediately after ink application. This subclass concerns integrated systems where the printing apparatus and the post-process equipment function together to improve production speed and print quality.
[B41F 15/14] Details -- B41F 15/14 covers specific details of screen-printing machines, including the construction, configuration and components that affect the deposition of ink through a mesh screen onto a substrate. It addresses elements such as squeegees, screen frames, registration systems and cleaning mechanisms used in screen-printing presses.
[B41F 15/16] Printing tables -- Printing tables are auxiliary devices used in screen-printing machines to hold, align or support the substrate and screen during the impression process. They may be adjustable, segmented or feature mechanisms for precise positioning, allowing consistent registration and pressure across multiple prints. This equipment falls under CPC class B41F 15/16.
[B41F 15/18] Supports for workpieces -- B41F 15/18 covers devices that hold or position the material being printed in screen-printing machines, such as frames, pads, clamps, fixtures or other supporting structures that keep workpieces stable during ink transfer. These supports are designed to maintain alignment, tension or planarity of the substrate while the screen prints onto it.
[B41F 1/52] Carriage driving gear -- The subclass B41F 1/52 covers the mechanisms that drive the carriage in platen printing presses, such as gears, belts and other transmission components that move the type-bed relative to the platen.
[B41F 15/20] with suction-operated elements -- The B41F 15/20 subclass pertains to screen-printing machines that incorporate suction-operated elements, such as suction tables, rollers or belts, to hold or index the substrate during the printing process. These features improve registration, prevent material drift and enable rapid handling of patterned workpieces within the screen-printer.
[B41F 15/22] for single sheets -- B41F 15/22 classifies screen-printing machines that are designed to print on individual sheets rather than continuous rolls or fabrics. It covers apparatuses whose feed, registration and drying systems are arranged to handle single-sheet loading, alignment and ejection, enabling precise control of each printed piece.
[B41F 15/24] for webs -- The code B41F 15/24 designates screen-printing machines that operate on continuous webs of material, such as paper, film or textile. It covers apparatus and methods for feeding, positioning and printing the web through a screen, typically for high-speed, roll-to-roll production. This subclass falls under printing machines or presses within the broader category of performing operations.
[B41F 15/26] for articles with flat surfaces -- The B41F 15/26 subclass covers screen-printing apparatus and methods specifically adapted for printing on flat-surface items such as sheets, panels or plates. It includes mechanisms that hold, feed, or support the flat workpiece during the squeegee impression of the stencil. The classification is used for patents on equipment that deposits inks or coatings on flat substrates.
[B41F 15/28] for elongated flat articles, e.g. strips, bands -- The B41F 15/28 sub-class refers to screen-printing machines or methods adapted for applying ink to elongated flat workpieces such as strips, bands or similar narrow articles. It covers the specific mechanisms, feed systems and control features needed to handle and print continuously on these shapes.
[B41F 15/30] for articles with curved surfaces -- B41F 15/30 refers to screen-printing machines or presses specifically adapted for printing on articles with curved surfaces, such as bottles, cans, or other three-dimensional objects, allowing ink or coating to be applied uniformly to non-flat substrates.
[B41F 15/32] for articles with conical surfaces -- B41F 15/32 classifies screen-printing machines that are specifically adapted to print on articles having conical surfaces, such as bottles, cones or tapered parts. The entry covers features for feeding, aligning and applying ink to these shaped objects during the printing process.
