[B41F 15/34] Screens, Frames -- B41F 15/34 covers the screens and frames used in screen-printing machines, including the mesh panels that hold the stencil pattern and the supporting frames that tension and position the screens during ink transfer. These components are essential for applying designs onto substrates with screen-printing presses.
[B41F 15/36] flat -- B41F 15/36 covers flat screen-printing machines, a subgroup of screen printers designed for applying inks through a flat, rigid screen onto substrates. These machines operate on a planar surface, allowing uniform, continuous printing for applications such as textiles, graphics and industrial markings. They are distinguished from rotary or curved-screen equipment by their flat work-piece handling and printing geometry.
[B41F 15/38] curved -- B41F 15/38 covers screen-printing equipment or methods that print on curved surfaces or produce curved patterns. It includes devices, tools, or processes that enable the deposition of ink onto non-flat substrates using screen-printing techniques. The class is part of the broader printing machines and presses category.
[B41F 1/54] Printing-pressure control devices -- This subclass covers devices that regulate and adjust the pressure applied by the platen during printing, ensuring uniform contact between type and substrate. It includes mechanisms such as cams, springs, hydraulic or pneumatic regulators that modulate pressure in platen presses.
[B41F 15/40] Inking units -- B41F 15/40 covers the inking units of screen-printing machines, i.e., the components that deliver, spread or control ink onto the screen mesh before transfer to the substrate. The claim includes mechanisms for ink reservoir, pump, distribution rollers and related control devices.
[B41F 15/405] Spraying apparatus -- A B41F 15/405 device is a spraying apparatus used in screen-printing machines to apply ink or other coating materials onto a substrate. It typically consists of a pump, nozzle and control system that atomises the fluid for even distribution across the screen. This equipment enhances precision and speed in the printing process.
[B41F 15/42] comprising squeegees or doctors -- B41F 15/42 covers screen-printing machines that incorporate squeegees or doctors as the means for forcing ink through the screen onto the substrate. The subclass includes any arrangement where such squeegees or doctors are integral to the printer's ink-transfer mechanism.
[B41F 15/423] Driving means for reciprocating squeegees -- B41F 15/423 covers the mechanisms that drive the back and forth movement of squeegees in screen-printing machines. These driving means may include cam systems, linkages, pneumatic or hydraulic actuators that convert motion to reciprocating squeegee strokes, enabling uniform ink transfer across the screen.
[B41F 15/426] the squeegees or doctors being magnetically attracted -- B41F 15/426 pertains to screen-printing equipment in which the squeegees or doctors are magnetically attracted to the screen. It covers mechanisms that employ magnetic forces to hold or guide the squeegee, improving pressure control and alignment during ink transfer.
[B41F 15/44] Squeegees or doctors -- B41F 15/44 covers the squeegees or doctor blades used in screen-printing machines to transfer ink through the screen onto a substrate, typically by moving across the screen to spread and press the ink evenly. These components are integral to the operation of screen printers for applying patterns or images.
[B41F 15/46] with two or more operative parts -- B41F 15/46 classifies screen-printing machines that incorporate two or more operative parts, such as dual squeegees, multiple ink-delivery units or combined drying and printing sections, allowing simultaneous or coordinated actions during the print cycle. The code applies to equipment where the functional components work together to transfer a screen image onto a substrate.
[B41F 1/56] Auxiliary devices -- This subclass covers auxiliary devices used with platen presses, where printing is performed by a flat pressure member against a flat type bed. It includes accessories that support or enhance the operation of such presses, such as feeding mechanisms, registration aides, or cleaning tools.
[B41F 1/565] Numbering devices  stamping numbering devices  B41K -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code B41F 1/565 pertains to numbering devices, specifically those used in platen presses where printing is performed using at least one flat pressure-applying member and a flat type-bed. This classification covers mechanisms for stamping numbers in such printing systems.
[B41F 1/58] Arrangements of counting devices for counting sheets -- B41F 1/58 pertains to platen printing presses and defines the layout and integration of sheet-counting mechanisms within such machines. It covers any structural or functional arrangement of devices that detect, tally or display the number of sheets processed, facilitating control of print runs and preventing over-feeding.
[B41F 1/60] Safety devices -- The CPC subclass B41F 1/60 covers safety devices for platen printing presses, which are machines that print by pressing a flat type-bed against a flat pressure-applying member. It includes mechanisms such as guards, interlocks, emergency stops and monitoring systems that protect operators and prevent accidents during the printing operation.
[B41F 16/00] Transfer printing apparatus -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines or presses which always use a sheet or web-like coloured or preprinted transfer element,  a printing element, usually a printing plate, applying by contact pressure said transfer element onto a substrate to be printed upon.
[B41F 16/0006] for printing from an inked or preprinted foil or band -- B41F 16/0006 covers transfer-printing devices that apply an image from an inked, pre-printed foil or band onto a workpiece. It includes mechanisms that feed the foil, align it with the substrate and press it to transfer the ink, typically used in industrial printing presses for decorative or labeling applications.
[B41F 16/0013] combined with other printing presses -- The CPC subclass B41F 16/0013 covers transfer printing devices that are integrated with additional printing presses. It pertains to machines that combine a transfer printing apparatus with one or more other press types to perform multiple printing operations in a single unit. This classification falls under printing machines or presses within the performing operations sector.
[B41F 16/002] Presses of the rotary type -- B41F 16/002 covers rotary-type presses used in transfer printing, where the image is transferred from a printing plate or carrier to the substrate by a rotating cylinder system. These machines are designed to apply inks or coatings continuously as the material moves through the press.
[B41F 16/0026] with means for applying print under heat and pressure, e.g. using heat activable adhesive -- B41F 16/0026 covers transfer-printing machines that incorporate devices for applying the image by heating and pressing, typically using a heat-activable adhesive to bond the print onto the substrate. This subclass refines the broader category of printing machines or presses.
[B41F 16/0033] with means for applying print under pressure only, e.g. using pressure sensitive adhesive -- The B41F 16/0033 subclass covers transfer-printing devices that apply the image to a substrate only when pressure is exerted, typically by employing a pressure-sensitive adhesive. It pertains to machines that press the printed medium against the target material rather than using heat, solvent, or other activation methods. This subclass lies within printing-machine categories under performing operations.
[B41F 16/004] Presses of the reciprocating type -- B41F 16/004 covers printing presses that work by a reciprocating motion, where the printing plate or blanket moves back-and-forth relative to the material being printed. These machines are applied in transfer-printing processes and employ a linear cyclic movement rather than continuous rotary action.
[B41F 16/0046] with means for applying print under heat and pressure, e.g. using heat activable adhesive -- This class covers transfer printing machines that apply a print to a substrate by means of a heat-activable adhesive, using combined heat and pressure to bond the image. The apparatus includes elements that heat the adhesive layer and press the print against the target material, enabling permanent transfer of graphics or text.
[B41F 16/0053] with means for applying print under pressure only, e.g. using pressure sensitive adhesive -- The CPC subclass B41F 16/0053 relates to transfer printing apparatus that apply the print solely by pressure, for example using a pressure-sensitive adhesive, without requiring heat or chemical activation. It is part of the broader category of printing machines and presses within the performing operations section.
[B41F 16/006] Arrangements for moving, supporting or positioning the printing foil or band -- The subclass B41F 16/006 covers mechanisms that transport, hold or align the foil or band used in transfer printing machines. It includes rollers, guides, clamps and indexing systems designed to ensure precise movement and positioning of the printing medium during operation.
[B41F 16/0066] Printing dies or forms -- B41F 16/0066 covers printing dies or forms employed in transfer-printing processes, where the die carries the image or pattern that is later transferred onto a substrate such as fabric or metal. These components receive ink or pigment and define the shape of the transferred design, and are integral to machines that apply the pattern by pressure or heat.
[B41F 16/0073] with means for printing on specific materials or products -- B41F 16/0073 pertains to transfer printing apparatus equipped with devices or attachments that enable printing on designated materials or products, such as fabrics, plastics, or metal parts. The classification captures machines that incorporate material-specific feeding, registration or curing mechanisms to ensure accurate image transfer onto the chosen substrate. It is a subcategory of printing machines within the performing operations field.
[B41F 16/008] for printing on three-dimensional articles -- The CPC subclass B41F 16/008 refers to transfer-printing apparatus designed to apply ink, pigment or other print media onto three-dimensional articles, such as molded or shaped objects with complex surfaces. It covers machines that transfer patterns or markings onto items whose geometry cannot be printed by flat-bed methods.
[B41F 16/0086] for printing on articles with cylindrical surfaces -- The subclass covers printing apparatus and methods that transfer ink, pigment or other markings onto items with cylindrical surfaces such as bottles, tubes, cans or rods. It includes devices that use rotary drums, pads, sleeves or similar transfer elements to apply a continuous or repeated image onto the curved exterior of the article.
[B41F 16/0093] Attachments or auxiliary devices -- B41F 16/0093 covers attachments or auxiliary devices for transfer printing apparatus, including supplemental mechanisms, guides, fixtures and other accessories that aid in positioning, feeding or processing substrates. The subclass pertains to devices that enhance or support the primary transfer printing machine during operation.
[B41F 16/02] for textile material -- B41F 16/02 covers transfer-printing apparatus designed specifically for applying printed images or patterns onto textile material. It falls under the printing machines and presses hierarchy, addressing devices that transfer designs from a carrier onto fabrics using heat, pressure, or chemical processes.
[B41F 1/62] for preventing injury to operator -- B41F 1/62 pertains to platen printing presses and specifies safety measures that prevent injury to the operator, such as protective guards, interlocks, or automatic shutdown devices that act when hazardous conditions are detected.
[B41F 1/64] to the hands of the operator -- This subgroup covers aspects of platen presses that involve the hands of the operator, such as controls, grips, or handling of the press that are directly manipulated. It includes ergonomic features, hand-operated levers, and safety devices affecting how the operator contacts the machine.
[B41F 1/66] responsive to incorrect operating conditions -- The code covers platen printing machines equipped with mechanisms that detect or correct abnormal operating conditions such as excess pressure, temperature spikes, mis-registration or faulty feed, and automatically adjust or halt the press to prevent damage or print defects. It includes sensors, control circuits and safety interlocks designed for real-time response to such irregularities.
[B41F 17/00] Printing apparatus or machines of special types or for particular purposes, not otherwise provided for -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pad printing apparatus,  textile printing apparatus,  printing apparatus using at least one printing belt,  printing apparatus for printing or numbering on non sheet- or web-like substrates having the following three-dimensional shapes:  or printing on specific articles, e.g.  books, manifolding sets e.g. booklets, calendars, tablets, pills, knitted fabric and hollow articles.
[B41F 17/001] Pad printing apparatus or machines -- Pad printing apparatus or machines are devices that transfer ink from a patterned plate onto a workpiece using a flexible silicone pad, allowing high-resolution images on irregular or three-dimensional surfaces. They are commonly employed for decorating promotional items, electronic components, and medical devices.
[B41F 17/002] Supports of workpieces in machines for printing on hollow articles -- This subclass includes any device that secures or holds hollow workpieces-such as bottles, tubes, or containers-while they are being printed, encompassing clamps, mandrels, fixtures and other support structures used in printing machines designed for hollow articles.
[B41F 17/003] Special types of machines for printing textiles  using the screen printing process  B41F15/00 ; printing on knitted fabrics  B41F17/38 -- This subclass covers printing apparatus designed specifically for applying inks to textile materials, especially knitted fabrics, by screen printing. Machines in this group are specialized beyond general printing presses to handle flexible, stretchable substrates and provide precise registration for textile patterns.
[B41F 17/005] Supports for workpieces; Devices for securing the workpieces to the supports -- The CPC subclass B41F 17/005 covers devices that hold or attach workpieces to the supporting structures of printing machines, such as fixtures, clamps, and mounting frames. It includes any specialised supports designed to secure plates, cylinders, or other printing elements during operation.
[B41F 17/006] for printing on curved surfaces not otherwise provided for -- B41F 17/006 refers to printing apparatus designed for applying ink or other print materials to curved surfaces that are not covered by other classifications. It belongs to the subgroup for special-type printing machines within the broader categories of printing and performing operations.
[B41F 17/007] Use of printing belts -- B41F 17/007 covers the use of printing belts in printing machines, including belts that convey substrate, transfer ink or improve registration between printing units. It applies to any belt-driven feeding, registration or intermediate transport system within special-type presses.
[B41F 17/008] Numbering devices for printing on articles other than sheets or webs -- This CPC subclass covers devices that apply sequential numbers, barcodes or other identifying marks to items such as containers, bottles or packaged goods during printing, rather than to flat sheets or continuous webs. It includes mechanisms integrated in printing equipment that position and imprint numbers onto individual articles.
[B41F 17/02] for printing books or manifolding sets -- B41F 17/02 covers printing machines specially adapted for producing books or manifolding sets, including equipment that folds, collates and binds sheets into signatures. The subclass addresses devices configured for the full book-making process, whether offset, letterpress or digital, when they are integral to the printing operation.
[B41F 17/04] for printing calendars -- This subclass pertains to printing apparatus specially adapted for producing calendars, encompassing machines that handle the layout, date sequencing and often the binding or finishing steps required for calendar manufacture.
[B41F 17/08] for printing on filamentary or elongated articles, or on articles with cylindrical surfaces -- The B41F 17/08 subclass covers printing apparatus or machines specifically designed to apply inks, pigments or coatings to filamentary or elongated items such as yarn, thread, wire, rope or to objects with cylindrical surfaces like tubes and rods, where the primary purpose is to produce printed markings, patterns or information directly on those shapes.
[B41F 17/10] on articles of indefinite length, e.g. wires, hoses, tubes, yarns -- This CPC subclass B41F 17/10 covers printing on articles of indefinite length such as wires, hoses, tubes or yarns, i.e. continuous or elongated substrates where a print is applied. It relates to printing apparatus or methods for such items.
[B41F 17/12] at an angle to axis -- The subclass deals with printing devices where the printing unit or the sheet is arranged at an angle to the main axis of the machine, such as off-set or inclined feed. It covers mechanisms that direct the printing medium or the print head obliquely to achieve specific printing effects or handling.
[B41F 17/13] for printing on rolls of material, the roll serving as impression cylinder, e.g. strip printers -- B41F 17/13 covers printing machines that print directly onto a continuous roll of material, using the material roll itself as the impression cylinder, as found in strip-printer configurations. This subclass is applied to apparatus designed for roll-to-roll printing where the substrate also forms the impression surface.
[B41F 17/14] on articles of finite length -- B41F 17/14 covers printing operations performed on items that have a defined, limited length, such as ribbons, tapes, or narrow web materials. It applies to machines or apparatus specially configured to handle and print on these finite-length articles, distinguishing them from continuous-web printing processes.
[B41F 17/16] on end or bottom surfaces thereof -- B41F 17/16 classifies features applied to the end or bottom surfaces of special-type printing machines, such as plates, guides, rollers or other components that are mounted on those surfaces to support, align or modify the printed product. It covers structural or functional elements specifically arranged on the machine's extremities or base.
[B41F 17/18] on curved surfaces of articles of varying cross-section, e.g. bottles, lamp glasses -- This subclass covers apparatus or methods for applying printed matter to curved surfaces of objects whose cross-section changes along their length, such as bottles, lamp glasses and similar items, where the shape is not uniform and requires specialized handling.
[B41F 17/20] on articles of uniform cross-section, e.g. pencils, rulers, resistors -- The CPC subclass B41F 17/20 pertains to printing on articles that have a uniform cross-section such as pencils, rulers or resistors. It covers methods and apparatuses for applying ink, toner or other markings to these consistently shaped items.
[B41F 17/22] by rolling contact -- B41F 17/22 covers printing apparatus in which the image-forming element contacts the substrate by rolling, typically using a printing roller that presses against a cylinder, plate or blanket. The classification includes machines that employ rubber, polymer or metal rollers to transfer ink, sublimation material or other media through direct rolling contact, and it excludes devices that use non-rolling methods such as stamping or direct impact.
[B41F 17/24] for printing on flat surfaces of polyhedral articles -- This subgroup covers printing apparatus or methods adapted to apply ink, toner or other media onto the flat faces of polyhedral objects, such as dice, prisms or multi-sided containers. It is part of the broader class for printing machines and presses, focusing on devices designed for precise, face-by-face deposition on three-dimensional items with flat surfaces.
[B41F 17/26] by rolling contact -- B41F 17/26 covers printing machines that form images by rolling contact, using rollers to transfer ink or other marking material onto a substrate. It applies to special-type or purpose-specific printing apparatus not covered by other subclasses, fitting within the broader categories of performing operations, printing, and printing machines or presses.
[B41F 17/28] for printing on curved surfaces of conical or frusto-conical articles -- This subclass covers printing apparatus or methods designed to apply ink, coating or decoration to the curved surface of articles that are conical or frusto-conical in shape, such as bottles, containers, or tapered objects, enabling uniform coverage on non-flat geometry.
[B41F 17/30] for printing on curved surfaces of essentially spherical, or part-spherical, articles -- This subclass of B41F 17 deals with machines or devices designed to apply ink or other markings onto surfaces that are curved in a spherical or partially spherical shape, such as balls, globes, or domes. It includes mechanisms that conform to the curvature to achieve uniform print and may involve rotating or stationary applicators adapted for such shapes.
[B41F 17/32] on lamp bulbs -- B41F 17/32 covers lamp bulbs employed in printing machines or presses, addressing the design, mounting, and replacement of illumination devices that are integral to the operation of such equipment.
[B41F 17/34] on articles with surface irregularities, e.g. fruits, nuts -- The subclass deals with printing processes applied to items whose surfaces are uneven or textured, such as fruits, nuts, or other irregularly shaped objects, using specialized apparatus. It includes methods for depositing ink or coating onto such irregular surfaces to achieve readable marks or decorative patterns.
[B41F 17/36] for printing on tablets, pills, or like small articles -- B41F 17/36 covers printing apparatus or processes specifically designed to apply markings, codes or graphics onto small solid articles such as tablets, pills or similar dosage forms. It includes specialized machines that can precisely deposit ink or other marking materials on these compact items, often for identification, branding or regulatory purposes.
[B41F 17/38] for printing on knitted fabrics -- This subclass covers apparatus and methods for applying ink, pigment or other printing media onto knitted textile material. It includes devices specially adapted to handle the stretch and looped structure of knit fabrics during printing, ensuring uniform coverage and minimal distortion. The classification is used for printing machines and processes not otherwise provided for in broader categories.
[B41F 19/00] Apparatus or machines for carrying out printing operations combined with other operations -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Embossing operations are herein considered to deform the substrate without colouring the substrate.
[B41F 19/001] with means for coating or laminating -- B41F 19/001 covers printing presses that incorporate additional units for applying a coating or a laminate to the printed sheet, enabling simultaneous printing and surface treatment. It includes machines where coating or lamination follows or accompanies the print process in a single operational line.
[B41F 19/002] with means for applying specific material other than ink -- B41F 19/002 designates printing apparatus that integrate a device for applying a material other than ink, such as adhesives, varnish, or protective coating, while performing the printing operation. The means are combined with the printing function in a single machine.
[B41F 19/004] with means for applying adhesive -- Apparatus or machines for printing that also incorporate a system for applying adhesive, such as rollers, spray nozzles or glue-dispensing units integrated into the press, enabling simultaneous printing and bonding of the printed substrate. This sub-class covers devices where the adhesive-applying means are built into the printing operation.
[B41F 19/005] with means for applying metallic, conductive or chargeable material -- B41F 19/005 covers printing apparatus that integrate a printing process with the application of metallic, conductive or otherwise chargeable material. Such machines combine ordinary printing operations with additional steps like metal deposition, conductive ink laying or electro-plating to produce printed electronics, functional coatings or decorative finishes in a single unit.
[B41F 19/007] with selective printing mechanisms, e.g. ink-jet or thermal printers -- This subclass covers printing apparatus that incorporate selective printing mechanisms-such as ink-jet or thermal heads-within machines that combine printing with other operations. It applies to devices where individual dots or patterns are deposited on a substrate under electronic control, enabling variable image formation alongside additional processing steps.
[B41F 19/008] with means for stamping or cutting out -- This subclass covers printing machines that also include devices for stamping or cutting out printed material, integrating these functions into the same apparatus. It applies to combined printing and finishing equipment where the stamping or cutting operation is performed directly after printing.
[B41F 19/02] with embossing -- This subclass covers printing machines that integrate embossing functions, allowing raised patterns to be formed on the printed substrate during the printing process. It includes devices that combine ink transfer with embossing rollers or plates in a single operation.
[B41F 19/04] using intaglio printing formes and wipers -- B41F 19/04 covers machines that integrate intaglio printing forms with wipers to enable combined printing operations, allowing simultaneous inking and cleaning of the printing surface. These devices are used where a printing press is combined with additional processing steps.
[B41F 19/06] Printing and embossing between a negative and a positive forme after inking and wiping the negative forme -- The B41F 19/06 class covers printing or embossing devices that place a negative forme and a positive forme together, after the negative has been inked and wiped, to transfer ink and create an image. Such apparatus combine the printing step with additional operations, like embossing, within a single machine.
[B41F 19/062] Presses of the rotary type -- B41F 19/062 covers rotary-type presses that integrate printing functions with additional processing steps in a single machine. These devices use continuously rotating cylinders to transfer inked images onto substrates while simultaneously performing operations such as cutting, folding, or laminating. The classification applies to equipment designed for high-speed, combined printing and post-printing tasks.
[B41F 19/064] Presses of the reciprocating type -- B41F 19/064 relates to reciprocating presses in which the printing plate or impression cylinder moves back-and-forth relative to the substrate to transfer ink, often combined with additional functions such as cutting, laminating or drying in sheet-fed or web printing processes.
[B41F 19/066] hand-driven -- B41F 19/066 covers printing presses that are powered manually by hand operation while performing a combined printing and additional process. These devices integrate the printing step with another function and are driven without electric or hydraulic power.
[B41F 19/068] motor-driven -- The CPC entry B41F 19/068 pertains to printing apparatus or presses that incorporate motor-driven mechanisms to perform the printing step in combination with other operations. It covers machines where the printing function is powered by an electric or other motor rather than by manual force, and falls under the hierarchy of Performing Operations - Printing - Printing Machines or Presses.
[B41F 19/08] Simultaneous moulding and printing -- B41F 19/08 covers apparatus that perform printing at the same time as a moulding process, integrating a printing unit with a moulding machine so that the substrate is printed while being shaped or formed. The invention combines the two operations in a single piece of equipment, eliminating the need for separate printing and moulding stages.
[B41F 21/00] Common details of printing machinery -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices or apparatus for conveying sheets  and devices or apparatus for guiding sheets.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Printing apparatus or machines through platen presses -> B41F1/28
[B41F 21/005] Electrostatic holding down devices -- B41F 21/005 covers electrostatic holding-down devices used in printing presses to secure paper, foil or other substrates against the printing surface. These systems generate an electrostatic charge that creates a uniform adhesive force, improving sheet alignment, reducing slippage and enabling high-speed, high-quality printing operations.
[B41F 21/02] Pins -- B41F 21/02 covers pins that are incorporated in printing machines or presses to secure plates, rollers, or other components during operation. The entry includes removable, adjustable and fixed pins used for positioning or fastening within the machinery.
[B41F 21/04] Grippers -- B41F 21/04 refers to grippers employed in printing machines or presses, covering devices that hold, feed, pull or release printed sheets or substrates during operation. These components are part of the common details of printing machinery, ensuring precise sheet handling and positioning throughout the printing process.
[B41F 21/05] In-feed grippers -- B41F 21/05 refers to the components of printing machines that grip the substrate as it is fed into the press. In-feed grippers include claws, suction pads, rollers or other mechanisms that seize, align and advance paper, film or other printable material for subsequent printing operations.
[B41F 21/06] Suction-operated grippers -- Suction-operated grippers are devices that use vacuum to grip, hold, or transport printed sheets, webs, plates or other workpieces within printing presses. They are employed to secure material during feeding, aligning, transferring, or releasing operations in various printing machines.
[B41F 21/08] Combinations of endless conveyors and grippers -- The entry covers printing machines that integrate endless conveyor belts with gripping devices to transport, position, or remove printed media. It includes configurations where the conveyor and gripper work together to feed, align, or discharge sheets during printing operations. This combination enhances handling efficiency in presses and related equipment.
[B41F 21/10] Combinations of transfer drums and grippers -- B41F 21/10 defines printing machines that integrate transfer drums with gripping devices, allowing the drum to convey printed sheets while grippers seize, position, or release them during operation. The combination enhances material handling and synchronization within the printing process.
[B41F 21/102] with pneumatic means  B41F21/108  takes precedence -- A sub-class of printing machines and presses that specifies apparatus or features that employ pneumatic means such as air-driven cylinders, actuators or control systems for moving, feeding or adjusting print-related components. It refines the broader category of common details of printing machinery and is superseded by the more specific B41F 21/108.
[B41F 21/104] Gripper details  B41F21/102  takes precedence -- B41F 21/104 in the Cooperative Patent Classification pertains to specific gripper details in printing machinery, with a note that B41F 21/102 takes precedence. This classification is used for patents detailing components of printing machines or presses.
[B41F 21/106] for reversing sheets, e.g. for perfecting machine -- A sub-group of common printing-machine details that deals with mechanisms for turning printed sheets within a press, enabling a perfecting process where a sheet is reversed after one side has been printed so the opposite side can be printed in the same pass.
[B41F 21/108] with pneumatic means -- B41F 21/108 covers printing machines or presses that incorporate pneumatic devices for moving, feeding, or actuating components. It falls under common details of printing machinery, focusing on the use of air pressure to perform operations such as sheet handling, impression, or platen movement.
[B41F 21/12] Adjusting leading edges, e.g. front stops -- The subclass B41F 21/12 covers devices or mechanisms that adjust the leading edge of a printing form or sheet, such as front stops used to set the position before printing, forming part of the common details of printing machines or presses.
[B41F 21/14] Adjusting lateral edges, e.g. side stops -- The B41F 21/14 subclass covers mechanisms that adjust the lateral edges of printed material or printing plates, such as side stops used to control width, alignment and registration in printing machines. It includes devices that can be set or moved laterally to fine-tune the edge position during operation.
[B41F 22/00] Means preventing smudging of machine parts or printed articles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Printing machine elements having specific materials or surfaces,  guiding means using air pressure.
[B41F 22/005] using a back grey cloth -- The CPC subclass B41F 22/005 covers the use of a back-grey cloth in printing machines as a means of preventing smudging of machine parts or printed articles, improving clean operation and print quality.
[B41F 23/00] Devices for treating the surfaces of sheets, webs, or other articles in connection with printing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cleaning devices for sheets or webs or other substrates to be printed upon,  Dampening or heat drying or cooling devices acting before and/or after printing,  Powder applying means, and  Print finishing devices including glossing or varnishing devices.
[B41F 23/002] cleaning devices for sheets or webs -- B41F 23/002 concerns apparatus for cleaning sheets or continuous webs in printing processes. The devices remove dust, ink, or other contaminants from the material's surface to maintain print quality and prevent fouling of subsequent equipment.
[B41F 23/005] of non-flat articles -- The B41F 23/005 subclass concerns devices that treat the surfaces of non-flat articles during printing operations, such as cylinders, rollers, three-dimensional objects or irregularly shaped webs. It includes mechanisms that apply ink, coating, drying or other surface-treating actions to these non-planar items in printing machines or presses.
[B41F 23/007] with heat treatment before printing -- The subclass B41F 23/007 covers printing machines or presses that incorporate a heating step on the sheet, web or other substrate before the actual image-forming operation. It includes devices such as pre-heating rollers, radiant or conductive heaters, and integrated heating units that treat the surface to improve ink adhesion, drying or color development prior to printing. This subclass falls under the broader group for devices treating surfaces of sheets, webs or articles in connection with printing.
[B41F 23/02] by dampening -- The subclass B41F 23/02 covers devices that treat printing sheets, webs or other articles by applying moisture, such as dampening rollers, spray or mist nozzles, and other mechanisms that uniformly wet the surface before ink application to improve ink absorption and print quality.
[B41F 23/04] by heat drying, by cooling, by applying powders -- This subclass pertains to printing machines or presses that treat the surfaces of sheets, webs or other articles by means such as heat drying, cooling, or the application of powders to achieve desired print finishes.
[B41F 23/0403] Drying webs  B41F23/0486  takes precedence -- The code covers apparatus that removes moisture from continuous sheets or webs during printing, such as drying drums, ovens, infrared or hot-air units integrated into presses. It falls under devices for treating the surfaces of sheets, webs, or other articles in connection with printing.
[B41F 23/0406] by radiation  B41F23/0436  takes precedence -- B41F 23/0406 pertains to the treatment of surfaces using radiation in conjunction with printing processes, specifically noting that this code is secondary to B41F 23/0436 when both are applicable.
[B41F 23/0409] Ultraviolet dryers -- B41F 23/0409 - Ultraviolet dryers are devices used in printing to cure inks, coatings or adhesives on sheets, webs or other articles by exposing them to ultraviolet radiation, accelerating drying and improving print quality, and are typically integrated into printing presses for rapid curing.
[B41F 23/0413] Infrared dryers -- Infrared dryers are heating devices that use radiant infrared energy to quickly dry inks, coatings, or adhesives on printed sheets, webs or other substrates during or after printing. By directing infrared radiation onto the printed surface, they evaporate solvents and cure materials without direct contact, improving production speed and energy efficiency.
[B41F 23/0416] by conduction  B41F23/0436  takes precedence -- B41F 23/0416 pertains to methods of treating the surfaces of sheets, webs, or other articles in connection with printing, specifically through conduction. This classification is part of a broader hierarchy that includes performing operations and printing machines or presses. It specifies that when conduction is used for surface treatment, it takes precedence over other methods under B41F23/0436.
[B41F 23/042] using heated rolls -- Heated rolls are employed in printing machines to dry, fuse, or otherwise treat sheet or web surfaces by applying controlled temperature and pressure during the printing process. They may be integrated into press units for fixing inks, drying coatings, or smoothing substrates, improving print quality and production speed.
[B41F 23/0423] by convection  B41F23/0436  takes precedence -- B41F 23/0423 pertains to devices that use convection for treating the surfaces of sheets, webs, or other articles in connection with printing processes. This classification is specific to methods involving heat transfer by air currents, but it is noted that B41F 23/0436 takes precedence when applicable.
[B41F 23/0426] using heated air -- The code B41F 23/0426 covers the use of heated air in devices that treat the surfaces of sheets, webs or other printed articles, such as drying, curing or setting inks and coatings. It specifies methods where a flow of warm air is directed onto the material during or after printing.
[B41F 23/043] using gas or fuel burners -- The B41F 23/043 subclass covers printing-machine or press devices that treat the surfaces of sheets, webs or other articles by applying heat from gas or fuel burners. It is part of the "devices for treating the surfaces of sheets, webs, or other articles in connection with printing" hierarchy within the broader performing operations and printing categories.
[B41F 23/0433] using steam -- This subclass includes methods and apparatus that apply steam to sheets, webs or other printable articles during or after printing to aid drying, ink fixation or surface modification. It covers steam generators, distribution nozzles and control systems integrated with printing machines or presses.
[B41F 23/0436] using a combination of radiation, conduction or convection -- This group covers printing-machine devices that treat the surface of sheets, webs or other articles in connection with printing by simultaneously using radiation, heat conduction and convection. It includes apparatus that combine these methods to dry, cure or otherwise modify printed material during or after printing.
[B41F 23/044] Drying sheets, e.g. between two printing stations  B41F23/0486  takes precedence -- B41F 23/044 covers apparatus or methods for drying printed sheets, such as conveyors or drying zones positioned between two printing stations, to prepare the material for subsequent printing steps. It falls under devices for treating sheet surfaces in printing and is superseded by the more specific sub-class B41F 23/0486.
[B41F 23/0443] after printing -- B41F 23/0443 covers equipment that performs post-printing treatments on sheets, webs or other printed articles, such as drying, curing, cooling, cleaning, coating, or other finishing operations applied after the ink or image has been transferred. It falls under devices for treating surfaces in printing, specifically addressing processes that follow the primary printing step.
[B41F 23/0446] Wicket conveyors -- Wicket conveyors are mechanisms that transport individual sheets or webs through printing machines by holding each item in a separate slot or wicket and advancing them synchronously with the press cycle. They ensure precise positioning and timing of the material as it moves between treatment stations, improving registration and reducing jams. This type of conveyor falls under the CPC subclass for devices treating sheet surfaces in printing.
[B41F 23/045] by radiation -- The B41F 23/045 subclass covers printing-related devices that treat the surface of sheets, webs or other articles using radiation, such as ultraviolet, infrared, laser or electron-beam energy, to cure, dry, harden, fix or otherwise modify the printed material. It applies to equipment integrated in printing machines or presses that employ these radiation processes for surface treatment.
[B41F 23/0453] by ultraviolet dryers -- This subclass defines printing-machine or press devices that employ ultraviolet dryers to cure inks, coatings or other surface treatments on sheets, webs or similar articles during the printing process. It is part of the broader category of equipment for treating surfaces of printed materials.
[B41F 23/0456] by infrared dryers -- B41F 23/0456 covers infrared drying devices employed in printing equipment for treating the surfaces of sheets, webs or other articles. The classification applies to systems that use infrared radiation to cure inks, coatings or other applied materials during the printing process.
[B41F 23/0459] by conduction, e.g. using heated rolls -- B41F 23/0459 covers surface-treatment methods applied to sheets, webs or other items in printing where heat is transferred by conduction, for example using heated rolls. It falls under devices for treating surfaces in printing machines.
[B41F 23/0463] by convection -- This subclass covers printing-related surface-treatment devices that use convection, meaning heat transferred by fluid movement, to dry, cure or otherwise treat inks, coatings or other materials on sheets, webs or articles. Such convection methods are employed in presses to accelerate drying without direct contact.
[B41F 23/0466] by using heated air -- The subclass B41F 23/0466 covers printing or press devices that treat the surface of a sheet, web or other article by directing heated air onto it, such as air-drying, heat-setting, or curing operations performed with hot air streams. This method is used to dry inks, adhesives or coatings, or to fix print images after application.
[B41F 23/0469] by using gas or fuel burners -- This subclass covers devices or processes that treat the surface of printed sheets, webs or similar articles by applying heat generated from gas or fuel burners, such as flame-drying, curing or surface heating steps integrated into printing machines. It includes burners used to remove moisture, set inks or modify substrate properties during or after printing.
[B41F 23/0473] by using steam -- The subclass B41F 23/0473 covers printing-related devices that treat the surfaces of sheets, webs or other articles by applying steam, typically to moisten, smooth or preheat the material before ink transfer. This method is used in various printing machines and presses to improve print quality and adhesion.
[B41F 23/0476] Cooling  B41F23/0483  takes precedence -- B41F 23/0476 pertains to cooling processes for the surfaces of sheets, webs, or other articles in connection with printing, but it is noted that B41F 23/0483 takes precedence when applicable.
[B41F 23/0479] using chill rolls -- B41F 23/0479 covers the use of chill rolls in printing equipment that treat sheet or web surfaces, such as cooling or solidifying inks, coatings, or adhesives applied during the printing process.
[B41F 23/0483] Drying combined with cooling -- A device for treating the surfaces of printed sheets or webs that incorporates a drying stage together with a cooling stage, typically using heated and then chilled air or other media in a single unit integrated into a printing machine or press.
[B41F 23/0486] Particular types of dryers -- B41F 23/0486 relates to specialised dryers employed in printing equipment for removing moisture or solvent from ink, coatings or treated sheets and webs. It includes particular dryer designs such as infrared, hot-air, radiant or contact types that are integrated into printing presses to accelerate drying while the material remains in motion.
[B41F 23/0489] Vacuum dryers -- Vacuum dryers are devices that dry printed sheets, webs or other articles by applying reduced pressure to lower the boiling point of moisture, enabling rapid removal of liquids without high temperatures. They are used in printing operations to prevent damage to inks or substrates while achieving uniform drying.
[B41F 23/0493] Microwave dryers -- B41F 23/0493 covers microwave drying equipment used in printing to remove moisture from sheets, webs, or other printed materials. These devices apply microwave energy to heat and evaporate water, providing rapid and uniform drying without direct contact. They fall under devices for treating the surfaces of sheets in connection with printing.
[B41F 23/0496] Proof dryers -- Proof dryers are devices used in printing operations to rapidly dry printed sheets, proofs or test prints after ink application, ensuring quick handling and preventing smudging. They are typically compact units that circulate heated air or employ infrared radiation to remove moisture from the substrate, facilitating fast turnaround in proofing workflows.
[B41F 23/06] Powdering devices, e.g. for preventing set-off -- B41F 23/06 covers devices that apply powder to printed sheets, webs or other substrates to prevent set-off, the unwanted transfer of ink from one sheet to another. Such powdering may be done by sprinkling, blowing or distributing fine particles onto the surface after printing, and the devices are typically integrated with printing presses or downstream handling equipment.
[B41F 23/065] for thermography -- B41F 23/065 relates to printing equipment that performs thermography, using heat to develop images on the surface of sheets, webs or other articles. The devices apply controlled thermal energy during or after printing to create tonal or raised effects, typically as a finishing step that enhances visual contrast and texture.
[B41F 23/08] Print finishing devices, e.g. for glossing prints -- B41F 23/08 refers to print-finishing devices that apply a final coating or treatment to printed sheets, such as glossing machines that add a protective or decorative layer. These apparatuses work after the printing step to improve the appearance, durability or functional properties of the printed product.
[B41F 25/00] Devices for pressing sheets or webs against cylinders, e.g. for smoothing purposes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for acting mechanically and/or pneumatically onto the sheets or webs.
[B41F 27/00] Devices for attaching printing elements or formes to supports -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Registering printing elements or printing forms to supports,  feeding printing elements or printing formes to forme cylinders or type-beds,  removing printing elements or printing formes from forme cylinders or type-beds,  tensioning devices for flexible printing plates,  flexing devices for flexible printing plates  and devices for filling cylinder gaps and/or removing the filler.
[B41F 27/005] Attaching and registering printing formes to supports -- The subclass B41F 27/005 covers devices that secure printing formes-such as plates, cylinders or other patterned elements-onto the supporting structures of a press and provide means for precise alignment during operation. It includes mechanisms that attach, lock and register the formes so they stay correctly positioned relative to the substrate.
[B41F 27/02] Magnetic devices -- Magnetic devices are used in printing presses to secure printing elements, such as plates or formes, to the machine's supporting structures. They employ magnetic attraction to hold the components in place during operation, enabling quick and repeatable positioning and easy removal for change-overs. This subclass covers the design and application of such magnetic fastening systems in printing equipment.
[B41F 27/04] for attaching printing elements to flat type-beds -- B41F 27/04 designates devices that secure printing elements such as type, plates or forms onto flat type-beds in printing presses, including mechanisms for mounting, clamping or fixing the elements in position.
[B41F 27/06] for attaching printing elements to forme cylinders -- B41F 27/06 covers mechanisms that secure printing plates, types or other printing elements to the surface of a forme cylinder in a press, including clamps, pins, suction devices or adhesive systems, and is intended to ensure precise placement and reliable retention of the elements during printing.
[B41F 27/08] for attaching printing formes to flat type-beds -- This subgroup covers devices that secure printing formes onto flat type-beds during the printing process. It includes mechanisms such as clamps, latches, and holders designed to attach the forme firmly to the bed to ensure accurate ink transfer. The classification is used for printing machines or presses.
[B41F 27/10] for attaching non-deformable curved printing formes to forme cylinders -- B41F 27/10 covers devices that securely fasten rigid, curved printing formes to the surface of a forme cylinder in printing machines. These attachments keep non-deformable formes in place, enabling precise image transfer during rotary printing processes.
[B41F 27/105] for attaching cylindrical printing formes -- B41F 27/105 covers devices that secure cylindrical printing formes to a supporting structure such as a rotary cylinder or mandrel. The classification includes clamping, locking or fastening mechanisms that enable quick replacement or precise positioning of the forme during printing operations.
[B41F 27/12] for attaching flexible printing formes -- B41F 27/12 covers devices used in printing presses to secure flexible printing formes to a supporting surface, enabling accurate placement and removal during the printing process. These mechanisms are designed for attaching and holding flexible plates or reliefs in various types of printing machines.
[B41F 27/1206] Feeding to or removing from the forme cylinder -- Devices that supply material to, or take it away from, the forme cylinder of a printing press, such as mechanisms that load sheets onto the cylinder for impression and remove them after printing. This includes feeders, conveyors, grippers and related control systems that operate with the forme cylinder in printing machines.
[B41F 27/1212] using pneumatic force -- B41F 27/1212 covers devices that attach printing elements or formes to supports by means of pneumatic force. The classification falls under Printing → Printing machines or presses and describes mechanisms that use compressed air to secure components during printing operations.
[B41F 27/1218] comprising printing plate tensioning devices -- B41F 27/1218 covers devices that tension printing plates within printing machines, ensuring the plate is held flat and securely during operation. These mechanisms adjust and maintain plate tension to improve print quality and reduce wear.
[B41F 27/1225] moving in the printing plate end substantially rectilinearly -- The subclass B41F 27/1225 covers mechanisms that move the end of a printing plate or form essentially in a straight line while it is being attached to a support or during operation, providing precise, rectilinear positioning of the plate end within printing machines or presses.
[B41F 27/1231] by translatory motion substantially tangential to support surface -- The code B41F 27/1231 designates mechanisms that attach printing elements or formes to a support by moving them in a straight line that runs essentially parallel to the surface. Such devices use translatory, tangential motion to press, fix or bond the element onto the substrate, commonly found in plate-mounting or screen-printing equipment.
[B41F 27/1237] by translatory motion substantially perpendicular to support surface -- A device for attaching printing elements or formes to a support in which the element moves by a translatory motion that is substantially perpendicular to the support surface, such as a lifting or lowering mechanism that positions plates or type before printing.
[B41F 27/1243] by pivotal or swivelling motion, e.g. by means of a rocking lever -- This CPC subclass covers mechanisms that attach printing elements or formes to a support by a pivoting or swivelling action, for example using a rocking lever. It is part of the printing machines or presses group within performing operations.
[B41F 27/125] moving in the printing plate end on a curvilinear path, e.g. by winding on a roll -- This CPC subclass covers mechanisms that guide the trailing end of a printing plate along a curved trajectory, such as winding the plate onto a roll, within printing machines or presses. The focus is on the motion path for plate handling rather than the printing process itself.
[B41F 27/1256] with two semi-cylinders movable relative to each other -- This CPC subclass relates to printing machines that include a device for attaching printing elements or formes to supports, using two semi-cylindrical components that can move relative to each other. The movable semi-cylinders enable precise positioning, engagement or release of the printing element during the printing operation.
[B41F 27/1262] without tensioning means -- The CPC subclass B41F 27/1262 covers devices in printing machines that attach printing elements or formes to their supports without employing tensioning means, meaning the elements are secured without stretching or pulling mechanisms. It pertains to mechanisms that hold type, plates or similar items in place solely by mechanical positioning, clamping or other non-tension methods.
[B41F 27/1268] by self-locking or snap-on means -- B41F 27/1268 covers devices that attach printing plates, formes or other elements to a support by means that lock themselves or snap onto the support. The mechanisms engage automatically when placed, providing quick, tool-free mounting and release for printing machines.
[B41F 27/1275] by means of adhesives, staples -- This subclass covers devices that fasten printing elements, such as type or plates, to their mounting surfaces by using adhesives or staples, falling under printing machines or presses.
[B41F 27/1281] details of the printing plate ends -- B41F 27/1281 covers the design and treatment of the ends of printing plates, including shaping, reinforcement, anchoring and methods for attaching the plate ends to the machine or supporting structure.
[B41F 27/1287] devices for bending the printing plates or the printing plate ends -- B41F 27/1287 covers devices that bend printing plates or the ends of the plates to obtain the required curvature before or during printing. These mechanisms are employed in printing presses to adjust plate shape for proper registration and pressure control, ensuring uniform ink transfer.
[B41F 27/1293] Devices for filling up the cylinder gap; Devices for removing the filler -- This subclass covers mechanisms mounted on printing cylinders that either supply material into the cylinder gap or extract excess filler, helping to maintain proper ink transfer between cylinder and blanket while preventing leakage. These devices are used in various printing presses to control the filler within the cylinder gap.
[B41F 27/14] for attaching printing formes to intermediate supports, e.g. adapter members -- The classification B41F 27/14 covers devices that attach printing formes to intermediate supports such as adapter members, enabling the mounting of printing elements onto a carrier before printing. It falls under printing machines or presses and addresses mechanisms for securing formes to supports.
[B41F 3/00] Cylinder presses, i.e. presses essentially comprising at least one cylinder co-operating with at least one flat type-bed -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Printing technology mostly used in the 19th century.
[B41F 30/00] Devices for attaching coverings or make-ready devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for attaching sheet-like flexible coverings, usually blankets, and/or cylindrical coverings, usually blankets, and devices for attaching underlays.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Make-ready devices -> B41N6/00
[B41F 30/02] attaching to impression cylinders -- B41F 30/02 covers devices that attach or secure materials to the impression cylinder of a printing press, such as mechanisms for mounting blankets, plates or other coverings during make-ready operations.
[B41F 30/04] attaching to transfer cylinders -- B41F 30/04 covers devices that attach coverings, such as blankets or sleeves, to transfer cylinders in printing presses. The classification includes make-ready and clamping mechanisms that secure the covering to the cylinder surface to ensure proper ink transfer during printing operations.
[B41F 30/06] attaching of endless or like continuously-fed coverings -- B41F 30/06 covers devices that secure endless or continuously fed coverings to printing machines, such as rollers, clamps, suction heads or adhesive systems used in make-ready and covering attachment operations.
[B41F 3/02] with impression cylinder or cylinders rotating unidirectionally -- This subclass covers cylinder-type printing machines in which the impression cylinder or cylinders rotate in only one direction relative to the flat type-bed, providing a unidirectional drive for transferring the inked image onto the substrate.
[B41F 3/04] intermittently -- The B41F 3/04 subclass refers to cylinder presses in which the cylinder operates intermittently, meaning it moves in a stop-start fashion rather than continuously while the flat type-bed remains stationary, enabling printing processes that require periodic feeding or pausing of the sheet between impressions.
[B41F 3/06] continuously -- B41F 3/06 pertains to cylinder printing presses that operate in a continuous mode, feeding material through the press so that printing proceeds without interruptions. It covers presses where the cylinder and flat type-bed cooperate to produce a continuous print output.
[B41F 3/08] Single-revolution presses -- B41F 3/08 covers cylinder printing presses in which the cylinder makes only one complete revolution to execute a printing cycle. The single-revolution motion is used to transfer the image from the type or plate to the substrate in one pass, typically for short-run or high-precision jobs. This subclass falls under printing machines that employ a cylinder working with a flat type-bed.
[B41F 3/10] Two-revolution presses -- Two-revolution presses are a type of cylinder printing press in which the cylinder completes two full rotations for each impression, allowing the sheet to be transferred to the type-bed and back within a single cycle. This configuration increases printing speed while maintaining the pressure uniformity characteristic of cylinder presses.
[B41F 31/00] Inking arrangements or devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Inking arrangements or devices including accessories specifically adapted for an inking arrangement like  driving means,  remote control of duct keys,  heating or cooling means,  recovering printing ink.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Inking units for platen presses -> B41F1/40 - Inking units for cylinder presses -> B41F3/81  **Glossary:** - Ink rail devices: two duct blades cooperating with the cylindrical surface of an inking roller having s screened surface, e.g. an anilox roller - Screen roller or anilox roller: roller having almost its whole cylindrical surface formed by a plurality of evenly distributed small engraved cells all having the same size
[B41F 31/001] Ink mist preventing means -- B41F 31/001 covers devices or methods incorporated in printing machines to prevent the formation or release of ink mist during operation, such as filters, enclosures or airflow systems that capture airborne ink droplets, thereby enhancing safety and print quality.
[B41F 31/002] Heating or cooling of ink or ink rollers -- B41F 31/002 covers methods and apparatus for regulating the temperature of ink or the rollers that carry ink in printing presses. It includes heating elements, cooling systems, temperature control devices and related mechanisms to maintain optimal ink viscosity and printing quality.
[B41F 31/004] Driving means for ink rollers -- B41F 31/004 covers mechanisms that transmit power to ink rollers in printing machines, including shafts, gears, belts or other devices that drive the rollers to ensure proper ink transfer. It relates to the inking arrangements of printing presses and specifies the driving means for the rollers.
[B41F 31/005] Ink viscosity control means -- Ink viscosity control means are devices or mechanisms that adjust the thickness of ink within a printing press to maintain consistent flow and print quality, often using heaters, coolers, mixers or rheological regulators integrated into the inking system.
[B41F 31/007] Removing water from ink trains -- The entry covers techniques and devices for eliminating moisture from the ink train in printing presses, such as drying chambers, filters or absorbent systems that reduce water content to improve ink stability and print quality.
[B41F 31/008] Inking devices for reversible printing presses -- B41F 31/008 covers inking devices designed for reversible printing presses, where the printing cylinder can be turned to print on both sides or multiple colors. The devices ensure consistent ink transfer to the cylinder and may include mechanisms for quick ink replenishment or removal during press reversal.
[B41F 31/02] Ducts, containers, supply or metering devices -- The CPC subclass B41F 31/02 covers ducts, containers, supply or metering devices used in inking arrangements for printing machines. It includes ink ducts, reservoirs, supply lines and mechanisms that regulate the flow or quantity of ink delivered to the print head or press. These components are essential for controlling ink distribution in printing processes.
[B41F 31/022] Ink level control devices -- B41F 31/022 pertains to ink level control devices used in printing machines or presses, focusing on mechanisms that ensure the consistent and precise management of ink supply during the printing process.
[B41F 31/025] Ducts formed between two rollers -- A duct created in the space between two interlocking rollers that transports ink from a supply to the printing surface, typically forming a continuous channel for precise ink delivery in printing presses. This arrangement is part of inking devices used to control ink flow and distribution during the printing process.
[B41F 31/027] Ink rail devices for inking ink rollers -- B41F 31/027 covers devices that hold and guide an ink rail to supply ink to ink rollers in printing presses. The rail is positioned to ensure uniform distribution of ink onto the rollers, often allowing adjustment of pressure or feed.
[B41F 31/03] Ink agitators -- Ink agitators are devices integrated into printing presses that continuously stir or circulate ink to maintain a uniform consistency and prevent pigment settling. They are typically mechanical, pneumatic or hydraulic elements positioned within the inking system, ensuring steady ink flow to the rollers and consistent print quality.
[B41F 31/04] with duct-blades or like metering devices -- The CPC entry B41F 31/04 covers inking arrangements for printing machines that employ duct-blades or comparable metering devices to control and distribute ink onto the printing surface, ensuring uniform application and precise ink flow. This sub-class falls under printing operations, specifically the category of inking devices for presses.
[B41F 31/045] Remote control of the duct keys -- The B41F 31/045 entry covers systems that enable remote operation of duct keys in printing presses, allowing the adjustment of ink flow and distribution without manual access to the keys. Such mechanisms are used to control inking arrangements from a distance, improving efficiency and precision in printing processes.
[B41F 31/05] Positioning devices therefor -- B41F 31/05 covers devices that adjust or locate the inking components of printing presses, ensuring precise alignment of rollers, bars or other ink-applying elements relative to the substrate. These positioning mechanisms may be manual, hydraulic, pneumatic or servo-controlled and are intended to maintain consistent ink transfer during operation.
[B41F 31/06] Troughs or like reservoirs with immersed or partly immersed, rollers or cylinders -- B41F 31/06 covers troughs or similar reservoirs that hold rollers or cylinders partly or wholly immersed in ink within printing-machine inking arrangements. The feature provides a means to supply, contain, or recycle ink directly on the roller surfaces, improving ink delivery and control during the printing process. This subclass falls under inking devices for printing machines.
[B41F 31/07] for rotogravure -- This CPC subclass B41F 31/07 relates to inking arrangements or devices used in rotogravure printing, a rotary gravure process that transfers ink from an engraved cylinder to the substrate. It includes mechanisms for applying, regulating and recovering ink in rotogravure presses.
[B41F 31/08] with ink ejecting means, e.g. pumps, nozzles -- B41F 31/08 covers printing machines or presses that incorporate ink-ejecting means such as pumps or nozzles to deliver ink to the printing surface. It relates to inking arrangements or devices within the broader category of printing operations.
[B41F 31/10] Applications of feed or duct rollers -- B41F 31/10 covers the use of feed or duct rollers in printing presses, where these rollers transport paper or substrate through the machine or guide it into various sections. The classification includes mechanisms that adjust roller speed, pressure or alignment to improve material handling and print quality.
[B41F 31/12] adjustable for regulating supply -- B41F 31/12 covers devices in printing presses that can be adjusted to control the amount of ink delivered to the printing surface. Such regulators modify ink flow or pressure, enabling operators to fine-tune ink supply for consistent print quality across different runs.
[B41F 31/13] Means for driving fountain rollers -- The subclass B41F 31/13 covers mechanisms that power the fountain rollers in printing presses, including motor-driven, gear-driven or other actuation systems that rotate the rollers which distribute ink to the printing plate. It falls under inking arrangements or devices within the broader category of printing machines or presses.
[B41F 31/14] Applications of messenger or other moving transfer rollers -- This subclass covers the use of messenger or other moving transfer rollers that convey ink from a supply unit to the printing plate or blanket in printing machines. It includes designs where the rollers are driven independently to improve ink distribution and handling.
[B41F 31/15] Devices for moving vibrator-rollers -- This subclass covers mechanisms that convey or transport vibrator rollers within printing presses, including cams, levers, gears, or other apparatus that drive the rollers to create oscillating motion for ink transfer. It falls under inking arrangements for printing machines.
[B41F 31/16] Continuous, e.g. endless, band apparatus -- B41F 31/16 covers apparatus that uses a continuous or endless band to convey ink within a printing press. The moving band forms a loop that receives, stores and transfers ink to the printing cylinder, providing a steady, uniform supply and allowing continuous operation without frequent re-inking stops.
[B41F 31/18] for inking selected parts of printing formes -- This subclass relates to devices or arrangements that apply ink only to selected areas of a printing forme, enabling selective inking of parts of the image during operation of printing machines or presses.
[B41F 3/12] Twin presses, i.e. with more than one cylinder or type-bed -- B41F 3/12 relates to twin printing presses that incorporate more than one cylinder or type-bed, allowing two printing units to operate together. These machines are designed to boost productivity or enable simultaneous processing of different print sections within a single press.
[B41F 31/20] Ink-removing or collecting devices -- B41F 31/20 covers devices that remove or collect excess ink from printing presses or printing machines. These devices are used to keep the printing area clean and maintain ink quality, and include mechanisms such as wipers, brushes, or suction systems.
[B41F 31/22] for inking from interior of cylinder -- This subclass covers printing machines that deliver ink to the printing cylinder from inside the cylinder, using internal channels or reservoirs that feed ink directly onto the cylinder surface. Such arrangements help achieve uniform ink distribution and limit external contamination.
[B41F 31/24] Absorbent pads -- Absorbent pads used in printing presses are placed in the inking section to soak up excess ink, control ink distribution, and keep rollers clean. They are typically made of porous or fibrous material and may be replaceable or reusable.
[B41F 31/26] Construction of inking rollers -- The B41F 31/26 subclass covers the design, structure and manufacturing of inking rollers for printing machines, including the selection of core and cover materials, surface treatments, bearing arrangements and methods of attaching the roller to the press. It addresses features that affect ink transfer and roller durability.
[B41F 31/28] Spray apparatus -- The B41F 31/28 subclass defines spray apparatus used in printing presses to apply ink, encompassing spray nozzles, air-atomizers and related mechanisms that deliver a fine mist of ink onto the substrate as part of the inking arrangement of a printing machine.
[B41F 31/30] Arrangements for tripping, lifting, adjusting, or removing inking rollers -- Code B41F 31/30 refers to inking arrangements in printing machines. It specifically covers mechanisms for tripping, lifting, adjusting, or removing inking rollers. This code is used in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[B41F 31/301] Devices for tripping and adjusting form rollers -- It covers mechanisms that initiate (trip) and fine-tune the position or pressure of form rollers in printing presses, ensuring proper ink transfer and sheet handling. These devices may be mechanical, pneumatic or hydraulic and are used to start, stop or adjust roller motion during operation.
[B41F 31/302] Devices for tripping inking devices as a whole -- B41F 31/302 covers mechanisms that activate or release the inking system of a printing press in a single action, allowing the whole inking device to be tripped or engaged together. Such devices are used to start, stop or synchronize ink application during printing operations.
[B41F 31/304] Arrangements for inking roller bearings, forks or supports  B41F31/301  takes precedence -- Arrangements for inking roller bearings, forks or supports describe the design of components that hold, align or guide ink-bearing rollers in printing presses. The code covers bearing housings, forked supports and related structures that ensure accurate roller positioning and reliable ink transfer during operation.
[B41F 31/305] Eccentric bearings -- Eccentric bearings are components in printing-ink delivery systems that convert rotational motion into an offset or reciprocating motion, allowing precise control of ink flow onto rollers. They are mounted off-centre to create a variable radial displacement, facilitating adjustable pressure and timing of inking in presses.
[B41F 31/307] Sliding bearings -- Sliding bearings are low-friction components that support the movable parts of inking devices in printing presses, enabling smooth relative motion between cylinders and rollers. They typically consist of a shaft or sleeve that slides within a housing, reducing wear and maintaining precise alignment during high-speed printing operations.
[B41F 31/308] Swinging bearings -- B41F 31/308 covers swinging bearings, which are bearing assemblies that can pivot or oscillate to accommodate the motion of ink rollers or other rotating components in printing presses. These bearings provide flexible support, allowing the inking mechanism to maintain proper alignment while tolerating positional changes during operation.
[B41F 31/32] Lifting or adjusting devices -- B41F 31/32 covers lifting or adjusting devices used in printing machines or presses, specifically mechanisms that raise, lower, or tilt components such as ink rollers, plates, blankets or other inking elements to achieve precise registration and pressure control during printing.
[B41F 31/34] Cam, eccentric, wedge, or like devices -- This classification covers devices such as cams, eccentrics, wedges and similar mechanisms used to adjust or control ink distribution in printing machines or presses. They are part of inking arrangements, allowing precise timing or pressure changes in the ink transfer process.
[B41F 31/36] fluid-pressure operated -- B41F 31/36 covers inking devices for printing presses that are powered by fluid pressure, typically hydraulic or pneumatic systems, to deliver ink to the form cylinder or printing plate. The classification includes arrangements where the pressure source drives the movement of rollers, pumps, or reservoirs, ensuring consistent ink flow without manual operation. It applies to both offset and other printing processes that use fluid-pressure mechanisms for ink transfer.
[B41F 31/38] magnetically operated -- B41F 31/38 covers inking arrangements for printing machines that are actuated by magnetic forces, such as electromagnetic or permanent-magnet mechanisms that move ink rollers, distributors or applicators. The magnetic actuation may replace mechanical or hydraulic drives to control ink flow or distribution during printing.
[B41F 3/14] with impression cylinder or cylinders rotating in opposite directions during each working cycle -- A subclass of cylinder presses in which the impression cylinder or cylinders turn in the opposite direction to the type-bed during each printing cycle, providing reciprocal motion between the rotating elements.
[B41F 3/16] Twin presses, i.e. with more than one cylinder or type-bed -- Twin presses are printing machines that incorporate two or more cylinders or type-beds, enabling simultaneous operation to increase printing speed or allow multiple colors or formats to be processed at once. They fall under the CPC subclass B41F 3/16, which groups cylinder presses with more than one cylinder or type-bed.
[B41F 3/18] of special construction or for particular purposes -- Cylinder presses that feature a distinctive design or are adapted for specific tasks, such as handling unusual substrates, achieving high precision, or operating under special conditions, fall under this subclass. It covers presses whose construction deviates from standard models to meet particular printing requirements.
[B41F 3/20] with fixed type-beds and travelling impression cylinders -- The subclass B41F 3/20 deals with cylinder presses that have a stationary type-bed while the impression cylinder travels past it, a configuration used in printing machines where the image is transferred from a fixed set of types to a moving impression surface.
[B41F 3/22] with more than one type-bed -- This subclass concerns cylinder printing presses that incorporate more than one flat type-bed, enabling the press to use several interchangeable beds either simultaneously or sequentially for different patterns or colours. It relates to machines where at least one cylinder cooperates with multiple type-beds.
[B41F 3/24] with cylinders travelling around type-beds having upper and lower printing surfaces -- B41F 3/24 covers cylinder presses in which one or more cylinders move around type-beds that provide printing surfaces on both the upper and lower sides of the bed. This subclass falls under printing machines or presses within the performing operations field.
[B41F 3/26] with type-beds and impression cylinders simultaneously movable relative to one another -- This subclass relates to cylinder printing presses in which the flat type-bed and the impression cylinder can be moved simultaneously relative to each other, allowing coordinated adjustment of both elements during operation to improve registration and printing flexibility.
[B41F 3/28] Proof-print presses for relief printing, lithography or intaglio printing, i.e. presses for checking accuracy of printing surfaces -- B41F 3/28 covers cylinder proof-print presses that are used to verify the accuracy of printing surfaces in relief, lithographic or intaglio processes. These machines employ a rotating cylinder to imprint test copies, allowing operators to check registration, tone and detail before full production runs.
[B41F 3/30] for lithography -- B41F 3/30 covers cylinder press technologies used for lithographic printing, where at least one rotating cylinder works together with a flat type-bed to transfer lithographic images onto a substrate. It includes mechanisms for feeding, ink application, and impression control specific to lithography on cylinder presses.
[B41F 33/00] Indicating, counting, warning, control or safety devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Central control units,  programming systems to sequence of machine operations,  means for starting up the machine,  safety devices preventing damage of machine parts including web break detection,  safety devices preventing injury to the operators,  devices for scanning printing formes and/or printed matter for quality control,  measuring the thickness of liquid films,  measuring or controlling the consumption of ink,  measuring contact pressure,  sensors,  counters  and marks, e.g. registering marks, on printed material.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Arrangements of counting devices in platen presses -> B41F1/00 , B41F1/58 - Cylinder presses -> B41F3/84 - Ink supply or metering devices -> B41F31/02  **Glossary:** - Tripping: removing machine parts in/out of operating position into non-operating position
