[B41J 19/34] Escapement-feed character-spacing mechanisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Type-writers only.
[B41J 19/36] Driving mechanisms, e.g. springs stressed during carriage return -- B41J 19/36 covers the driving mechanisms that move a typewriter carriage, such as springs that are tensioned during the carriage-return stroke. These mechanisms provide the force needed to return the carriage to its home position after a line is printed.
[B41J 19/38] adapted for silent return -- A subclass for typewriter mechanisms that automatically return the carriage or paper after a line is typed while minimizing audible noise, using silent-return devices such as spring-loaded or pneumatic systems. It is part of the character- or line-spacing mechanisms in printing typewriters.
[B41J 19/40] Escapements having a single pawl or like detent -- B41J 19/40 refers to escapement mechanisms in typewriters that use a single pawl or comparable detent to control character or line spacing. This falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, printing, typewriters, and character- or line-spacing mechanisms.
[B41J 19/42] Escapements having two pawls or like detents -- B41J 19/42 covers typewriter escapement mechanisms that use two pawls or comparable detents to control character and line spacing during printing. These dual-pawl designs ensure precise advancement of the carriage or print head after each keystroke.
[B41J 19/44] coacting with two toothed members, e.g. racks or wheels -- B41J 19/44 covers mechanisms in typewriters where two toothed elements, such as racks or wheels, engage each other to control character or line spacing. The co-acting toothed members transmit motion precisely, ensuring consistent spacing during printing operations. This sub-class falls under PERFORMING OPERATIONS → PRINTING → TYPEWRITERS → Character- or line-spacing mechanisms.
[B41J 19/46] and mounted on a single rocker -- B41J 19/46 covers typewriter mechanisms that control character or line spacing by using a single rocker to which the spacing components are mounted, allowing coordinated movement of the paper feed or carriage during printing. This arrangement simplifies the spacing system and improves the reliability of spacing adjustments in typewriters.
[B41J 19/48] and mounted on a single slider -- B41J 19/48 refers to character- or line-spacing mechanisms in typewriters where the mechanism is mounted on a single slider, facilitating precise control over spacing during typing.
[B41J 19/50] Electromagnetically-controlled escapements -- B41J 19/50 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to electromagnetically-controlled escapements, which are mechanisms used in typewriters and similar devices for precise character or line spacing. These systems use electromagnetic forces to control the movement of the carriage or print head, ensuring accurate positioning during the printing process.
[B41J 19/52] Escapements controlling positive-feed mechanism -- Escapements that regulate the positive-feed movement in typewriters, ensuring each character advances the paper or typing mechanism by a precise increment after every keystroke, thereby controlling spacing and alignment during printing. This component synchronizes the feed mechanism with the typing action to maintain consistent line formation.
[B41J 19/54] Construction of universal bars -- The CPC subclass B41J 19/54 covers designs and constructions of universal spacing bars employed in typewriters to set character or line spacing. It includes mechanisms that allow a single bar to be adjusted for multiple spacing widths, facilitating consistent printing alignment across different typewriter models. This subclass falls under Performing Operations → Printing → Typewriters → Character- or line-spacing mechanisms.
[B41J 19/56] Escapements controlling web or strip feed -- This subclass covers escapement mechanisms that control the advance of paper or printing strip in typewriters, ensuring precise spacing of characters or lines during printing. It deals with devices that incrementally feed the web or strip, often linked to line justification or character spacing, and forms part of printing operations under typewriter technology.
[B41J 19/58] Differential or variable-spacing arrangements -- This subclass covers mechanisms that adjust the spacing between characters or lines in typewriters, such as proportional or variable-width spacing and automatic feed adjustments. It includes devices that alter the print head or paper movement to produce differential spacing during printing.
[B41J 19/60] Auxiliary feed or adjustment devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Type-writers only.
[B41J 19/62] for back spacing -- Back-spacing mechanisms in typewriters are devices that move the carriage or print head backward after a character is struck, creating additional space before the next character. They enable precise control of character and line spacing for improved layout and correction of typing errors.
[B41J 19/64] for justifying -- B41J 19/64 refers to mechanisms that adjust character or line spacing to produce justified text in typewriters. It falls under the cooperative patent classification for printing operations, specifically the subcategory dealing with typewriter spacing devices.
[B41J 19/66] Carriage-release mechanisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Type-writers only.  **Glossary:** - Carriage: a paper supporting device moving sideward during printing (opposed to a print head carriage of e.g. an ink jet printer)
[B41J 19/68] Carriage-return mechanisms, e.g. manually actuated -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Type-writers only.
[B41J 19/70] power driven -- The B41J 19/70 subclass covers power-driven mechanisms that control the character- or line-spacing functions of typewriters. It includes electrically or pneumatically operated devices that advance the paper or carriage by preset increments without manual actuation, facilitating consistent spacing during printing.
[B41J 19/72] with power stored during character spacing -- B41J 19/72 refers to typewriters that incorporate a power-storage feature for character spacing, using devices such as springs, capacitors or other means to retain energy between keystrokes and release it to move the carriage or print element. It falls under character- or line-spacing mechanisms within the broader printing-typewriter subclass.
[B41J 19/74] with special means to maintain character-spacing or back- spacing elements in engagement during case-shift or like movement -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Type-writers only.
[B41J 19/76] Line-spacing mechanisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Paper movement in horizontal line direction for only type-writers.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Special line-feeds, e.g. long feeds -> B41J11/36
[B41J 19/78] Positive-feed mechanisms -- Positive-feed mechanisms are devices that actively advance paper or a typewriter carriage by a controlled amount per keystroke, ensuring consistent character or line spacing. They typically involve gears, rollers or feed rods that push the medium forward rather than relying on gravity or torsion springs.
[B41J 19/80] Pawl-and-ratchet mechanisms -- B41J 19/80 relates to pawl-and-ratchet mechanisms that control character or line spacing in typewriters. The devices use a spring-loaded pawl engaging a toothed wheel to move the carriage or paper in fixed increments, providing consistent spacing during printing.
[B41J 19/82] moving a paper or like carriage -- The subclass B41J 19/82 covers mechanisms in typewriters that advance the paper or a comparable carriage to provide line or character spacing, typically by moving the paper forward after each typing action. It includes the moving parts and drive systems that control this paper-or-carriage motion.
[B41J 19/84] in the form of a roller rotated for line spacing -- A rotating roller is used to advance the carriage or paper by a set line spacing in typewriters, providing a mechanical means of precise vertical movement during printing. This mechanism replaces traditional fixed pins or cams, allowing smooth, adjustable line feeds for consistent text layout.
[B41J 19/86] the pawl being normally in engagement with the ratchet -- The B41J 19/86 subclass covers typewriter or printing mechanisms in which the pawl is normally engaged with a ratchet to control character or line spacing. It specifies the continuously attached pawl-ratchet arrangement used to advance the paper or carriage in a stepwise fashion. This falls under the broader categories of performing operations, printing, and typewriters.
[B41J 19/88] moving a type carriage -- B41J 19/88 concerns the mechanisms that move the type carriage in typewriters, enabling the carriage to shift horizontally for proper character spacing during printing. The subclass includes drives, linkages and control systems that provide the required carriage motion.
[B41J 19/90] moving a paper or like web or strip, e.g. over a stationary support, automatically in response to movements other than carriage return -- A B41J 19/90 mechanism automatically transports paper, web or strip over a stationary support using motions other than the carriage-return action, such as feed rollers or belts. It is applied in typewriter printing systems where continuous or intermittent paper movement is required without manual carriage movement.
[B41J 19/92] Electromagnetically-operated mechanisms -- Electromagnetically-operated mechanisms are devices that use magnetic fields generated by electric current to control the spacing of characters or lines in typewriters and related printing equipment. They replace or assist purely mechanical spacing systems, enabling faster, more precise adjustment of text layout during printing.
[B41J 19/94] automatically operated in response to carriage return -- The code B41J 19/94 refers to typewriter line-spacing or character-spacing devices that are automatically activated by a carriage-return action, eliminating the need for manual adjustment.
[B41J 19/96] Variable-spacing arrangements -- B41J 19/96 covers mechanisms that allow a typewriter to vary the spacing between characters or lines, such as adjustable pitch controls, proportional spacing devices, or automatic line-spacing systems. These arrangements enable users to change default spacing settings for different fonts or document formats while typing.
[B41J 19/98] Escapement-feed mechanisms -- B41J 19/98 refers to escapement-feed mechanisms used in typewriters, part of the character- or line-spacing subsystem. These mechanisms control the precise movement of typebars or print hammers to ensure consistent spacing between printed characters and lines. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, printing, and typewriters.
[B41J 2/00] Typewriters or selective printing mechanisms characterised by the printing or marking process for which they are designed -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Bringing liquid or particles selectively onto a printing material, either by jetting or through contact. In particular, the sub-groups contain inkjet printers and thermal printers. It covers the structure of the apparatuses used for bringing liquid or particles selectively onto a printing material, their driving/controlling methods and the methods to manufacture them.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Mounting, arrangement, or disposition of types or dies -> B41J1/00
[B41J 2002/0052] Control methods or devices for non ink jet heads -- B41J 2002/0052 covers control methods and devices for spray or printing heads that are not based on ink-jet technology. It includes mechanisms for regulating fluid flow, timing, pressure or positioning of non-ink-jet heads used in printing, coating or dispensing applications.
[B41J 2002/0055] Heating elements adjacent to nozzle orifices of printhead for warming up ink meniscuses, e.g. for lowering the surface tension of the ink meniscuses -- This subclass covers printheads that incorporate heating elements positioned next to the nozzle orifices to warm the ink meniscus, thereby reducing its surface tension and improving droplet formation. The heating can be used to control viscosity and promote reliable jetting during printing.
[B41J 2002/012] with intermediate transfer member -- The CPC subclass B41J 2002/012 covers printing processes that employ an intermediate transfer member, such as a belt, drum or roller, to receive the ink or toner from the primary image carrier before transferring it onto the final substrate. It includes apparatus and methods where the image is first deposited on this intermediate element and then subsequently transferred to the target material.
[B41J 2002/022] Control methods or devices for continuous ink jet -- B41J 2002/022 covers technologies for controlling continuous-ink-jet printing, including methods and devices that regulate ink flow, droplet formation, trajectory, and charge to maintain stable, high-quality printing. It addresses feedback systems, sensors, and actuators used to adjust parameters in real time.
[B41J 2002/031] Gas flow deflection -- The CPC subclass B41J 2002/031 covers inventions that redirect or modify the path of a moving gas stream. It includes aerodynamic surfaces, vanes, diffusers, deflectors and related control devices used in turbines, compressors, exhaust systems and other fluid-handling equipment to change direction, velocity or pressure of the gas flow.
[B41J 2002/032] Deflection by heater around the nozzle -- The CPC subclass B41J 2002/032 relates to spray or atomising devices in which a heating element is positioned around the nozzle to modify the direction of the fluid jet. The heater creates thermal expansion or gas generation that causes deflection, enabling control of spray pattern, droplet size or flow direction, particularly in applications such as coating, drying or cooling.
[B41J 2002/033] Continuous stream with droplets of different sizes -- The CPC subclass B41J 2002/033 covers processes that generate a continuous jet or stream in which the emitted droplets are intentionally varied in size, rather than being uniform. It includes methods and devices for producing such heterogeneous droplet streams, typically used in applications like multi-material printing, spray coating, or controlled atomisation where different droplet dimensions serve distinct functional purposes.
[B41J 2002/041] Electromagnetic transducer -- B41J2002/041 refers to electromagnetic transducers, devices that convert electrical energy into mechanical motion by means of magnetic fields. It covers components such as speakers, actuators and other apparatus that produce sound, vibrations or linear movement using electromagnetic principles.
[B41J 2002/043] Electrostatic transducer -- Electrostatic transducers are devices that convert electrical energy into mechanical motion or vice versa by exploiting electrostatic forces between charged elements. The classification covers actuators, micromirrors, variable capacitors and similar components that operate on the principle of attraction or repulsion of electrically charged surfaces. It includes both stand-alone units and integrated parts used in optical, acoustic or precision-positioning applications.
[B41J 2002/061] Ejection by electric field of ink or of toner particles contained in ink -- B41J 2002/061 relates to the ejection of ink or toner particles from a printing device by means of an electric field. It includes methods and apparatus where voltage is applied to move charged particles from a supply chamber onto a recording surface, as used in electrophotographic and ink-jet printers.
[B41J 2002/062] by using a divided counter electrode opposite to ejection openings of an electrostatic printhead, e.g. for controlling the flying direction of ejected toner particles by providing the divided parts of the counter electrode with different potentials -- The technique places a counter electrode split into separate sections directly opposite the ejection openings of an electrostatic printhead, with each section biased at a different voltage to influence the trajectory of the ejected toner particles.
[B41J 2002/063] Moving solid toner particles in carrier liquid by eletrostatic force acting on the toner particles, e.g. for accumulating the toner particles around an ejection electrode of an electrostatic printhead -- B41J 2002/063 covers techniques for transporting solid toner particles dispersed in a carrier liquid by applying electrostatic forces, for example to gather the particles around an ejection electrode of an electrostatic printhead.
[B41J 2002/14169] Bubble vented to the ambience -- B41J 2002/14169 covers a bubble-venting feature that discharges gas bubbles from a melt or blown film to the surrounding atmosphere. The vent improves surface quality by allowing entrapped air to escape during extrusion or blowing processes, and may include openings, slots or perforated elements integrated in the die or bubble-forming zone.
[B41J 2002/14177] Segmented heater -- B41J 2002/14177 covers segmented heaters, which are heating elements divided into two or more independent zones that can be controlled separately. The classification applies to devices used in industrial processes such as drying, curing, or molding where temperature gradients or localized heating are required.
[B41J 2002/14185] characterised by the position of the heater and the nozzle -- B41J 2002/14185 designates a process subgroup in which the defining feature is the relative position of the heater and the nozzle, typically used in thermal spray or coating operations where heating of the material and its delivery through a nozzle are coordinated. The placement of these components is specified to control temperature gradients and deposition characteristics during the manufacture of coated articles.
[B41J 2002/14193] movable member in the ink chamber -- The CPC subclass B41J 2002/14193 covers printing apparatus features that include a movable component inside the ink chamber, such as pistons, diaphragms, or other actuated elements that adjust ink volume, pressure, or flow during operation. It is used to classify devices where internal motion controls ink delivery to the printhead.
[B41J 2002/14217] Multi layer finger type piezoelectric element -- The CPC code B41J 2002/14217 covers multilayer finger-type piezoelectric elements, which consist of a stack of thin piezoelectric plates arranged as parallel fingers. These components function as actuators or sensors in ultrasonic transducers, receivers and other acoustic devices, providing high displacement and precise resonance control.
[B41J 2002/14225] Finger type piezoelectric element on only one side of the chamber -- B41J 2002/14225 covers devices in which a finger-shaped piezoelectric element is mounted on a single side of a fluid-filled chamber to generate or receive acoustic waves. The configuration is used in ultrasonic transducers where the element contacts only one wall, allowing efficient vibration coupling while keeping the opposite side free. This subclass falls under the broader group dealing with piezoelectric converters for ultrasonic applications.
[B41J 2002/14241] having a cover around the piezoelectric thin film element -- This subclass covers devices where the piezoelectric thin-film element is enclosed in a protective cover, which may be a housing, membrane or sealing layer that isolates the element from environmental influences while allowing acoustic transmission. The cover often serves to protect, support or focus the acoustic wave in ultrasonic transducers and similar components.
[B41J 2002/1425] Embedded thin film piezoelectric element -- The code B41J 2002/1425 refers to thin-film piezoelectric elements that are incorporated directly into a device or structure, such as sensors, actuators, MEMS or integrated circuits. It covers the materials, fabrication methods and functional integration of these embedded piezoelectric layers for converting electrical signals to mechanical motion or vice-versa.
[B41J 2002/14258] Multi layer thin film type piezoelectric element -- B41J 2002/14258 pertains to the production of multilayer thin-film piezoelectric elements, in which a series of thin piezoelectric layers are alternated with electrode layers to create compact devices used in sensors, actuators or resonators.
[B41J 2002/14266] Sheet-like thin film type piezoelectric element -- A sheet-like thin-film piezoelectric element is a flexible device in which a piezoelectric material is deposited as a thin layer on a supporting substrate, allowing the film to bend or deform under an applied electric field. Such elements are used especially in ink-jet print heads and other micro-actuation systems where compact, low-profile actuation or sensing is required.
[B41J 2002/14306] Flow passage between manifold and chamber -- The code B41J 2002/14306 classifies the internal fluid-conducting features that link a distribution manifold to a chamber in spray, atomising or mixing devices, covering the shape, geometry and arrangement of the passage or duct that carries the working medium between these two components.
[B41J 2002/14322] Print head without nozzle -- B41J 2002/14322 designates technology for a printing apparatus whose print head lacks a traditional nozzle, employing alternative means such as porous layers, electro-static or acoustic ejection, or other nozzle-free ink-delivery methods to form images on a substrate.
[B41J 2002/14338] Multiple pressure elements per ink chamber -- The code defines ink-jet printing heads in which a single ink chamber houses two or more pressure-generating elements that act on the same fluid volume, allowing varied droplet size or firing sequences. It includes arrangements such as multiple piezoelectric crystals or thermal resistors coupled to one chamber.
[B41J 2002/14346] Ejection by pressure produced by thermal deformation of ink chamber, e.g. buckling -- B41J 2002/14346 covers ink-jet printheads that generate ejection pressure by heating the ink reservoir so that the chamber deforms, often by buckling, to force ink out of the nozzle. The method relies on thermal expansion or phase change to create sufficient force without mechanical actuators.
[B41J 2002/14354] Sensor in each pressure chamber -- The CPC entry B41J 2002/14354 concerns the integration of a sensor into each pressure chamber of a pressure-raising device or inflatable structure. It covers designs in which separate chambers each contain a pressure-sensing element to monitor internal conditions such as pressure or temperature, enabling real-time control and safety functions.
[B41J 2002/14362] Assembling elements of heads -- B41J 2002/14362 covers the processes and apparatus used to assemble the constituent parts of a firearm's head or breech, including the fitting, joining and securing of components such as the breech block, firing pin, extractor and related mechanisms. The entry addresses both manual and automated assembly steps that create the functional head assembly for rifles, pistols and similar weapons.
[B41J 2002/1437] Back shooter -- B41J 2002/1437 - Back shooter designates an apparatus that projects a jet of gas, vapor or coating material from a position behind the workpiece so that the stream strikes the front surface for uniform treatment. The classification covers devices where the nozzle or discharge source is mounted rearward of the target and may include mechanisms for moving the nozzle or substrate to achieve even coverage.
[B41J 2002/14379] Edge shooter -- The entry pertains to printing equipment that applies ink, toner or other marking material to the edge of a substrate. It includes devices that generate, transport or direct a stream of droplets or particles to the perimeter of a sheet for edge marking, trimming or finishing operations.
[B41J 2002/14387] Front shooter -- B41J 2002/14387 - Front shooter covers the structural and functional features of weapons in which the projectile is launched from a forward-mounted barrel or firing mechanism, including front-loaded firearms, cannons and launchers and the related means for supporting, aiming or feeding the ammunition. It addresses the geometry, mounting and operation of the front-firing arrangement.
[B41J 2002/14395] Electrowetting -- The B41J 2002/14395 classification covers technologies that manipulate liquids by applying an electric field to change their wettability on a surface, known as electrowetting. It includes devices such as micro-fluidic chips, variable-focus lenses, and display elements that rely on electrically controlled fluid movement.
[B41J 2002/14403] including a filter -- B41J 2002/14403 covers spray or atomizing printing devices that incorporate a filter to remove particulates or contaminants from the fluid being applied. The filter is integrated into the delivery system to ensure a clean, uniform coating or ink layer during the printing process.
[B41J 2002/14411] Groove in the nozzle plate -- The CPC group B41J 2002/14411 refers to nozzle plates that incorporate a groove to control the flow of fluid, typically in spray or dispensing devices used for applying liquids such as inks, paints, adhesives or cleaning agents onto a surface. It covers the geometry and function of the grooved passage within the plate.
[B41J 2002/14419] Manifold -- B41J 2002/14419 covers manifolds used in printing equipment to channel or distribute inks, toners, solvents or other fluids to one or more printheads or nozzles, including designs for pressure regulation, mixing or filtering. The classification is applied to apparatus where the manifold forms an integral part of the ink supply system in printers, plotters or similar devices.
[B41J 2002/14435] Moving nozzle made of thermal bend detached actuator -- The subclass B41J 2002/14435 relates to moving nozzles that are actuated by a detached thermal-bend element, where heating causes the actuator to bend and shift the nozzle relative to the feed system. It covers designs used in spray, printing or dispensing applications that employ such thermally driven motion.
[B41J 2002/14443] Nozzle guard -- B41J 2002/14443 covers nozzle guards, which are protective devices fitted around the discharge openings of ink-jet or other printing nozzles to shield them from foreign particles, mechanical impact or drying of the fluid. The guard may be removable, adjustable, or integrated into the printhead housing and is used to maintain print quality and extend nozzle life.
[B41J 2002/14459] Matrix arrangement of the pressure chambers -- B41J 2002/14459 covers inventions that organize pressure chambers in a regular matrix pattern to apply uniform or selectively controlled pressure across a flexible sheet or workpiece, typically in forming, molding, or conveyance equipment. The subclass falls within the CPC group for devices that regulate suction and pressure in sheet-forming processes.
[B41J 2002/14467] Multiple feed channels per ink chamber -- B41J 2002/14467 pertains to ink-jet printing heads that provide more than one feed channel for each ink chamber. The multiple channels allow separate delivery paths for ink, facilitating precise control of droplet formation, color mixing or multi-material deposition. This feature improves printing accuracy and can support complex printing applications.
[B41J 2002/14475] characterised by nozzle shapes or number of orifices per chamber -- B41J 2002/14475 is a cooperative patent classification subgroup that groups inventions involving spray or atomising devices distinguished by the shape of their nozzles or by the number of orifices in each chamber. It is applied to patents for spray guns, misting heads and similar apparatus where nozzle geometry or orifice count defines the functional characteristics.
[B41J 2002/14483] Separated pressure chamber -- B41J 2002/14483 covers printing devices in which a pressure chamber is isolated from the printing surface, allowing the application of a controlled force without direct contact with the inked or coated area. The separation protects the workpiece, improves uniformity of pressure, and is typical of offset or flexographic presses that employ a sealed chamber to transmit force to the plate or web. This feature is classified under the broader B41J group for printing apparatus.
[B41J 2002/14491] Electrical connection -- B41J relates to printing and writing implements, and the 2002 sub-group covers apparatuses for feeding, transporting or handling printed material. Code 14491 specifies features concerning the electrical connection of such devices, including power and signal wiring, connectors and coupling means that link the printing mechanism to its control or drive electronics. This entry classifies inventions that focus on how the apparatus is electrically linked to its functional components.
[B41J 2002/1655] with wiping surface parallel with nozzle plate and mounted on reels, e.g. cleaning ribbon cassettes -- This subclass covers cleaning devices in which a wiping surface is positioned parallel to a nozzle plate and is wound on reels, exemplified by replaceable cleaning-ribbon cassettes used in printing equipment.
[B41J 2002/16555] Air or gas for cleaning -- This CPC subclass covers methods and apparatuses that use a stream of air or other gas to remove contaminants from surfaces, parts or equipment. It includes jet cleaning, pneumatic blowing and gas-assisted dust removal for industrial or domestic applications.
[B41J 2002/16558] Using cleaning liquid for wet wiping -- B41J 2002/16558 covers methods and apparatuses that employ a cleaning liquid together with a wet-wiping action to remove contaminants from a surface. It includes the application of the liquid, contact with the material to be cleaned, and subsequent removal by a cloth, pad or similar wiping medium, typically for industrial or household cleaning processes.
[B41J 2002/16561] by an electrical field -- B41J 2002/16561 covers printing processes that employ an electric field to move or deposit a liquid material onto a substrate, such as electrostatic or electrophoretic ink-jet methods. The classification is used for inventions where the electrical field is the primary means of directing the printing medium.
[B41J 2002/16564] Heating means therefor, e.g. for hot melt inks -- The CPC subclass B41J 2002/16564 covers heating devices that are integral to printing or marking systems and are used to melt or maintain the temperature of hot-melt inks. It includes any form of heater, temperature-control element or thermal arrangement specifically designed to keep the ink in a liquid state for application during printing. This subclass is applied to technologies such as thermal inkjet, solid-ink, and other hot-melt ink printers.
[B41J 2002/16567] using ultrasonic or vibrating means -- The subclass B41J 2002/16567 covers processes in which ultrasonic or other high-frequency vibrating energy is applied to flexible sheet materials such as textiles, films, or similar substrates for cleaning, conditioning, embossing, surface modification or inspection. It includes apparatus and methods that generate and direct these vibrations to achieve the desired material treatment.
[B41J 2002/1657] Cleaning of only nozzles or print head parts being selected -- B41J 2002/1657 covers cleaning operations that are applied only to selected nozzles or specific parts of a print head, rather than to the entire print head assembly. It includes methods for targeted removal of debris, ink residue or blockages from individual spray openings to maintain printing performance. This subclass is used for inventions that focus on localized nozzle or print-head part cleaning.
[B41J 2002/16573] Cleaning process logic, e.g. for determining type or order of cleaning processes -- The code pertains to logical or decision-making aspects of cleaning operations in printing, coating or related industries, including algorithms, control systems and software that select the appropriate cleaning type or sequence based on material, equipment condition or product requirements. It covers methods for determining when and how cleaning steps are applied and the use of sensor data to optimise the order of cleaning processes.
[B41J 2002/16576] Cleaning means pushed or actuated by print head movement -- The subclass covers cleaning devices for printing apparatus that are displaced or triggered by the movement of the print head, such as brushes, wipers or other means that travel with the head to remove ink or residue. It includes mechanisms where the cleaning action is directly actuated by the head's motion rather than by separate drive systems.
[B41J 2002/16582] Maintenance means fixed on the print head or its carriage -- B41J 2002/16582 relates to maintenance devices that are permanently attached to a print head or its carriage, such as wipers, scrubbers or other cleaning elements that keep the head free of ink, debris or paper residue during operation.
[B41J 2002/16591] for line print heads above an endless belt -- The CPC subclass B41J 2002/16591 covers printers in which a linewise print head is mounted above an endless moving belt that serves as the printing medium. The belt receives the ink or toner from the head and transports the printed line onward, enabling continuous high-speed printing.
[B41J 2002/16594] Pumps or valves for cleaning -- B41J 2002/16594 classifies pumps and valves that are specifically designed for cleaning operations, covering devices that transfer cleaning fluids, remove waste or control the flow of liquids during a cleaning process. The entry includes mechanical arrangements, control mechanisms and associated accessories used in industrial or domestic cleaning equipment.
[B41J 2002/16597] Pumps for idle discharge of liquid through nozzles -- B41J 2002/16597 covers pumps that supply an idle or low-flow discharge of liquid through a nozzle, used to keep the nozzle primed, clean it, or provide a standby spray in firefighting, agricultural spraying and similar systems. The entry includes devices that deliver a continuous trickle or spray when the main pump is not operating.
[B41J 2002/17516] comprising a collapsible ink holder, e.g. a flexible bag -- This subclass of B41J covers pens, pencils or similar writing instruments that incorporate a collapsible ink holder, such as a flexible bag, enabling the ink reservoir to be compressed or folded when not in use. It relates to designs where the ink container can change shape to accommodate varying ink volumes or to improve portability.
[B41J 2002/17569] based on the amount printed or to be printed -- The CPC entry B41J 2002/17569 refers to printing apparatus or methods whose operation is controlled according to the quantity of material to be printed, such as devices that adjust feed, speed, or layout based on the amount of output required for a given job. It encompasses systems that determine printing parameters from the intended print run size or the actual amount already printed.
[B41J 2002/17573] using optical means for ink level indication -- This subclass covers devices that detect, monitor or show the remaining amount of ink in cartridges or containers by using optical sensors such as photodetectors, infrared or laser beams, or visual inspection. It includes systems that rely on light transmission, reflection, scattering or colour changes to determine ink level.
[B41J 2002/17576] using a floater for ink level indication -- The subclass covers devices that employ a floating element inside an ink reservoir to provide a visual indication of the remaining ink. The floater moves with the ink level and actuates a display, pointer or other signal to show how much ink is left, commonly used in printers, pens or marking implements.
[B41J 2002/17579] Measuring electrical impedance for ink level indication -- The B41J 2002/17579 entry covers methods and apparatuses that determine ink quantity by monitoring the electrical impedance of the ink reservoir or delivery path. It includes sensors that detect impedance variations caused by changes in ink volume, enabling real-time level indication in printing or plotter devices.
[B41J 2002/17583] using vibration or ultra-sons for ink level indication -- This subgroup pertains to devices that detect the remaining amount of ink in a printer or similar by measuring vibrations or ultrasonic signals generated by the ink reservoir or flow. It covers methods and apparatus that monitor ink levels through acoustic or vibratory detection, typically integrated into printing heads or cartridges.
[B41J 2002/17586] using ink bag deformation for ink level indication -- B41J 2002/17586 covers ink-jet printing devices that determine remaining ink by observing the deformation of an ink-bag reservoir. The method monitors changes in the bag's shape or volume as ink is consumed, providing an indication of ink level for automatic refill or low-ink warnings. This allows printers to assess ink status without direct measurement of the liquid.
[B41J 2002/17589] using ink level as input for printer mode selection or for prediction of remaining printing capacity -- The CPC subclass B41J 2002/17589 relates to printer systems that monitor the amount of ink or toner and use that level as an input to automatically select a printing mode or to predict how much printing capacity remains, enabling adaptive quality or resource-saving choices.
[B41J 2002/1853] ink collectors for continuous Inkjet printers, e.g. gutters, mist suction means -- B41J 2002/1853 pertains to ink collection components for continuous-inkjet printers, including gutters and mist-suction devices that gather and remove surplus ink droplets. These elements help maintain print quality and prevent ink waste by directing stray ink away from the print path.
[B41J 2002/1856] waste ink containers -- B41J 2002/1856 pertains to containers intended for the collection, storage or disposal of waste ink generated by printing apparatus. It includes disposable or refillable receptacles, cartridges or tanks that separate used ink from fresh supply, facilitating ink recycling or safe handling.
[B41J 2002/2058] selecting different ink densities from one colour -- The CPC subclass B41J 2002/2058 covers printing techniques that obtain a range of visual densities from a single colour by varying the amount or thickness of ink applied. It includes methods such as adjusting ink feed, printing speed, pressure or exposure to create light, medium and dark tones of the same hue without switching inks. This is used in processes like digital, offset and screen printing to produce gradations or shading from one colour source.
[B41J 2002/453] self-scanning -- B41J 2002/453 relates to self-scanning devices used in image formation or copying, where the machine incorporates a sensor that moves across the original to capture its image without external feeder. It includes mechanisms that combine scanning and processing within a single apparatus, typical in multifunction printers and digital copiers.
[B41J 2002/4756] Erasing by radiation -- B41J 2002/4756 covers methods that erase or remove a pattern or material from a substrate by means of radiation such as light, laser, electron or ion beams, or plasma. The process typically modifies a photosensitive or radiation-sensitive layer so that the exposed areas can be developed or otherwise eliminated, as in lithography or micro-fabrication.
[B41J 2/005] characterised by bringing liquid or particles selectively into contact with a printing material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ink reaches paper without forming a flying drop  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Printing by selective application of impact or pressure on a printing or impression-transfer material -> B41J2/22
[B41J 2/0057] where an intermediate transfer member receives the ink before transferring it on the printing material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[B41J 2/01] Ink jet -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Inkjet printers in general, when no details on structure is provided.
[B41J 2/015] characterised by the jet generation process -- The B41J 2/015 entry covers typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that create characters by a jet generation process, such as ink-jet, pneumatic or similar jet-based systems. It applies to devices where a stream of fluid or particles is formed and directed onto the medium to produce the printed mark.
[B41J 2/02] generating a continuous ink jet -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Heads generating a continuous stream of droplets, and selecting some to be printed while others are collected back into the ink supply system.
[B41J 2/025] by vibration -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[B41J 2025/008] comprising a plurality of print heads placed around a drum -- B41J 2025/008 covers printing apparatus that uses several print heads positioned around a cylindrical drum, allowing simultaneous or sequential deposition of ink as the drum rotates. This arrangement is typical for high-speed or multi-colour printers where the circumferential placement of heads improves coverage and printing efficiency.
[B41J 2029/3932] Battery or power source mounted on the carriage -- This CPC subclass covers devices in which a battery or other power source is mounted on the carriage of a printing, recording or imaging apparatus, allowing the carriage to be powered independently of a fixed supply. It includes arrangements that provide mobile or portable operation by supplying energy directly to the moving carriage.
[B41J 2029/3935] by means of printed test patterns -- B41J 2029/3935 relates to the use of printed test patterns for evaluating and controlling printing processes, including colour reproduction, registration, dot gain and other quality parameters. The method involves generating reference patterns on the substrate and measuring their appearance to adjust machine settings.
[B41J 2029/3937] Wireless communication between the printer and the cartridge, carriage or printhead -- The subclass B41J 2029/3937 pertains to printers that use wireless links-such as infrared, radio-frequency, Bluetooth, NFC or similar technologies-to exchange data with cartridges, carriages or printheads for purposes like status monitoring, ink-level reporting, configuration or firmware updates, eliminating the need for direct physical connections.
[B41J 2/03] by pressure -- B41J 2/03 covers typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that produce marks by applying pressure, such as impact printers that strike a ribbon or directly emboss the paper. The classification includes any device where the character is formed through mechanical force rather than ink-jet or electrostatic methods.
[B41J 2/035] by electric or magnetic field -- B41J 2/035 covers typewriters or other selective printing devices whose printing or marking operation is driven by an electric or magnetic field, such as electro-static or electromagnetic actuation of type elements, rather than purely mechanical force. This sub-group focuses on field-controlled imprinting mechanisms within the broader printing and typewriter categories.
[B41J 2/04] generating single droplets or particles on demand -- **Definition:** This place covers:  On-demand inkjet heads (every formed ink droplet will be printed).
[B41J 2/045] by pressure, e.g. electromechanical transducers -- B41J 2/045 covers typewriters or selective printing devices that create characters by applying pressure, typically using electromechanical transducers such as impact hammers or piezoelectric actuators. The classification falls within the CPC hierarchy of Performing Operations - Printing - Typewriters and defines the pressure-based printing method.
[B41J 2/04501] Control methods or devices therefor, e.g. driver circuits, control circuits -- B41J 2/04501 covers control methods and devices such as driver or control circuits for typewriters and other selective printing mechanisms, focusing on how the printing or marking process is managed. It falls under the performing operations, printing, typewriters hierarchy.
[B41J 2/04503] aiming at compensating carriage speed -- This subgroup concerns typewriter and selective printing devices that include mechanisms to offset or adjust for the speed of the carriage during operation, ensuring consistent character registration and print quality. It falls under printing typewriters within performing operations.
[B41J 2/04505] aiming at correcting alignment -- B41J 2/04505 is a CPC sub-class in the Performing Operations → Printing → Typewriters group, covering typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that are specifically designed to correct alignment during the printing or marking process.
[B41J 2/04506] aiming at correcting manufacturing tolerances -- This subclass covers typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that incorporate adjustments or aiming methods intended to compensate for manufacturing tolerances, helping maintain consistent print placement and quality. It includes designs that correct dimensional variations introduced during production.
[B41J 2/04508] aiming at correcting other parameters -- The subgroup B41J 2/04508 covers typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that incorporate means for adjusting or compensating other printing parameters, such as pressure, alignment or speed, after the primary marking operation. It refers to features aimed at correcting these ancillary variables to improve print quality or consistency.
[B41J 2/0451] for detecting failure, e.g. clogging, malfunctioning actuator -- The B41J 2/0451 subclass covers typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that incorporate means for detecting failures such as clogging of the printing element or a malfunctioning actuator. It relates to diagnostic features that monitor the printing or marking process and alert the user or trigger corrective action when a fault is identified.
[B41J 2/04511] for electrostatic discharge protection -- B41J 2/04511 covers typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that incorporate measures to protect the printing or marking process from electrostatic discharge. The feature ensures reliable operation by preventing damage or erroneous marks caused by static electricity.
[B41J 2/04513] for increasing lifetime -- B41J 2/04513 pertains to typewriter and selective printing mechanism designs aimed at enhancing the durability and longevity of the printing or marking process. This classification focuses on specific techniques or features that contribute to a longer operational life of the device.
[B41J 2/04515] preventing overheating -- B41J 2/04515 covers measures incorporated in typewriters or selective printing mechanisms to avoid overheating during operation, such as thermal sensors, cooling fans, heat-dissipating materials or control circuits that limit power. The subclass focuses on design features that protect the printing process from temperature-related failures.
[B41J 2/04516] preventing formation of satellite drops -- The subgroup B41J 2/04516 covers features of typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that prevent the creation of satellite drops during ink or toner ejection, thereby improving print precision and reducing unintended marks. It includes devices, coatings or control methods aimed at stabilising the primary drop.
[B41J 2/04518] reducing costs -- The subclass B41J 2/04518 addresses ways to lower the manufacturing, operating or maintenance expenses of typewriters and other selective printing mechanisms, covering design simplifications, cheaper materials, energy-saving features and other cost-reduction measures applied to the printing or marking process for which the devices are intended.
[B41J 2/0452] reducing demand in current or voltage -- This subclass relates to typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that incorporate features to lower the electrical current or voltage required for operation, such as energy-efficient drive systems, low-power actuation methods or power-management circuitry designed to reduce overall power consumption during printing.
[B41J 2/04521] reducing number of signal lines needed -- The subclass B41J 2/04521 covers typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that use design features to reduce the number of signal lines required for controlling printing elements, simplifying wiring and improving reliability. It focuses on methods that minimise the electrical connections needed to activate typebars, matrix heads or similar devices during the printing process.
[B41J 2/04523] reducing size of the apparatus -- The subclass B41J 2/04523 covers typewriters or other selective printing mechanisms whose design is altered to make the apparatus smaller. It includes structural or functional changes that reduce overall dimensions while preserving the intended printing or marking function. This falls under the broader categories of performing operations, printing, and typewriters.
[B41J 2/04525] reducing occurrence of cross talk -- The CPC subclass B41J 2/04525 refers to typewriter or selective printing mechanisms that incorporate features to minimise cross talk-unwanted interference between adjacent print elements-during the printing or marking process. It covers designs, materials or control methods that reduce stray signals or mechanical coupling that cause characters to blur or overlap.
[B41J 2/04526] controlling trajectory -- B41J 2/04526 covers typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that include means for controlling the trajectory of the type element or print head during operation. It pertains to systems that direct the path of impact to achieve accurate character placement.
[B41J 2/04528] aiming at warming up the head -- The entry B41J 2/04528 covers typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that incorporate a means to preheat the print head before use, a feature intended to improve ink flow or reduce start-up lag. It is located in the hierarchy under Performing Operations → Printing → Typewriters and specifically addresses the warming-up function of the head.
[B41J 2/0453] controlling a head having a dummy chamber -- The subclass covers methods and devices for controlling a printing head that incorporates a dummy chamber, used in typewriters or selective printing mechanisms to manage ink flow, impact timing or other printing characteristics.
[B41J 2/04531] controlling a head having a heater in the manifold -- The entry covers methods and devices for regulating a printing head that incorporates a heater within its manifold, as used in typewriters or selective printing mechanisms. It includes control circuits, temperature sensors or other means that adjust heating to maintain proper ink or ribbon flow during the marking process.
[B41J 2/04533] controlling a head having several actuators per chamber -- B41J 2/04533 falls under the CPC subgroup for typewriters or selective printing mechanisms, specifically those designed for the printing or marking process. It covers the control of a printing head that contains multiple actuators within each chamber, enabling precise actuation of individual printing elements.
[B41J 2/04535] involving calculation of drop size, weight or volume -- The B41J 2/04535 subclass covers typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that integrate calculations of drop size, weight or volume as part of their printing or marking process, using these measurements to control ink or pigment deposition. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, printing and typewriters.
[B41J 2/04536] using history data -- The entry covers typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that incorporate stored history data to influence the printing process, such as recalling previously typed characters, employing predictive text, or adjusting stroke formation based on past inputs. It relates to devices where memory of prior actions guides current marking or character generation.
[B41J 2/04538] involving calculation of heater resistance -- B41J 2/04538 covers typewriters or selective printing mechanisms in which the resistance of the heating element is calculated, typically to control temperature for consistent marking. The subclass deals with methods and circuits that determine heater resistance for reliable print quality.
[B41J 2/0454] involving calculation of temperature -- The subclass B41J 2/0454 covers typewriter or selective printing mechanisms that incorporate temperature calculation, such as devices that adjust ink flow, heating elements or print head operation based on measured heat. It pertains to designs where temperature monitoring is integral to the printing process.
[B41J 2/04541] Specific driving circuit -- B41J 2/04541 classifies typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that incorporate a particular driving circuit for controlling the print-strike or marking process. It concerns electrical or electronic circuits that generate the timing and energy required to move the print element in the device.
[B41J 2/04543] Block driving -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[B41J 2/04545] Dynamic block driving -- Dynamic block driving refers to a mechanism in typewriters or selective printing devices where the type-bars or printing elements are moved by a continuously rotating or reciprocating block that imparts kinetic energy to each strike, enabling rapid, consistent imprinting.
[B41J 2/04546] Multiplexing -- The B41J 2/04546 entry covers typewriters or other selective printing devices that use multiplexing techniques, meaning they can generate several characters or lines in a single act of printing. It includes mechanisms such as multiple typebars, line-wise impact systems, or any arrangement that prints more than one mark simultaneously.
[B41J 2/04548] Details of power line section of control circuit -- This subclass concerns the power-line portion of the control circuit in typewriters or selective printing mechanisms, covering the design, routing, voltage regulation and protective features of the electrical supply that drives the printing elements and actuators.
[B41J 2/0455] Details of switching sections of circuit, e.g. transistors -- This subclass addresses the electrical switching circuitry used in typewriters or selective printing mechanisms, focusing on the design and arrangement of components such as transistors that control the activation of specific printing elements. It covers details of how the circuit sections route signals to select characters, typebars or print-head elements during the marking process.
[B41J 2/04551] using several operating modes -- The subgroup B41J 2/04551 covers typewriters or selective-printing mechanisms that can be switched among several operating modes, such as different character sets, fonts, widths or printing speeds, allowing the user to select the most suitable mode for a given task. This classification includes devices where the change of mode is integral to the printing process.
[B41J 2/04553] detecting ambient temperature -- B41J 2/04553 relates to typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that include means for detecting the ambient temperature. The detection is used to adjust printing parameters such as ink flow, ribbon heating or character formation to maintain consistent print quality under varying environmental conditions.
[B41J 2/04555] detecting current -- A feature of typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that monitors the electrical current flowing in the printing or marking components, used to detect actuation, control timing or diagnose faults in the device's operation. This detection is applied to ensure proper strike of keys or print elements and to provide feedback for power management.
[B41J 2/04556] detecting distance to paper -- B41J 2/04556 pertains to typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that include means for sensing the gap between the print head and the paper, allowing the device to adjust impact force or feed timing based on the measured distance.
[B41J 2/04558] detecting presence or properties of a dot on paper -- This subclass covers methods and devices that sense whether a dot has been printed on paper and evaluate its properties such as size, shape or colour. It relates to typewriter or selective-printing mechanisms that monitor the printing process.
[B41J 2/0456] detecting drop size, volume or weight -- B41J 2/0456 covers typewriter or selective printing devices equipped with means to detect the size, volume or weight of ink or other printing drops during the marking process, enabling control of drop formation and print quality. This subclass falls under the broader categories of performing operations, printing, and typewriters.
[B41J 2/04561] detecting presence or properties of a drop in flight -- The code covers methods and devices that sense an ink or fluid drop while it is travelling from the printhead to the medium in typewriters or other selective printing mechanisms, enabling detection of the drop's presence, trajectory, size or other properties during flight.
[B41J 2/04563] detecting head temperature; Ink temperature -- This subclass covers mechanisms for monitoring the temperature of the print head or ink in typewriters or selective printing devices, including sensors and control methods that ensure optimal printing performance and prevent overheating.
[B41J 2/04565] detecting heater resistance -- The subclass B41J 2/04565 covers typewriters or selective printing devices that incorporate means for detecting the electrical resistance of their heating elements. This detection is used to monitor heater condition, control temperature, or signal faults during the printing or marking process.
[B41J 2/04566] detecting humidity -- B41J 2/04566 is a sub-class of typewriters and selective printing mechanisms that includes features for sensing ambient humidity. The humidity-detection function is used to modify ink flow, type-bar impact or other printing parameters to maintain consistent mark quality under varying moisture conditions. This subclass groups inventions that integrate moisture sensors or control circuits into typewriter-type printing devices.
[B41J 2/04568] Control according to number of actuators used simultaneously -- B41J 2/04568 relates to typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that use control methods based on how many actuators operate at the same time. It covers systems that coordinate multiple striking elements to produce characters, adjusting the printing process according to the simultaneous activation of these actuators.
[B41J 2/0457] Power supply level being detected or varied -- B41J 2/0457 covers typewriters and other selective printing devices in which the power-supply level is detected or varied during operation, including circuitry or mechanisms that monitor voltage or current to ensure consistent printing performance.
[B41J 2/04571] detecting viscosity -- This CPC subclass concerns methods or devices built into typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that detect the viscosity of the ink or printing medium, enabling the system to adjust feed, pressure, or other parameters. Detecting viscosity helps maintain consistent mark quality by adapting the printing process to the fluid's properties.
[B41J 2/04573] Timing; Delays -- B41J 2/04573 relates to timing or delay mechanisms in typewriters and other selective printing devices, covering features that control the interval between key actuation and the printing action, such as buffered key strokes, delayed ribbon advance or programmable pause functions that affect print quality or enable special effects.
[B41J 2/04575] controlling heads of acoustic type -- B41J 2/04575 covers mechanisms that control the heads in acoustic-type typewriters, adjusting impact and timing of the striking elements to produce characters. These controlling heads manage the selection and drive of the typebars or printing elements in systems where sound is generated by the impact.
[B41J 2/04576] controlling heads of electrostatic type -- B41J 2/04576 covers control systems for the heads of electrostatic type-printing devices, addressing how the charge, position or operation of the print heads is regulated to achieve accurate marking. It pertains to typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that rely on electrostatic processes.
[B41J 2/04578] controlling heads based on electrostatically-actuated membranes -- B41J 2/04578 covers typewriter mechanisms where the printing heads are regulated by membranes that move electrostatically, allowing selective activation of the print elements. This sub-group falls under printing typewriters and defines control of heads via electrostatically-actuated membranes.
[B41J 2/0458] controlling heads based on heating elements forming bubbles -- B41J 2/0458 relates to typewriters or selective printing devices whose print heads are driven by heating elements that create bubbles to control the marking process. The subclass covers mechanisms that use bubble-forming thermal actuation to move ink or pigment onto the substrate.
[B41J 2/04581] controlling heads based on piezoelectric elements -- Typewriter or selective printing devices in which the print-or-marking head is driven or positioned by piezoelectric elements, using the electrical response of the material to produce precise head motion for each character or dot. This control method replaces mechanical levers or electromagnetic coils, enabling faster and more accurate printing actions.
[B41J 2/04583] controlling heads based on discharge by lowering the surface tension of meniscus -- B41J 2/04583 covers typewriter or selective printing mechanisms that adjust the printing heads by manipulating the meniscus surface tension to control ink or ribbon discharge. The method reduces surface tension to trigger or inhibit ink release, allowing precise regulation of mark formation during printing. This falls under the broader categories of performing operations, printing, and typewriters.
[B41J 2/04585] controlling heads based on thermal bent actuators -- The entry B41J 2/04585 covers typewriter or selective printing mechanisms that employ thermal-bent actuators to control the printing heads, using heat-induced bending of materials such as bimetallic strips or shape-memory alloys to drive the head movement and ink or impact application. This subclass falls under the broader categories of Performing Operations, Printing, and Typewriters.
[B41J 2/04586] controlling heads of a type not covered by groups  B41J2/04575  -  B41J2/04585 , or of an undefined type -- The entry B41J 2/04586 covers mechanisms that control printing heads of a type not included in the preceding groups B41J 2/04575-85, typically found in typewriters or other selective printing devices. It relates to the control of heads whose design or operation differs from the classified types, encompassing undefined or novel head configurations used for marking or printing.
[B41J 2/04588] using a specific waveform -- This subclass covers typewriters or selective printing devices that employ a particular electrical waveform to control the printing or marking process, such as shaping the drive current or impact pulse to achieve desired print characteristics. The waveform is used to regulate the actuation of the print element for improved quality or speed.
[B41J 2/0459] Height of the driving signal being adjusted -- B41J 2/0459 relates to typewriters or selective printing mechanisms in which the height of the driving signal can be adjusted, enabling the operator to vary the force or depth of the print impression. This adjustment changes the signal amplitude to control the intensity of the mark produced and is used to modify printing quality.
[B41J 2/04591] Width of the driving signal being adjusted -- This subclass covers typewriter or selective printing mechanisms where the width of the driving signal that controls the impact of the print element is varied or adjusted, affecting line width or character stroke. The adjustment may be used to produce different font widths or to fine-tune printing quality.
[B41J 2/04593] Dot-size modulation by changing the size of the drop -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[B41J 2/04595] Dot-size modulation by changing the number of drops per dot -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[B41J 2/04596] Non-ejecting pulses -- This CPC subclass covers typewriter or selective printing mechanisms that generate printing marks by means of non-ejecting pulses, meaning the marking action is produced without ejecting ink, ribbon or other marking medium from the device. It relates to the way the printing or marking process is driven rather than to specific characters or media.
[B41J 2/04598] Pre-pulse -- The B41J 2/04598 pre-pulse subclass covers typewriter or selective printing devices that apply a preliminary pulse before the main printing impulse. This pre-pulse conditions the print element or ribbon to improve ink transfer, reduce smearing and enhance durability, and is a specific technique within the broader printing operations of typewriters.
[B41J 2/05] produced by the application of heat -- B41J 2/05 refers to typewriters or selective printing devices that create marks by applying heat, such as thermal printing heads that darken heat-sensitive paper or ribbons. The classification covers any mechanism where the printing process is generated through thermal energy rather than ink transfer.
[B41J 2/055] Devices for absorbing or preventing back-pressure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[B41J 2/06] by electric or magnetic field -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This sub-group contains documents in which an electric field causes the ejection of ink directly, without involvement of a membrane or the like.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[B41J 2/065] involving the preliminary making of ink protuberances -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this group.
[B41J 2/07] characterised by jet control -- B41J 2/07 covers typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that create characters by means of a controllable jet of ink or marking fluid. The subclass includes devices where the jet is directed or modulated to place each print mark precisely.
[B41J 2/072] by thermal compensation -- This subclass of the typewriter group covers mechanisms that adjust the printing process to compensate for temperature variations, ensuring consistent ink transfer and character shape despite thermal expansion or contraction of components. Thermal-compensation features may include calibrated drive rates, heated elements, or feedback sensors that modify striking force or timing.
[B41J 2/075] for many-valued deflection -- The code B41J 2/075 covers typewriters or selective printing devices that use a many-valued deflection system, where the striking element can be moved to a large number of discrete positions to produce different characters. It pertains to the printing mechanism rather than the keyboard layout.
[B41J 2/08] charge-control type -- Charge-control type refers to typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that use an electrically controlled charge to attract or repel ink-bearing particles, allowing precise marking of characters. This approach governs the printing process rather than mechanical impact, enabling finer control over ink transfer and often improving print quality and speed. It falls under the broader categories of performing operations, printing, and typewriters.
[B41J 2/085] Charge means, e.g. electrodes -- This subclass concerns the devices that supply electric charge to the printing element of typewriters or selective printing mechanisms, such as electrodes that energise ribbons, impact pins or other marking elements to create printed characters.
[B41J 2/09] Deflection means -- This class covers devices that deflect the impression element in typewriters, such as levers or cams that shift the type slug or print head to produce different characters. It includes mechanisms that alter the path of the printing element during the marking process.
[B41J 2/095] electric field-control type -- B41J 2/095 pertains to typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that use an electric field to control the printing or marking process, falling within the printing subgroup of performing operations.
[B41J 2/10] magnetic field-control type -- The B41J 2/10 class covers typewriters or selective printing devices in which the printing or marking action is governed by a magnetic field. This includes mechanisms that use magnetic forces to position, drive or actuate the type-bars, print heads, or characters, rather than purely mechanical or electronic controls.
[B41J 21/00] Column, tabular or like printing arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Type-writers only.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Carrier-release mechanisms therefor -> B41J19/66
[B41J 21/02] Stops or stop-racks -- The B41J 21/02 class pertains to stops or stop-racks used in typewriter-type printing devices, particularly in column, tabular or similar printing arrangements. It covers mechanisms that hold, limit or accurately position the carriage or print head during operation.
[B41J 21/04] Mechanisms for setting or restoring tabulation stops -- This subclass covers devices and methods used in typewriters and similar printers to adjust, set, or restore tab stops, which control the horizontal positioning of characters for columnar printing.
[B41J 2/105] for binary-valued deflection -- B41J 2/105 covers typewriters or other selective printing mechanisms that use a binary-valued deflection to create marks, meaning the print element moves either fully to the printing position or remains retracted, without intermediate positions. This subclass concerns the design of the printing or marking process where the deflection is limited to two states, typically for simplified or high-speed printing actions.
[B41J 21/06] with means for preventing rebound from stops -- B41J 21/06 covers typewriter or similar printing devices that include mechanisms to stop the carriage or printing element from rebounding after it hits a stop, ensuring precise character placement and preventing over-travel. This sub-class falls under column, tabular or like printing arrangements within the broader categories of performing operations and printing.
[B41J 21/08] Mechanisms for initiating, effecting, skipping, or stopping tabulation movement -- This CPC subclass covers mechanisms that initiate, control, skip or stop the tabulation movement of a typewriter-style printing device. It is part of the column, tabular or similar printing arrangements within typewriters.
[B41J 2/11] for ink spray -- Typewriters or selective printing devices that apply ink by spraying it onto the print medium, rather than using impact or ribbon transfer methods. This sub-class covers mechanisms that generate a fine ink mist or jet to form characters, suitable for high-speed or continuous-flow printing. It is part of the broader group of printing operations within the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[B41J 21/10] with central, counter, or equivalent stop projected into path of tabulation stops -- The subclass B41J 21/10 covers typewriter or similar printing mechanisms that use a central, counter or equivalent stop that projects into the line of tabulation stops. This stop limits the movement of the carriage or printing head, providing fixed spacing or alignment for columns during printing.
[B41J 21/12] characterised by arrangements of electrical contacts -- B41J 21/12 concerns printing mechanisms, particularly typewriters or similar devices that produce columnar or tabular output, where the functional feature is the specific arrangement of electrical contacts that control the printing actions. It addresses the design of contact matrices, circuitry or switching schemes used to coordinate character selection and line formation in such printers.
[B41J 21/14] characterised by denominational arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  In general: decimal tabulation for type-writers.
[B41J 2/115] synchronising the droplet separation and charging time -- The entry covers methods for coordinating the moment a droplet is separated from a liquid source with the instant it receives an electrical charge in typewriter-style printing heads. By synchronising droplet formation and charging time, the device ensures consistent droplet size and placement, improving print quality and reducing mis-registration.
[B41J 21/16] controlled by the sensing of marks or formations on the paper being typed, an undersheet, or the platen -- The subclass B41J 21/16 relates to typewriters whose operation is governed by detecting marks, impressions or formations on the paper being typed, an undersheet, or the platen, enabling the machine to adjust timing, positioning or to stop automatically based on those sensed signals.
[B41J 21/17] controlled by stored information -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Memory.  Register controlled

[B41J 2/12] testing or correcting charge or deflection -- B41J 2/12 covers methods and apparatus for testing or adjusting the electrical charge or the deflection of the printing elements in typewriters and other selective printing devices, ensuring accurate character formation.
[B41J 2/125] Sensors, e.g. deflection sensors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Deflection sensors for continuous ink jet printers. [B41J 2/13] for inclination of printed pattern -- The B41J 2/13 code refers to typewriters or printing mechanisms designed for inclination of printed patterns. [B41J 2/135] Nozzles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This group should be left empty. Classify in the lower subgroups that cover structures of on-demand inkjet heads (not only of nozzles). [B41J 2/1621] Manufacturing processes -- B41J 2/1621 covers manufacturing processes used to produce typewriters or other selective printing mechanisms, focusing on the methods and steps that create the devices' printing or marking components. It falls under the broader categories of Performing operations, Printing, and Typewriters, and pertains to the fabrication techniques specific to these machines.
[B41J 2/1623] bonding and adhesion -- The CPC subclass B41J 2/1623 pertains to typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that employ bonding or adhesion to secure the ink, toner or other marking material onto the receiving surface during the printing process. It covers methods, devices or materials that create a lasting attachment of the mark through adhesive or binding actions.
[B41J 2/1625] electroforming -- B41J 2/1625 refers to the use of electroforming techniques in typewriter-type selective printing devices, covering processes that deposit metallic layers electrochemically to form printing elements or surfaces specifically designed for the marking or printing function of such machines.
[B41J 2/1626] etching -- The B41J 2/1626 subclass covers typewriter-type or selective printing devices that create marks by an etching process, where the image is produced through chemical or physical removal of material on a surface rather than by ink transfer. It includes mechanisms designed specifically for this form of printing.
[B41J 2/1628] dry etching -- The B41J 2/1628 entry covers dry etching, a non-liquid material-removal technique that uses gases or plasma to create patterns or characters on a surface. It is applied to typewriter-type selective printing mechanisms where the printing or marking process itself is defined by this dry-etching method.
[B41J 2/1629] wet etching -- The B41J 2/1629 entry covers typewriters or selective printing mechanisms that employ wet chemical etching to create the printing characters or plates. It specifies the use of liquid etchants to dissolve material on a surface, forming the desired marks for the printing or marking process.
[B41J 2/1631] photolithography -- B41J 2/1631 relates to photolithography methods used in typewriters or other selective printing mechanisms, where a light-sensitive pattern is created and transferred onto a substrate to form printed characters. The entry sits within the CPC hierarchy performing operations - printing - typewriters, covering devices whose printing or marking process is defined by this light-based technique.
[B41J 2/1632] machining -- B41J 2/1632 falls under the CPC section for performing operations, specifically printing typewriters and selective printing mechanisms, and covers machining processes applied to the construction or adjustment of typewriter components such as typebars, hammers or other parts that affect the printing or marking action.
[B41J 2/1634] laser machining -- B41J 2/1634 concerns laser machining techniques used on typewriters or other selective printing mechanisms, where a laser is employed to cut, shape, or otherwise modify printing elements or the substrate. It falls within the CPC hierarchy under Performing Operations → Printing → Typewriters.

The subclass addresses the manufacturing step where a mould is employed to create parts that are integral to the printing or marking process for which the device is designed.

[B41J 2/164] thin film formation -- B41J 2/164 covers typewriter-type or selective printing mechanisms that create a thin film of marking material on a substrate as part of the printing process. It includes apparatus and methods for depositing very fine layers of ink, toner or coating-such as vapor, spray or ink-jet techniques-directly onto the medium.
[B41J 2/1642] thin film formation by CVD [chemical vapor deposition] -- B41J 2/1642 covers typewriters and other selective printing mechanisms that employ thin-film formation through chemical vapor deposition, detailing how CVD processes are used to create the printing or marking surfaces. This subclass sits within the broader hierarchy of performing operations, printing, and typewriters.
[B41J 2/1643] thin film formation by plating -- A B41J 2/1643 method creates a thin metallic coating on a typewriter or selective-printing element by plating, such as electro- or chemical deposition, to improve ink transfer or durability. The process forms the film directly on the ribbon, print head or other marking surface, providing a uniform, controllable layer that enhances printing performance.
[B41J 2/1645] thin film formation by spincoating -- In the CPC class B41J 2/1645 thin film formation by spincoating is covered. It relates to typewriters or selective printing mechanisms where a liquid coating is spread onto a substrate by rotating the substrate at high-speed to create a uniform thin layer, used in printing or marking processes.
[B41J 2/1646] thin film formation by sputtering -- The B41J 2/1646 subclass covers the formation of thin films on substrates by sputtering for use in printing or marking devices such as typewriters and other selective printing mechanisms. It addresses sputtering deposition processes that create functional layers essential to the printing or marking operation.

[B41J 2/16541] Means to remove deposits from wipers or scrapers -- Means for cleaning the wiping or scraping elements of typewriters, such as devices that strip ink, dust or other deposits from wipers or scrapers to maintain consistent printing performance.
