The image is created during or after printing by a chemical change rather than by applying pre-coloured inks.
[B41M 3/144] using fluorescent, luminescent or iridescent effects -- B41M 3/144 covers printing processes that incorporate fluorescent, luminescent or iridescent effects into the printed product, such as applying light-reactive pigments to create glowing or color-shifting patterns. It relates to the use of these special visual effects during or after the printing operation.
[B41M 3/146] using a non human-readable pattern which becomes visible on reproduction, e.g. a void mark -- B41M 3/146 covers printing processes that embed a pattern not readable by humans in the original work but that becomes visible when the item is reproduced, such as a void-mark that appears on copies or scans. This subclass falls under printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes for producing specific printed designs.
[B41M 3/148] Transitory images, i.e. images only visible from certain viewing angles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  E.g. images printed with differently reflecting pigments.
[B41M 3/16] Braille printing -- B41M 3/16 deals with Braille printing, covering methods and apparatus for producing tactile printed material such as Braille characters for visually impaired users. It is a subset of printing processes that create specialized printed work.
[B41M 3/18] Particular kinds of wallpapers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for printing on wallpapers.
[B41M 5/00] Duplicating or marking methods -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[B41M 5/0005] Enlarging or reduction of graphic information on a support by stretching or contracting the support, optionally in combination with the recording -- This CPC subclass B41M 5/0005 relates to processes that enlarge or reduce graphic information on a support by physically stretching or contracting the support itself, optionally combined with a recording step.
[B41M 5/0011] Pre-treatment or treatment during printing of the recording material, e.g. heating, irradiating  after-treatment of prints  B41M7/00 ; printers for treating or overcoating copy materials before, during or after printing  B41J11/0015 -- The code B41M 5/0011 refers to processes and devices that apply pre-treatment or post-treatment to a recording material during printing, such as heating, irradiation or over-coating, and to printers specially equipped to perform these treatments before, during or after the print operation.
[B41M 5/0017] Application of ink-fixing material, e.g. mordant, precipitating agent, on the substrate prior to printing, e.g. by ink-jet printing, coating or spraying -- B41M 5/0017 covers the step of applying an ink-fixing material such as a mordant or precipitating agent to a substrate before the printing operation, often by ink-jet, coating or spraying. This prepares the surface to retain the ink more permanently in duplicating or marking processes.
[B41M 5/0023] Digital printing methods characterised by the inks used  inks  per se   C09D11/00 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods where the ink is used in a special way, e.g. two per se known inks are used in combination giving a special effect.
[B41M 5/0029] Formation of a transparent pattern using a liquid marking fluid -- The code B41M 5/0029 covers a method of forming a transparent pattern on a substrate by applying a liquid marking fluid that creates a latent image visible only under specific lighting or treatment. The technique is used in security printing and other applications where concealed markings are required.
[B41M 5/0035] Uncoated paper  paper making  D21 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for printing characterised by the ink receiving properties of the paper without a separate ink receiving layer.
[B41M 5/0041] Digital printing on surfaces other than ordinary paper  B41M5/0082  takes precedence; printing on textiles  D06P5/00 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Digital printing methods, i.e. not using printer's forme, used for printing on other surfaces than orfinary paper, including: metals and oxidated metal surfaces, plastics, horn, rubber, other organic polymers, glass, ceramic, tiles, concrete, stones, wooden surfaces, leather, linoleum, skin or flowers.  **Glossary:** - Ordinary paper: sheets made of traditional paper mass
[B41M 5/0047] by ink-jet printing -- B41M 5/0047 covers duplicating or marking methods that use ink-jet printing to apply ink onto a substrate. It falls under the printing hierarchy, specifically the subcategory for printing, duplicating, marking, or copying processes, and details the use of ink-jet technology for creating images, text, or patterns.
[B41M 5/0052] by thermal printing -- The CPC subclass B41M 5/0052 pertains to duplicating or marking methods that employ thermal printing, a process in which heat-generated images are formed on a substrate. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, printing, and printing-duplicating-marking processes.
[B41M 5/0058] on metals and oxidised metal surfaces -- This subclass covers processes for duplicating, marking or otherwise applying information to metal substrates and to metal surfaces that have been oxidised. It includes techniques such as laser etching, inkjet printing, thermal transfer or other methods that directly affect the metal or its oxide layer.
[B41M 5/0064] on plastics, horn, rubber, or other organic polymers -- This subclass covers duplicating, marking or copying methods applied to substrates such as plastics, horn, rubber or other organic polymers. It is situated under printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes within the broader performing-operations hierarchy.
[B41M 5/007] on glass, ceramic, tiles, concrete, stones, etc. -- B41M 5/007 covers methods for printing, duplicating or marking hard, non-metallic substrates such as glass, ceramic, tiles, concrete, stone and similar materials. The classification includes techniques that apply inks, pigments, laminates or other marking agents to these surfaces, using processes like screen printing, ink-jet, laser or heat-based transfers. It is situated under the broader categories of performing operations, printing and duplicating or marking processes.
[B41M 5/0076] on wooden surfaces, leather, linoleum, skin, or flowers -- The classification covers methods for duplicating, marking or printing on non-paper substrates such as wooden surfaces, leather, linoleum, skin and flowers. It pertains to printing and duplicating processes that adapt ink-or pigment-based techniques to these organic or porous materials.
[B41M 5/0082] Digital printing on bodies of particular shapes  sublimation or volatilisation of pre-printed design  B41M5/035 -- B41M 5/0082 covers digital printing techniques applied to three-dimensional objects of specific shapes, where a pre-printed image is transferred onto the substrate by sublimation or volatilisation. The method enables precise, high-resolution coloration of irregular surfaces such as curved panels, containers or decorative items.
[B41M 5/0088] by ink-jet printing -- B41M 5/0088 covers methods of duplicating or marking that employ ink-jet printing, a technology that propels droplets of ink onto a substrate to form images or text. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations → printing → printing, duplicating, marking, or copying processes, specifically addressing ink-jet techniques used for rapid, non-contact marking in industrial and commercial applications.
[B41M 5/0094] by thermal printing -- This subclass of the CPC covers duplicating or marking processes that employ thermal printing, where heat-activated inks or toners produce an image on a substrate without a traditional ink-transfer mechanism. It includes direct thermal, thermal-transfer and related techniques used for producing printed or marked copies.
[B41M 5/025] by transferring ink from the master sheet -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Transfer printing with intermediate carrier.  The principle of transfer printing is as follows:  the image transfers by melting: B41M2205/06  the image transfer by sublimation: B41M2205/02  B41M5/025 (melting image)  B41M5/035 (sublimating image)  B41M5/0256 (melting + sublimating image)
[B41M 5/0253] using a chemical colour-forming ink, e.g. chemical hectography  B41M5/035  takes precedence -- The Cooperative Patent Classification code B41M 5/0253 pertains to duplicating or marking methods that use a chemical colour-forming ink, such as in chemical hectography, though it is noted that the classification B41M 5/035 takes precedence when applicable.
[B41M 5/0256] the transferable ink pattern being obtained by means of a computer driven printer, e.g. an ink jet or laser printer, or by electrographic means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  E.g. transfer pictures for T-shirts are quite often printed with this kind of method.  **Glossary:** - Computer driven printer: printers for digital printing methods, i.e. methods not using printer's form
[B41M 5/03] by pressure -- The B41M 5/03 subgroup relates to duplication or marking processes that create an image by applying pressure to a medium, such as stamping, embossing, or pressure-sensitive printing. It covers techniques where the pressure itself transfers ink, pigment or a relief pattern onto the substrate without the use of heat, chemicals or other energy sources.
[B41M 5/035] by sublimation or volatilisation of design -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Methods concerning imagewise heating -> B41M5/382
[B41M 5/0351] on anodized aluminium -- B41M 5/0351 covers methods for marking, duplicating or printing on anodized aluminium, including techniques that apply inks, pigments, lasers or other agents to the anodized surface to create durable images or markings. It is classified under duplicating or marking methods within the broader printing and operating processes.
[B41M 5/0353] using heat shrinkable film material; Thermotransfer combined with the shaping of the workpiece; Recto-verso printing; Image correction -- B41M 5/0353 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers techniques involving the use of heat shrinkable film, combining thermotransfer with workpiece shaping, recto-verso printing, and image correction methods.
[B41M 5/0355] characterised by the macromolecular coating or impregnation used to obtain dye receptive properties -- B41M 5/0355 covers duplicating or marking processes in which the material to be printed is treated with a macromolecular coating or impregnated with a polymeric layer to make the surface receptive to dyes. The coating provides dye-binding sites, improving colour uptake and image quality in printing, copying or marking operations.
[B41M 5/0356] characterised by the inks used for printing the pattern on the temporary support or additives therefor, e.g. dyes, transferable compounds, binders or transfer promoting additives -- This CPC subclass (B41M 5/0356) covers duplicating or marking processes that are defined by the inks applied to a temporary support, including dyes, transferable compounds, binders and transfer-promoting additives. It falls under Performing operations → Printing → Printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes → Duplicating or marking methods.
[B41M 5/0358] characterised by the mechanisms or artifacts to obtain the transfer, e.g. the heating means, the pressure means or the transport means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Printing methods where the transfer of the ink from the master sheet is made by sublimation or volatilisation of desing and wherein the method is characterised by specific details of the mechanisms to obtain the transfer, e.g. the heating means, the pressure means or the transport means.
[B41M 5/04] using solvent-soluble dyestuffs on the master sheets, e.g. alcohol-soluble -- This subclass covers methods of applying solvent-soluble dyestuffs, such as alcohol-soluble inks, to master sheets used in duplicating or marking processes. It includes techniques where the dye is dissolved in a solvent before transfer to the substrate, enabling efficient colour reproduction in printing.
[B41M 5/06] using master sheets coated with jelly-like materials, e.g. gelatin -- B41M 5/06 relates to duplicating or marking techniques that employ a master sheet coated with a gelatin-like material to create a relief image, which is then used to transfer ink or pigment onto a target surface.
[B41M 5/08] Sheet materials therefor -- This subclass covers sheet-like substrates used in printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes, such as paper, film, foil and other flexible materials on which images or text are recorded. It includes various types of sheets, their properties and preparation for use in the processes defined in the parent classes.
[B41M 5/10] by using carbon paper or the like -- B41M 5/10 covers duplicating or marking processes that employ carbon paper or similar transfer media to reproduce images or text onto a substrate. The method transfers pigment from a coated sheet to the target surface by pressure, allowing fast, low-cost copying without electronic equipment.
[B41M 5/124] using pressure to make a masked colour visible, e.g. to make a coloured support visible, to create an opaque or transparent pattern, or to form colour by uniting colour-forming components -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pressure sensitive papers.
[B41M 5/1243] Inert particulate additives, e.g. protective stilt materials -- B41M 5/1243 covers the use of inert particulate additives, such as protective stilt or spacer materials, in duplicating, marking or copying operations. These additives are introduced to prevent direct contact between the substrate and the printing or marking surface, reducing abrasion, smearing or transfer of material. The entry addresses methods of applying such particles during the process.
[B41M 5/1246] Application of the layer, e.g. by printing -- B41M 5/1246 relates to techniques for applying a material layer onto a substrate as part of printing, duplicating or marking operations, for example by ink-jet, screen or other printing methods. It covers the deposition of functional or decorative coatings during the formation of printed images or markings.
[B41M 5/128] Desensitisers -- B41M 5/128 covers desensitisers, which are substances applied to photosensitive printing plates or other duplicating media to reduce their light sensitivity after exposure, facilitating safer handling, storage and further processing. These agents help prevent unintended image development and improve the stability of the recorded image before subsequent processing steps.
[B41M 5/132] Chemical colour-forming components -- B41M 5/132 pertains to the chemical components that generate colour in printing, duplicating or marking processes, such as dyes, pigments, developers or colour-forming agents used in inks, toners and photographic emulsions. These substances are employed to produce the visible image or copy during the printing operation.
[B41M 5/136] Organic colour formers, e.g. leuco dyes -- The CPC subclass B41M 5/136 pertains to organic colour formers, such as leuco dyes, that develop color when activated by oxidation, heat or other means. These compounds are used in printing, duplicating and marking processes to create visible images or text. It falls under the duplicating or marking methods category of printing operations.
[B41M 5/1363] Leuco dyes forming a complex with a metal cation -- The B41M 5/1363 subclass covers printing, duplicating or marking methods that employ leuco dyes which, after activation, form a stable complex with a metal cation. This includes processes where the metal-dye complex creates a visible image or mark, such as thermographic or electro-chemical printing techniques that rely on the metal-induced color development of the leuco compound.
[B41M 5/1366] characterised solely by tri (aryl or hetaryl)methane derivatives  B41M5/1363  takes precedence -- B41M 5/1366 relates to printing, duplicating or marking methods that are distinguished solely by the use of tri-(aryl or heteroaryl)methane derivatives, for example as components of inks, toners or surface-active agents. The subclass is nested under B41M 5/1363, which provides the broader context for such chemical additives in printing processes.
[B41M 5/145] with a lactone or lactam ring -- This subclass covers duplicating or marking processes that employ a marking material comprising a lactone or lactam ring, such as inks, resins or coatings in which these cyclic esters or amides serve as the active component.
[B41M 5/1455] characterised by fluoran compounds -- The CPC subclass B41M 5/1455 covers duplicating or marking processes that employ fluoran compounds as the marking agents, typically to impart fluorescence or distinctive coloration to the reproduced image. It includes methods and apparatuses where fluoran-based inks, dyes or toners are applied in printing, copying or marking operations.
[B41M 5/15] Spiro-pyrans -- The code B41M 5/15 covers the use of spiro-pyran compounds as colorants in printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes. Spiro-pyrans are organic dyes that provide bright, stable hues in inks and toners applied to substrates through various marking methods.
[B41M 5/155] Colour-developing components, e.g. acidic compounds -- B41M 5/155 concerns chemical substances that create or intensify colour in printing, duplicating or marking operations, such as acidic developers that react with inks or dyes to produce visible images.
[B41M 5/1555] Inorganic mineral developers, e.g. clays -- B41M 5/1555 relates to the use of inorganic mineral developers, such as clays, in printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes. These mineral developers are applied to enhance image formation or contrast on a substrate during the operation.
[B41M 5/165] characterised by the use of microcapsules -- This subclass covers printing, duplicating or marking processes that employ microcapsules, where the color-forming material is encapsulated and released by a stimulus such as pressure, heat or light. The technique is used to create images or text by breaking the capsules at the desired locations on the substrate.
[B41M 5/1655] Solvents -- B41M 5/1655 covers the selection and use of solvents in duplicating or marking operations, such as those employed to dissolve or disperse printing inks, stains, or cleaning agents on substrates. It pertains to processes within the printing, duplicating, marking, or copying field that rely on solvent-based formulations to achieve image transfer or substrate preparation.
[B41M 5/20] using electric current -- B41M 5/20 covers duplicating or marking methods that use an electric current to effect the transfer or alteration of material on a substrate. It includes electrostatic, electrochemical, laser-induced or discharge-based processes for printing, marking or copying, where the energy source is supplied as an electric current.
[B41M 5/205] and an eroding electrode -- B41M 5/205 covers printing, duplicating or marking processes that employ an eroding electrode, where the electrode is used to remove material electro-mechanically to create a pattern, image or mark on a substrate. It is a sub-category of duplicating or marking methods within the broader printing domain.
[B41M 5/24] Ablative recording, e.g. by burning marks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods wherein the material is removed from the surface (only ablation imaging).
[B41M 5/245] Electroerosion or spark recording -- The code B41M 5/245 covers methods that use electroerosion, spark discharge or similar electrical discharges to record images, text or patterns on a substrate. It falls under performing operations → printing → printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes → duplicating or marking methods, and pertains to spark-based or electro-erosion recording techniques.
[B41M 5/26] Thermography ; -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods wherein the material changes colour, when marked (without ablation).
[B41M 5/262] recording or marking of inorganic surfaces or materials, e.g. glass, metal, or ceramics  marking of plastic artifacts with inorganic additives  B41M5/267 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Thermographic recording or marking methods of inorganic surfaces or materials, including: glass, metal or ceramics. The recording or marking is usually made by an infrared laser.
[B41M 5/265] for the production of optical filters or electrical components -- B41M 5/265 relates to printing-based methods for making optical filters or electrical components, where the desired functional layers are deposited or patterned on a substrate by duplicating, marking or other printing operations. The subclass includes techniques that form filter structures or conductive features using inks, pastes or coatings applied with printing equipment.
[B41M 5/267] Marking of plastic artifacts, e.g. with laser -- The CPC subclass B41M 5/267 covers techniques for marking plastic artifacts, such as parts or products made of polymer material, using processes like laser inscription, engraving, or other non-contact methods. It falls under the broader groups for printing and duplicating operations, specifically addressing the application of marking technologies to plastic items.
[B41M 5/28] using thermochromic compounds or layers containing liquid crystals, microcapsules, bleachable dyes or heat- decomposable compounds, e.g. gas- liberating -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[B41M 5/281] using liquid crystals only -- B41M 5/281 covers duplicating or marking processes that employ liquid crystals as the only functional material. It relates to printing and copying technologies where the image or pattern is created by the orientation or phase change of a liquid-crystal layer, without the use of inks, toners or other additives, and falls under the PRINTING-DUPLICATING-MARKING hierarchy of the CPC.
[B41M 5/282] using thermochromic compounds -- The subclass B41M 5/282 covers printing, duplicating or marking methods that employ thermochromic compounds, which change colour in response to temperature. It includes processes where heat-sensitive pigments are applied during or after printing to produce images or marks that appear, disappear or shift hue with temperature variations.
[B41M 5/283] Inorganic thermochromic compounds -- B41M 5/283 covers inorganic thermochromic compounds, substances that undergo a reversible colour change in response to temperature variations and are employed in printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes to produce temperature-dependent visual effects.
[B41M 5/284] Organic thermochromic compounds -- B41M 5/284 covers organic thermochromic compounds-materials that change colour with temperature-and their use in printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes, where the colour shift provides visual effects, security features or functional indicators.
[B41M 5/285] Polyacetylenes -- B41M 5/285 covers the use of polyacetylenes-conductive polymer materials-in duplicating, marking or copying processes within the printing field. It relates to methods where these polymers are applied to substrates to create or enhance printed images, often by exploiting their electrical or optical properties. This subclass falls under the broader categories of performing operations, printing, and specific duplicating or marking techniques.
[B41M 5/286] using compounds undergoing unimolecular fragmentation to obtain colour shift, e.g. bleachable dyes -- The B41M 5/286 entry covers duplicating or marking techniques that create a colour shift by applying compounds that undergo unimolecular fragmentation, such as bleachable dyes that change hue when the dye molecule breaks down. This method is used to produce temporary or erasable colours in printing and marking processes.
[B41M 5/287] using microcapsules or microspheres only -- The subclass B41M 5/287 covers printing, duplicating or marking processes in which the functional material is applied exclusively via microcapsules or microspheres, such as ink or dye particles encased in a protective shell that releases the content during exposure, heat, pressure or other activation. This method is used to create images, text or markings with controlled release of the marking substance.
[B41M 5/288] using gas liberating compounds, e.g. to obtain vesicular or blow-up images  B41M3/16 ,  B41M9/04  take precedence; thermogravure printing  B41M7/02 -- B41M 5/288 covers printing methods that employ gas-liberating compounds to form vesicular or blown-up images on a substrate, typically in duplicating or marking operations. The technique creates a raised, bubble-like impression by releasing gas during the printing process. It is subordinate to B41M 3/16 and B41M 9/04 and is distinct from thermogravure printing classified under B41M 7/02.
[B41M 5/30] using chemical colour formers -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[B41M 5/305] with reversible electron-donor electron-acceptor compositions -- B41M 5/305 covers duplicating or marking processes that employ reversible electron-donor/electron-acceptor compositions, such as electro-chromic or thermochromic materials that can switch between visible states to create or erase images. The classification is part of the cooperative patent system for printing and marking technologies.
[B41M 5/32] one component being a heavy metal compound, -- This subclass covers duplicating or marking methods in which a heavy-metal compound is used as a component of the marking material, such as inks, toners, or coatings applied in printing, stamping or labeling. It includes processes that exploit the heavy metal for properties like coloration, conductivity or radiation shielding.
[B41M 5/323] Organic colour formers, e.g. leuco dyes -- The B41M 5/323 subclass covers organic colour-forming compounds employed in printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes, particularly leuco dyes that develop colour upon oxidation or heating. It falls under the hierarchy of Performing Operations → Printing → Printing, Duplicating, Marking, or Copying Processes → Duplicating or Marking Methods.
[B41M 5/327] with a lactone or lactam ring -- B41M 5/327 covers duplicating or marking methods that employ a lactone or lactam ring in the printing medium or surface treatment. The claim typically involves using monomers or polymers containing these cyclic structures to form durable inks, coatings or cured layers during printing, copying or marking operations.
[B41M 5/3275] Fluoran compounds -- The B41M 5/3275 subclass covers the use of fluoran compounds in duplicating or marking methods, such as inks, sensitizers or colorants applied during printing, copying or other marking processes. It groups technologies that rely on the specific chemical properties of fluoran derivatives to achieve desired coloration or image formation. This falls under the broader category of performing operations for printing and related duplication techniques.
[B41M 5/333] Colour developing components therefor, e.g. acidic compounds -- **Glossary:** - Colour developing components: anything that reacts with the colour formers
[B41M 5/3331] Macromolecular compounds -- The B41M 5/3331 subclass covers macromolecular compounds employed in duplicating or marking processes, such as polymer-based inks, resins, or coating materials that form the functional layer on printed or marked substrates. It includes the formulation and use of these large-molecule substances in printing, copying, or marking operations.
[B41M 5/3333] Non-macromolecular compounds -- It covers the use of low-molecular-weight chemicals such as solvents, developers, fixatives or other additives that are not polymers, employed in printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes to affect ink, toner or image formation. The class excludes macromolecular substances.
[B41M 5/3335] Compounds containing phenolic or carboxylic acid groups or metal salts thereof -- B41M 5/3335 covers the use of chemical compounds that contain phenolic or carboxylic acid groups, including their metal salts, in printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes. These substances are applied as inks, dyes, toners or protective coatings to achieve coloration, adhesion or corrosion resistance on substrates during the printing operation.
[B41M 5/3336] Sulfur compounds, e.g. sulfones, sulfides, sulfonamides -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sulphur compounds having phenolic or carboxylic acid groups.
[B41M 5/3338] Inorganic compounds -- B41M 5/3338 falls under the printing, duplicating, marking and copying processes group and specifically addresses the use of inorganic compounds in these operations. It includes inorganic pigments, fillers, catalysts or any other non-organic chemicals employed in printing inks, toners, dyes or surface treatments for duplicating and marking.
[B41M 5/337] Additives -- The subclass B41M 5/337 covers additives employed in duplicating or marking methods, such as inks, toners, or other printing media. It includes substances that alter properties like viscosity, drying speed, color intensity, adhesion, or resistance to fading, thereby improving the performance or quality of printed outputs.
[B41M 5/3372] Macromolecular compounds -- This CPC subclass covers the use of macromolecular compounds such as polymers, resins or gels in duplicating or marking processes, including their role as inks, coatings or binding agents. It applies to printing operations where large-molecule materials are employed to form images, text or patterns on a substrate. The class is used for inventions that incorporate such compounds in marking or duplication methods.
[B41M 5/3375] Non-macromolecular compounds -- B41M 5/3375 refers to the use of non-macromolecular compounds such as inks, dyes, toners or other low-molecular-weight chemicals in printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes. It covers the formulation, delivery and handling of these substances to achieve the desired image or code on a substrate.
[B41M 5/3377] Inorganic compounds, e.g. metal salts of organic acids -- B41M 5/3377 concerns the use of inorganic compounds, such as metal salts of organic acids, in printing, duplicating or marking processes. It covers their application as developers, mordants, catalysts or other chemical agents that facilitate the formation, fixing or enhancement of marks on substrates during duplication or marking operations.
[B41M 5/34] Multicolour thermography -- Multicolour thermography refers to thermal printing processes that produce images or marks in two or more colors by selectively heating different areas of a thermally sensitive medium, often using multiple heat sources or sequential heating steps. The technique is employed in duplicating, marking or coding applications where colour differentiation is required.
[B41M 5/345] by thermal transfer of dyes or pigments -- This subclass covers duplicating or marking methods that apply dyes or pigments by means of a thermal-transfer technique. It is situated within the printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes branch of the CPC hierarchy.
[B41M 5/36] using a polymeric layer, which may be particulate and which is deformed or structurally changed with modification of its' properties, e.g. of its' optical hydrophobic-hydrophilic, solubility or permeability properties -- This subclass covers methods where a polymeric coating or particulate layer applied to a substrate is physically altered - by deformation, stretching, or other structural changes - to intentionally modify its functional characteristics such as optical appearance, wettability, solubility or barrier properties during printing or duplicating processes.
[B41M 5/361] using a polymeric matrix with inorganic particles forming an image by orientation or agglomeration -- A method for printing or marking that employs a polymeric matrix containing inorganic particles, where the image is produced by orienting or causing the particles to agglomerate within the polymer. The particle arrangement creates the visual pattern, often using magnetic, electric or mechanical forces.
[B41M 5/363] using materials comprising a polymeric matrix containing a low molecular weight organic compound such as a fatty acid, e.g. for reversible recording -- The code B41M 5/363 covers duplicating or marking processes that employ a polymeric matrix loaded with a low-molecular-weight organic compound, such as a fatty acid, to create a reversible recording medium. This method enables temporary marks that can be erased or altered by subsequent treatment.
[B41M 5/366] using materials comprising a polymeric matrix containing a polymeric particulate material, e.g. hydrophobic heat coalescing particles -- The subclass B41M 5/366 covers printing, duplicating or marking processes that employ a material formed from a polymeric matrix in which polymeric particulate additives are dispersed, such as hydrophobic heat-coalescing particles that enhance ink drying or image fixing. This approach is used to improve the performance of inks, toners or other marking media during the transfer of images.
[B41M 5/368] involving the creation of a soluble/insoluble or hydrophilic/hydrophobic permeability pattern; Peel development  B41M5/366  takes precedence -- B41M 5/368 covers printing, duplicating or marking methods that create a pattern of differing permeability-such as soluble versus insoluble or hydrophilic versus hydrophobic areas-and employ a peel-development step to reveal the image. It is a subcategory of B41M 5/366, which has priority over it.
[B41M 5/382] Contact thermal transfer or sublimation processes -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[B41M 5/38207] characterised by aspects not provided for in groups  B41M5/385  -  B41M5/395 -- B41M 5/38207 is a CPC subclass within performing operations - printing - printing, duplicating, marking, or copying processes, focusing on duplicating or marking methods. It covers devices or processes that are characterised by aspects not provided for in groups B41M5/385 - B41M5/395.
[B41M 5/38214] Structural details, e.g. multilayer systems  composition of individual layers  B41M5/42 -- The subclass B41M 5/38214 covers structural aspects of printing and duplicating media, specifically the design and composition of multilayer systems and the individual layers that make up those media. It includes the materials, thicknesses and arrangements of layers used to achieve particular functional or optical properties.
[B41M 5/38221] Apparatus features -- B41M 5/38221 relates to the design and construction details of devices used in duplicating or marking operations. It covers mechanical or functional parts of printers, duplicators, stampers and similar equipment, such as frames, feed mechanisms, rollers, guides or mounting structures that affect the process.
[B41M 5/38228] characterised by the use of two or more ink layers -- B41M 5/38228 covers printing, duplicating or marking processes that employ two or more ink layers, such as overprinting or layered ink applications to achieve specific visual effects, improved durability, or functional properties. This subclass defines methods where successive ink layers are applied in a controlled sequence as a core feature of the marking technique.
[B41M 5/38235] characterised by transferable colour-forming materials -- A subclass of duplicating and marking methods that use colour-forming substances which can be transferred from a carrier medium onto a substrate. It includes processes such as thermal transfer, ribbon-based printing, and other techniques where inks, pigments or dyes are moved from a backing material to produce the desired image or marking.
[B41M 5/38242] characterised by the use of different kinds of energy to effect transfer, e.g. heat and light -- A sub-group of printing, duplicating and marking processes that use more than one form of energy-typically heat together with light-to transfer ink, toner or other marking material onto a substrate. It is placed under B41M 5/38 - duplicating or marking methods.
[B41M 5/3825] Electric current carrying heat transfer sheets -- The entry B41M 5/3825 covers heat-transfer sheets that incorporate conductive elements to carry electric current and generate heat for transferring images or markings. These sheets are used in printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes where a controlled current heats the transfer layer to bond ink or toner onto a substrate. The technology enables precise, on-demand image creation without conventional ink-jet or laser mechanisms.
[B41M 5/38257] characterised by the use of an intermediate receptor -- This subclass covers duplication or marking processes that employ an intermediate receptor-such as a receiving film, plate or surface-to capture ink, toner or a latent image before it is transferred to the final substrate, enabling improved image quality or handling of delicate materials.
[B41M 5/38264] Overprinting of thermal transfer images -- The B41M 5/38264 subclass covers methods for overprinting thermal-transfer images, where a second image is printed directly on top of an existing thermal-transfer layer. It includes techniques that align, register, or otherwise modify the original image using additional thermal-transfer inks or toners to add colour, detail, or security features.
[B41M 5/38271] using microcapsules -- This subclass covers the use of microcapsules in duplicating or marking processes, where the capsules release a marking material during printing, copying or other imprinting steps. It includes methods that employ microencapsulated inks, pigments or chemicals to achieve selective deposition, colour change or security features on a substrate.
[B41M 5/38278] using ink-containing structures, e.g. porous or microporous layers, alveoles or cellules -- This subclass covers printing or duplicating techniques that employ ink-containing structures such as porous or microporous layers, alveoles or cellules to hold and transfer ink. The method relies on the ink being retained within these structured media before being deposited onto a substrate, enabling controlled ink release in processes like stamping, marking or copying.
[B41M 5/38285] characterised by magnetic components in the transfer ink -- The subclass B41M 5/38285 covers duplicating or marking methods that employ transfer inks containing magnetic particles, enabling magnetic recording, alignment or manipulation of the printed image. It pertains to printing processes where the ink's magnetic components are a defining feature of the technique.
[B41M 5/38292] with correction means -- B41M 5/38292 is a Cooperative Patent Classification sub-group under PERFORMING OPERATIONS → PRINTING → PRINTING, DUPLICATING, MARKING, OR COPYING PROCESSES, covering duplicating or marking methods that incorporate correction means to amend or remove errors made during the process. This code is used to classify inventions that provide ways to adjust printed or marked output after it has been produced.
[B41M 5/385] characterised by the transferable dyes or pigments -- This CPC subclass covers printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes in which the image is formed using transferable dyes or pigments that are applied to a substrate and then transferred, e.g., dye-transfer printing, pigment transfer. It includes methods where the colorant is first deposited on an intermediate carrier before being moved to the final material.
[B41M 5/3852] Anthraquinone or naphthoquinone dyes -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[B41M 5/3854] Dyes containing one or more acyclic carbon-to-carbon double bonds, e.g., di- or tri-cyanovinyl, methine -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[B41M 5/3856] Dyes characterised by an acyclic -X=C group, where X can represent both nitrogen and a substituted carbon atom -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[B41M 5/3858] Mixtures of dyes, at least one being a dye classifiable in one of groups  B41M5/385  -  B41M5/39 -- B41M 5/3858 covers formulations that combine two or more dyes for printing, duplicating, marking or copying, where at least one component belongs to the dye families defined in sub-groups B41M5/385 to B41M5/39. These mixtures are used to obtain specific colour or functional properties in the process.
[B41M 5/388] Azo dyes -- Azo dyes are synthetic organic pigments that contain an -N=N- azo group and provide vivid colors for inks, toners and other marking media. They are widely used in printing and duplicating processes such as offset printing, laser copying and flexographic printing to achieve bright and stable hues. This entry covers the chemical classes and applications of these dyes within the CPC grouping for duplicating or marking methods.
[B41M 5/39] Dyes containing one or more carbon-to-nitrogen double bonds, e.g. azomethine -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[B41M 5/392] Additives, other than colour forming substances, dyes or pigments, e.g. sensitisers, transfer promoting agents -- B41M 5/392 refers to additives used in duplicating or marking methods that are not colour-forming agents such as dyes or pigments. The entry includes substances like sensitisers and transfer-promoting agents that enhance the performance of printing, copying or marking processes.
[B41M 5/395] Macromolecular additives, e.g. binders -- Macromolecular additives such as polymeric binders used to improve the performance of inks, toners, coatings or other materials in printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes, including those that enhance adhesion, film formation, rheology or durability. This subclass covers the composition and function of these large-molecule additives within the broader context of printing operations.
[B41M 5/398] Processes based on the production of stickiness patterns using powders -- B41M 5/398 covers processes that create patterned stickiness by applying powdery adhesives to a surface, then forming the desired design through selective activation or removal of the powder. These methods are used in printing, duplicating or marking operations to produce decorative, functional or security patterns without liquid inks.
[B41M 5/40] characterised by the base , intermediate, or covering layers, -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The layer structures of both direct thermal recording and thermal transfer sheets (= thermal transfer donor sheets and thermal transfer receiving sheets) except the image receiving layer of the thermal transfer receiving sheets (see the table below).
[B41M 5/405] characterised by layers cured by radiation  layers cured after recording  B41M7/0072 -- B41M 5/405 covers printing or duplicating methods in which successive material layers are solidified by radiation after they have been recorded, such as UV or laser curing of inks or polymers. The classification applies to processes that build up an image or pattern layer-by-layer by exposing each layer to a curing source.
[B41M 5/41] Base layers -- B41M 5/41 covers methods for applying a base layer to a substrate in printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes, including priming, coating or otherwise preparing the surface before the final image or text is transferred. It addresses the composition, application technique and drying of the underlying layer that supports subsequent printing steps.
[B41M 5/42] Intermediate, , or covering layers -- B41M 5/42 relates to printing, duplicating or marking processes that employ intermediate, protective or covering layers. It covers coatings, primers, release films or similar materials applied between a substrate and the printed image to enhance adhesion, protect the substrate, or enable later removal.
[B41M 5/423] characterised by non-macromolecular compounds, e.g. waxes -- The CPC subclass B41M 5/423 covers duplicating or marking methods that employ non-macromolecular compounds, such as waxes, as the functional material. It includes processes where waxes are applied to create reproduced images, text or markings on substrates, rather than using polymer-based inks or coatings. This subclass falls under printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes within Performing Operations.
[B41M 5/426] characterised by inorganic compounds, e.g. metals, metal salts, metal complexes -- B41M 5/426 covers printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes that are defined by the use of inorganic compounds such as metals, metal salts or metal complexes, typically as inks, coatings or deposited layers. The classification applies to methods where these substances provide the functional marking or image-forming effect.
[B41M 5/44] characterised by the macromolecular compounds -- B41M 5/44 relates to duplicating or marking processes that employ macromolecular compounds such as polymeric inks, resins or binder systems to form the image or mark. The classification covers methods where these large-molecule materials are applied, cured or fixed during printing, stamping or copying operations.
[B41M 5/443] Silicon-containing polymers, e.g. silicones, siloxanes -- The CPC subclass B41M 5/443 falls under Printing → Printing, Duplicating, Marking, or Copying Processes → Duplicating or Marking Methods and covers the use of silicon-containing polymers, such as silicones and siloxanes, in printing-related operations. This includes inventions where these polymers serve as inks, coatings, release agents, or other functional materials that facilitate marking, duplicating, or other printing processes.
[B41M 5/446] Fluorine-containing polymers -- It refers to the use of fluorine-containing polymers as inks, coatings or substrates in printing, duplicating, marking or copying operations, where their chemical resistance, low surface energy and thermal stability are exploited for durable, non-staining or specialty prints.
[B41M 5/46] characterised by the light-to-heat converting means -- This subclass includes duplicating or marking methods where the energy source is light that is transformed into heat to drive the printing or marking action, such as laser or infrared heating of a substrate or recording layer. It covers processes that rely on photothermal conversion to achieve image formation, transfer, or alteration of material.
[B41M 5/465] Infrared radiation-absorbing materials, e.g. dyes, metals, silicates, C black -- B41M 5/465 concerns infrared-radiation-absorbing substances, including dyes, metals, silicates and carbon black, that are used in printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes to improve heat absorption or curing.
[B41M 5/48] combined with other image registration layers or compositions -- B41M 5/48 relates to duplicating or marking processes in which the printed image is aligned or integrated with additional registration layers or compositions, such as pre-coated substrates, adhesives or functional coatings applied before or during printing. The feature concerns the combination of these layers to improve image positioning, adhesion or functional performance.
[B41M 5/50] Recording sheets characterised by the coating used to improve ink, dye or pigment receptivity, e.g. for ink-jet or thermal dye transfer recording -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The subgroup B41M5/50 and its hierarchical lower subgroups cover also recording sheets for non-digital printing methods (i.e. methods classified in group B41M1/00 and its hierarchical lower subgroups).
[B41M 5/502] characterised by structural details, e.g. multilayer materials  supports, backcoats or intermediate layers for thermal dye transfer donor and receiver sheets  B41M5/41 ,  B41M5/42 -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[B41M 5/504] Backcoats -- A backcoat is a thin coating applied to the reverse side of a printing plate, blanket or other substrate in duplicating or marking processes. It serves to control ink transfer, protect the surface and improve plate stability during printing. This method falls under CPC class B41M 5/504 for duplicating or marking methods.
[B41M 5/506] Intermediate layers -- B41M 5/506 covers the use of intermediate layers in printing, duplicating or marking operations, such as protective, release or transfer layers applied between the substrate and the image-forming medium. The code addresses methods for preparing, applying and removing these layers to facilitate subsequent processing steps.
[B41M 5/508] Supports -- The B41M 5/508 subclass covers supports employed in duplicating or marking processes, such as plates, rollers, belts, drums or other substrates that hold, guide or receive the material being printed, copied or marked. It relates to the means that provide a stable surface or tension for the workpiece during these operations.
[B41M 5/52] Macromolecular coatings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ink, dye or pigment receiving coatings/layers for recording sheets.
[B41M 5/5209] Coatings prepared by radiation-curing, e.g. using photopolymerisable compositions -- B41M 5/5209 covers the use of radiation-curable coatings, especially those formed from photopolymerisable compositions, in printing, duplicating, marking or copying operations. It relates to methods where a light source or other radiation initiates polymerisation to create functional surface layers.
[B41M 5/5218] characterised by inorganic additives, e.g. pigments, clays -- The subclass B41M 5/5218 covers printing, duplicating or marking processes that employ inorganic additives such as pigments, clays or similar mineral particles to modify the properties of the ink, coating or substrate, enhancing colour, opacity, durability or other functional characteristics. This is a specialized subset within duplicating and marking methods that focus on the use of non-organic fillers to achieve desired printing effects.
[B41M 5/5227] characterised by organic non-macromolecular additives, e.g. UV-absorbers, plasticisers, surfactants -- The CPC subgroup B41M 5/5227 relates to printing, duplicating or marking processes that employ organic non-macromolecular additives in the material being processed. It includes the use of low-molecular compounds such as UV-absorbers, plasticisers or surfactants to modify the properties of inks, toners, films or other printable media.
[B41M 5/5236] characterised by the use of natural gums, of proteins, e.g. gelatins, or of macromolecular carbohydrates, e.g. cellulose -- B41M 5/5236 designates duplicating or marking processes that rely on natural gums, protein substances such as gelatin, or macromolecular carbohydrates like cellulose, using these biodegradable binders to form inks, coatings or transfer layers. It is situated within the printing, duplicating, marking, or copying processes segment of the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[B41M 5/5245] characterised by the use of polymers containing cationic or anionic groups, e.g. mordants -- This subclass concerns duplicating or marking processes that use polymer-based mordants containing cationic or anionic groups to fix inks or dyes onto a substrate. The charged polymers improve adhesion and image quality in printing and copying operations.
[B41M 5/5254] characterised by the use of polymers obtained by reactions only involving carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds, e.g. vinyl polymers -- The B41M 5/5254 classification covers printing, duplicating or marking processes that employ polymers produced exclusively from carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds, such as vinyl polymers. It pertains to methods where these specific polymer resins are used as inks, coatings or marking materials in the duplication or marking steps.
[B41M 5/5263] characterised by the use of polymers obtained otherwise than by reactions only involving carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds -- B41M 5/5263 is a CPC subclass under printing-duplicating-marking processes that specifies duplicating or marking methods characterized by using polymers that are produced by routes other than reactions limited to carbon-to-carbon unsaturated bonds. It narrows the broader category of printing operations to cover such polymer-based techniques.
[B41M 5/5272] Polyesters; Polycarbonates -- The subclass B41M 5/5272 covers duplicating or marking methods that employ polyester or polycarbonate materials, such as films, sheets or coated substrates, as the medium for ink transfer, image formation or print-out. It includes the preparation, handling and use of these polymeric products within printing, copying or marking processes.
[B41M 5/5281] Polyurethanes or polyureas -- B41M 5/5281 classifies the use of polyurethanes or polyureas in duplicating, marking or copying operations within the printing field. It falls under the hierarchy of Performing Operations → Printing → Printing, Duplicating, Marking, or Copying Processes → Duplicating or Marking Methods, covering resin-based inks, coatings or adhesives applied in these processes.
[B41M 5/529] characterised by the use of fluorine- or silicon-containing organic compounds -- B41M 5/529 refers to duplicating or marking processes that employ fluorine- or silicon-containing organic compounds, such as specialty inks, coatings or toners, to achieve specific functional or aesthetic effects during printing, labeling or copying operations. This subclass falls under the broader hierarchy of performing operations, printing, and printing-duplicating-marking-or-copying processes.
[B41M 7/00] After-treatment of prints, e.g. heating, irradiating, -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[B41M 7/0009] Obliterating the printed matter; Non-destructive removal of the ink pattern, e.g. for repetitive use of the support -- **Definition:** This place covers:  E.g. methods relating to mechanical removing of the ink.
[B41M 7/0018] using ink-fixing material, e.g. mordant, precipitating agent, after printing, e.g. by ink-jet printing, coating or spraying -- This CPC subclass covers processes in which a fixing agent such as a mordant or precipitating chemical is applied to a printed image after the print is produced, for example by ink-jet deposition, coating or spray techniques, to improve durability, color fastness or other properties. It falls under printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes.
[B41M 7/0027] using protective coatings or layers by lamination or by fusion of the coatings or layers -- The subclass relates to post-printing treatments in which a protective film or coating is applied to a printed medium by lamination or by fusing the coating onto the surface, creating a durable, functional layer. It covers methods that bond or melt protective layers to improve abrasion resistance, moisture protection, or other performance characteristics of the print.
[B41M 7/0036] using protective coatings or layers dried without curing -- The subclass B41M 7/0036 covers after-treatment steps for printed media where a protective coating or layer is applied and then dried without a curing process. It pertains to methods that add a non-cured protective film to enhance durability, moisture resistance or visual appearance of prints.
[B41M 7/0045] using protective coatings or film forming compositions cured by mechanical wave energy, e.g. ultrasonics, cured by electromagnetic radiation or waves, e.g. ultraviolet radiation, electron beams, or cured by magnetic or electric fields, e.g. electric discharge, plasma -- This subclass covers processes for applying protective coating or film-forming compositions to printed media and curing them using mechanical wave energy such as ultrasounds, electromagnetic radiation like ultraviolet light or electron beams, or magnetic/electric fields including electric discharge or plasma. It is an after-treatment step within printing operations.
[B41M 7/0054] using protective coatings or film forming compositions cured by thermal means, e.g. infrared radiation, heat -- This subclass covers methods where printed items are treated with protective layers that are set or cured using heat, such as infrared radiation, to improve durability or appearance. It includes the application of films or coatings that are subsequently hardened by thermal energy as part of post-print processing.
[B41M 7/0063] Preservation or restoration of currency, books or archival material, e.g. by deacidifying -- B41M 7/0063 covers processes for preserving or restoring printed materials such as currency, books or archival documents, for example by deacidifying them. It is classified under printing operations and after-treatment of prints.
[B41M 7/0072] using mechanical wave energy, e.g. ultrasonics; using magnetic or electric fields, e.g. electric discharge, plasma -- B41M 7/0072 covers post-treatment operations applied to printed items in which mechanical wave energy such as ultrasonics, or magnetic and electric fields including electric discharge or plasma, are used to cure, harden, modify surface characteristics, or otherwise improve the printed material after it leaves the printer. This subclass falls under printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes within the broader performing operations category.
[B41M 7/0081] using electromagnetic radiation or waves, e.g. ultraviolet radiation, electron beams -- B41M 7/0081 pertains to after-treatment of printed products by means of electromagnetic radiation or waves, including ultraviolet light, electron beams or similar energy sources, to cure, harden, modify or otherwise improve the printed surface.
[B41M 7/009] using thermal means, e.g. infrared radiation, heat -- B41M 7/009 relates to post-processing of printed items by applying thermal energy, such as infrared radiation or direct heating, to alter or improve the print. It is classified under printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes, specifically the after-treatment stage.
[B41M 7/02] Dusting -- Dusting refers to the removal of loose particles from printed surfaces after the printing process. It includes methods such as air blowing, brushing, or electrostatic discharge to clean the print before further after-treatment steps, thereby improving image quality and preventing defects.
[B41M 9/00] Processes wherein make-ready devices are used -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Printing methods wherein make-ready devices are used. Typical methods using said devices are disclosed e.g. in documents US1730229, US2825282 and US2587439.
[B41M 9/02] Relief make-readies -- The B41M 9/02 class covers make-ready devices used to prepare relief printing plates before they are mounted on the press. It includes equipment and methods for adjusting, aligning, and securing plates to ensure proper registration and pressure during printing. This entry pertains to processes that condition plates for relief, duplicating, marking, or copying operations.
[B41M 9/04] photomechanical -- Photomechanical processes employ make-ready devices that use light to create printing plates, drums, or other media by transferring images onto a substrate through photochemical means, preparing these elements for subsequent printing, duplicating, marking or copying operations. This entry falls under the printing-process hierarchy of the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[B41M 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- B41M 99/00 is a residual subgroup in the Cooperative Patent Classification for printing, duplicating, marking or copying processes. It covers any printing-related subject matter that does not fall within the more specific groups of the subclass, serving as a catch-all category for inventions that lack a dedicated classification elsewhere.
[B41N] PRINTING PLATES OR FOILS -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[B41N 1/00] Printing plates or foils -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Substrates for printing plates or foils.  Treatment of such plates or foils.
[B41N 10/00] Blankets or like coverings -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Transfer cylinders; Offset cylinders -> B41F13/193
[B41N 10/005] Coverings for wipers -- This class concerns protective blankets or similar coverings applied to wipers used in printing plate or foil handling, designed to prevent damage or contamination during cleaning or transfer. They are typically made of rubber, polymer or fabric, and are part of the printing process.
[B41N 10/02] Blanket structure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Inventions relating to e.g. monolayer blankets, blankets having a special support, blankets having a surface of specific smoothness, etc.
[B41N 1/003] with ink abhesive means or abhesive forming means, such as abhesive siloxane or fluoro compounds, e.g. for dry lithographic printing -- B41N 1/003 covers printing plates or foils that incorporate ink-abhesive or abhesive forming agents, such as siloxane or fluorine-based compounds, used especially in dry lithographic printing. The code belongs to the CPC hierarchy under Performing Operations - Printing - Printing Plates or Foils.
[B41N 10/04] multi-layer -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Multilayer printing blankets (two or more layers).
[B41N 1/006] made entirely of inorganic materials other than natural stone or metals, e.g. ceramics, carbide materials, ferroelectric materials -- The B41N 1/006 subclass covers printing plates or foils that are formed wholly from inorganic, non-metallic, non-stone materials, such as ceramics, carbide compounds or ferroelectric substances, which are employed in various printing operations.
[B41N 10/06] facilitating fastening to, or location on, supports -- The subclass deals with means that help attach or position blankets, foils or similar coverings on a support in printing operations, including fasteners, hooks, adhesives or other securing arrangements. It covers any device or method that simplifies the fastening or precise location of these coverings during the printing process.
[B41N 1/02] made of stone -- B41N 1/02 refers to printing plates or foils that are made of stone, such as lithographic stones used as the substrate in printing processes. This subclass falls under the broader categories of performing operations, printing, and printing plates or foils in the CPC scheme.
[B41N 1/04] metallic -- B41N 1/04 covers metallic printing plates or foils used in printing operations, including their composition, preparation, and treatment. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, printing, and printing plates or foils, and is applied to patents concerning metal-based plate substrates and foil media.
[B41N 1/06] for relief printing or intaglio printing -- B41N 1/06 covers printing plates or foils made for relief or intaglio printing, where the image is transferred from a raised or recessed surface onto paper. These plates are used in techniques such as letterpress, gravure, or lithography, applying ink only to the intended areas of the plate.
[B41N 1/08] for lithographic printing -- B41N 1/08 refers to technologies and methods specifically for lithographic printing, covering the preparation, treatment, and use of printing plates or foils that employ lithographic principles in offset printing processes. It includes related materials, surface modifications, and equipment designed to produce or apply such plates for high-quality image reproduction.
[B41N 1/083] made of aluminium or aluminium alloys or having such surface layers  B41N1/086  takes precedence -- B41N 1/083 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to printing plates or foils made of aluminum or aluminum alloys, with a note that B41N 1/086 takes precedence if applicable. This classification is used by the EPO and USPTO for patent documentation.
[B41N 1/086] laminated on a paper or plastic base -- B41N 1/086 covers printing plates or foils that are laminated onto a paper or plastic substrate, describing the method of bonding the imaging layer to a flexible base. This subclass falls within the hierarchy of Performing Operations → Printing → Printing Plates or Foils.
[B41N 1/10] multiple -- B41N 1/10 covers arrangements and handling of multiple printing plates or foils, such as systems that stack, align, feed or otherwise process several plates or foils together during printing operations. It includes devices that enable simultaneous use or treatment of more than one plate or foil to improve efficiency or registration. This subgroup falls under the broader categories of printing plates or foils within the CPC hierarchy.
[B41N 11/00] Stereotype mats -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Molded matrices or mats of papier-mâché or similar material for casting the stereotype printing plates from hot metal.
[B41N 1/12] non-metallic other than stone -- B41N 1/12 covers printing plates or foils that are made from non-metallic materials other than stone, such as polymers, ceramics, glass or composite substrates. These plates are used in various printing processes where a flexible, non-metal surface is required to transfer images or text onto a medium. The classification groups inventions relating to the preparation, treatment, and use of such non-metallic printing plates or foils.
[B41N 1/14] Lithographic printing foils -- Lithographic printing foils are thin, flexible sheets bearing a lithographic image that can be transferred to a substrate during the printing process. They serve as a master for offset printing, allowing repeated high-quality reproductions of the intended design. This subclass groups foils specifically designed for lithographic printing applications.
[B41N 1/16] Curved printing plates, especially cylinders -- B41N 1/16 is a subclass of the CPC covering curved printing plates, particularly cylindrical forms used in printing processes. It falls under the hierarchy of performing operations, printing, and printing plates or foils, and addresses the design, manufacture, and use of such curved plates.
[B41N 1/18] made of stone -- B41N 1/18 designates printing plates or foils that are made of stone, used in relief or intaglio printing processes and falling under the broader category of printing plates or foils in the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[B41N 1/20] made of metal -- B41N 1/20 designates printing plates or foils that are made of metal. These metal substrates are employed in printing operations to provide durability, heat resistance and dimensional stability during image transfer.
[B41N 1/22] made of other substances -- B41N 1/22 classifies printing plates or foils that are manufactured from substances other than the standard metal or polymer materials ordinarily covered in other subclasses. It applies to any plate or foil used in offset, gravure or other printing processes where the substrate material is of a different composition. This subclass captures innovative or alternative material choices for printing masters.
[B41N 1/24] Stencils -- The B41N 1/24 entry classifies stencils employed as printing plates or foils, comprising thin, often flexible sheets with cut-out designs that allow ink, pigment or other media to be transferred onto a substrate. It covers any stencil used for forming images, text or patterns in printing processes.
[B41N 1/241] characterised by the adhesive means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Stencils, stencil materials or carriers therefor, wherein the adhesive means is between different layers.
[B41N 1/242] Backing sheets; Top sheets; Intercalated sheets, e.g. cushion sheets; Release layers or coatings; Means to obtain a contrasting image, e.g. with a carbon sheet or coating -- B41N 1/242 covers backing sheets, top sheets, intercalated sheets such as cushion sheets, release layers or coatings, and methods for creating a contrasting image, for example with a carbon sheet or coating, used in conjunction with printing plates or foils.
[B41N 1/243] characterised by the ink pervious sheet, e.g. yoshino paper -- This CPC subgroup for printing plates or foils covers plates that incorporate an ink-pervious sheet, such as Yoshino paper, allowing ink to pass through the substrate during image transfer.
[B41N 1/245] characterised by the thermo-perforable polymeric film heat absorbing means or release coating therefor -- B41N 1/245 covers printing plates or foils that include a thermo-perforable polymeric film with a heat-absorbing or release coating, enabling image formation by localized heating. Such plates use the heat-sensitive film to create perforations that define the printable area.
[B41N 1/246] characterised by the electroconductive means or additives -- B41N 1/246 covers printing plates or foils that incorporate electroconductive materials or additives, enabling the plate to become conductive for processes such as electro-static imaging, electroless deposition or other electrically assisted plate preparation methods. This subclass is used for plates whose performance relies on conductive means rather than purely chemical or mechanical properties.
[B41N 1/247] Meshes, gauzes, woven or similar screen materials; Preparation thereof, e.g. by plasma treatment -- The subclass B41N 1/247 covers mesh, gauze, woven or comparable screen materials used for printing plates or foils, and the processes for preparing such materials, including surface-treating techniques such as plasma treatment. It is part of the broader hierarchy of performing operations, printing, and printing plates or foils.
[B41N 1/248] Mechanical details, e.g. fixation holes, reinforcement or guiding means; Perforation lines; Ink holding means; Visually or otherwise detectable marking means; Stencil units -- B41N 1/248 covers the mechanical aspects of printing plates or foils such as fixation holes, reinforcement or guiding elements, perforation lines, ink-holding structures, visible or otherwise detectable markings, and integrated stencil units.
[B41N 2207/00] Location or type of the layers in shells for rollers of printing machines -- B41N 2207/00 concerns patents that describe the arrangement or material type of layered shells used as rollers in printing machines. It is part of the CPC group for performing operations, specifically under printing plates or foils, and addresses how the roller shells are constructed for ink transfer.
[B41N 2207/02] Top layers -- The B41N 2207/02 subclass concerns the specification of top layers on the shells of printing-machine rollers, describing their location and material type. It addresses the design and characteristics of surface coatings applied to roller shells used in printing operations.
[B41N 2207/04] Intermediate layers -- B41N 2207/04 covers intermediate layers applied within the shells of rollers used in printing machines, situated between the core and the outer surface to provide cushioning, adhesion or other functional properties. These layers are part of the printing plates or foils sub-category and are defined by their location and type within the roller construction.
[B41N 2207/06] Backcoats -- Backcoats are protective or functional coatings applied to the undersurface or rear side of printing plates, foils or roller shells to enhance durability, reduce wear and improve adhesion of subsequent layers.
[B41N 2207/10] characterised by inorganic compounds, e.g. pigments -- B41N 2207/10 refers to printing-machine rollers whose shell layers are defined by the use of inorganic compounds such as pigments. It classifies inventions where the material composition of the roller coating relies on inorganic substances, falling under the broader categories of performing operations, printing, and printing plates or foils, specifically the location or type of layers in roller shells.
[B41N 2207/12] characterised by non-macromolecular organic compounds -- B41N 2207/12 is a CPC subclass under Performing Operations → Printing → Printing plates or foils that concerns the location or type of the layers in shells for rollers of printing machines, specifically when those layers are characterised by non-macromolecular organic compounds.
[B41N 2207/14] characterised by macromolecular organic compounds -- This subclass covers printing plates or foils whose roller-shell layers are defined by the presence of macromolecular organic compounds. It relates to the specific location or type of these polymer-based layers used in printing-machine rollers.
[B41N 2210/00] Location or type of the layers in multi-layer blankets or like coverings -- This subclass deals with the arrangement and material type of the individual layers in multilayer printing blankets or similar coverings. It covers patents that specify where each layer is positioned - such as surface, intermediate, or backing layers - and the kind of material used, like rubber, polymer or fabric, in printing presses.
[B41N 2210/02] Top layers -- The B41N 2210/02 classifies the top layers of multi-layer blankets or similar coverings used in printing plates or foils, specifying the material, properties or function of the outermost layer such as protective, release or adhesion characteristics. It helps identify patents that focus on the uppermost coating or film in a layered printing blanket system.
[B41N 2210/04] Intermediate layers -- B41N 2210/04 covers the specification of intermediate layers positioned between outer and backing layers in multi-layer blankets or similar coverings used for printing plates or foils, defining their material type, thickness or functional role such as cushioning, heat dissipation or pressure distribution. This subclass addresses how these middle strata contribute to the overall performance of the blanket in printing operations.
[B41N 2210/06] Backcoats -- B41N 2210/06 covers backcoats, which are coating layers applied to the reverse side of printing plates or foils, often forming part of a multi-layer blanket or covering. The classification pertains to the location or type of these layers within such multi-layer printing structures.
[B41N 2210/10] characterised by inorganic compounds, e.g. pigments -- The B41N 2210/10 subclass refers to printing plates or foils whose multi-layer blankets or coverings contain layers defined by inorganic compounds, such as pigments, distinguishing the material composition and placement of these layers within the printing structure.
[B41N 2210/12] characterised by non-macromolecular organic compounds -- This subclass covers multi-layer blankets or similar coverings used in printing plates or foils, where the layers are defined by non-macromolecular organic compounds rather than polymeric materials. It addresses the location or type of the layers within such structures for printing operations.
[B41N 2210/14] characterised by macromolecular organic compounds -- B41N 2210/14 relates to printing plates or foils that employ multilayer blankets or similar coverings, where the location or type of the layers is defined by the use of macromolecular organic compounds, such as polymers, to form the functional layers.
[B41N 3/00] Preparing for use and conserving printing surfaces -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pre-treatment of printing plates.  After-treatment of printing plates.  Treatment of both imaged and non-imaged printing plates.
[B41N 3/003] of intaglio formes, e.g. application of a wear-resistant coating, such as chrome, on the already-engraved plate or cylinder; Preparing for reuse, e.g. removing of the Ballard shell; Correction of the engraving -- This subclass deals with post-engraving treatments of intaglio printing forms, including the application of wear-resistant coatings such as chrome, the removal of protective shells for plate reuse, and methods for correcting engraving errors, all aimed at preparing and conserving the printing surface.
[B41N 3/006] Cleaning, washing, rinsing or reclaiming of printing formes other than intaglio formes  B41N3/06  takes precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The regeneration of printing plates.
[B41N 3/03] Chemical or electrical pretreatment -- The B41N 3/03 subclass covers methods for chemically or electrically treating printing plates or foils before use, aiming to ready the surface for image transfer and to preserve its quality during storage or handling. This includes pretreatment steps such as cleaning, surface activation, charge deposition, or other processes that enhance adhesion, durability, or performance of the printing surface.
[B41N 3/032] Graining by laser, arc or plasma means -- This subclass covers the creation of surface texture, or graining, on printing plates or foils using high-energy sources such as lasers, electric arcs or plasma. The methods are applied to modify the plate surface before printing to control ink transfer or enhance durability.
[B41N 3/034] characterised by the electrochemical treatment of the aluminum support, e.g. anodisation, electro-graining; Sealing of the anodised layer; Treatment of the anodic layer with inorganic compounds; Colouring of the anodic layer -- The code covers methods for treating the aluminum base of printing plates by electrochemical processes such as anodisation or electro-graining, including subsequent sealing, inorganic-compound treatment and colouring of the anodic layer to prepare and preserve the printing surface.
[B41N 3/036] characterised by the presence of a polymeric hydrophilic coating -- The subclass B41N 3/036 covers printing plates or foils that are prepared or preserved by applying a polymeric hydrophilic coating. The coating provides a water-attracting surface to improve wetting, protect the plate, and facilitate subsequent processing steps such as ink transfer or cleaning. It is a specific treatment within the broader group of operations for preparing and conserving printing surfaces.
[B41N 3/038] Treatment with a chromium compound, a silicon compound, a phophorus compound or a compound of a metal of group IVB; Hydrophilic coatings obtained by hydrolysis of organometallic compounds -- The B41N 3/038 subclass concerns chemical treatments applied to printing plates or foils, specifically using chromium, silicon, phosphorus or a group IVB metal compound, to prepare and preserve the surface. It also includes hydrophilic coatings formed by hydrolysing organometallic compounds.
[B41N 3/04] Graining or abrasion by mechanical means -- B41N 3/04 relates to the mechanical graining or abrasion of printing plates or foils, a process used to prepare the surface for printing or to preserve its condition before use.
[B41N 3/06] by use of detergents -- The subgroup deals with preparing or conserving printing plates or foils by cleaning them with detergent solutions, removing contaminants, residues or oxidation to restore a suitable printing surface.
[B41N 3/08] Damping -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Developing with dampening water, also the composition of the dampening liquids.  The compositions of fountain solutions and gumming solutions.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Processing photosensitive materials -> G03F7/26 - Liquid compositions for imagewise removal of photosensitive materials using liquid means, e.g. developers -> G03F7/32
[B41N 6/00] Mounting boards; Make-ready devices, e.g. underlays, overlays -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Devices for attaching printing elements or formes to supports (i.e. mechanical aspects of the fastenings as such) -> B41F27/00 - Devices for attaching coverings or make-ready devices; Guiding devices for coverings (i.e. mechanical aspects of the fastenings as such) -> B41F30/00
[B41N 6/02] Chemical means for fastening printing formes on mounting boards -- The subclass B41N 6/02 covers chemical adhesives or bonding agents employed to secure printing forms, such as plates or foils, to mounting boards, underlays, overlays or other make-ready devices used in printing operations. It includes methods and compositions for fastening the forms to these support structures.
